EDLIZ   2025  330  U RINARY   T RACT   I NFECTIONS   (UTI)   IN   A DULTS  Cystitis  Usually   presents   with   dysuria,   urinary   urgency   and   frequency,   and   suprapubic   pain,  but   note   that   in   men   dysuria   more   commonly   indicates   a   sexually   transmitted  infection   (STI).   Always   exclude   an   STI.   With   UTI,   urine   is   often   cloudy   and  smelly.   Where   possible   diagnosis   should   be   made   with   leucocyte   dipstick,  microscopy   or   culture.     Treat   with:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  nitrofurantoin*   po   C   V   100mg   2   times   a   day   3   days  or   amoxicillin   po   C   V   500mg   3   times   a   day   3   days     If   still   symptomatic   after   3   days,   refer .  Urinary   tract   infections   in   children   (including   acute   pyelonephritis)  Recommend   urgent   urine   microscopy   and   culture,   and   the   immediate  commencement   of   antibiotics   if:  1. Age   <3   months  2. Specific   urinary   symptoms   in   children   of   3   months   to   2   years   of   age  3. Non-specific   symptoms   in   children   of   3   months   to   2   years   of   age   at   high   risk  of   serious   illness.  In   children   3   years   of   age   and   older,   a   urine   dipstick   should   be   used   to   initially  diagnose   UTI.  Management  Children<3   months     Intravenous   (IV)   antibiotic   for   at   least   3   days   before   switching   to   oral   antibiotic  if   clinically   improved.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ceftriaxone   inj   C   V   50mg/kg   daily   at   least   3   days  amoxicillin/clavul  anic   acid   po  B   V   20-30mg/kg   2   times   a   day   until   14   days

EDLIZ   2025  331     Treat   for   a   total   of   2   weeks  Children   >3   months     If   vomiting,   treat   with   IV   antibiotics   for   at   least   2   to   4   days   then   oral   antibiotics  for   a   total   of   7   to   10   days     If   no   vomiting,   use   oral   antibiotics   for   7   to   10   days.  If   vomiting  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ceftriaxone   inj   C   V   50mg/kg   daily   at   least   2-4   days  amoxicillin/clavulanic  acid   po  B   V   20-30mg/kg   2   times   a   day   Until   7-10days  No   vomiting  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  amoxicillin/clavulanic  acid   po  B   V   20-30mg/kg   2   times   a   day   7-10days  or   cefuroxime   po   B   E   10-15mg/kg   2   times   a   day   7-10   days  Children   >3   months   with   lower   urinary   tract   symptoms   (cystitis)     Oral   antibiotics   for   3   days.  Factors   distinguishing   upper   UTI   and   lower   UTI  Bacteriuria   and   fever   of   38   degrees   or  higher  Upper   UTI  Bacteriuria,   loin   pain/tenderness   and  fever   of   <   38   degrees  Upper   UTI  Bacteriuria,   but   no   systemic   features   Lower   UTI  Refer   all   children   with   atypical   UTI   e.g.:     Seriously   ill   child     Poor   urine   flow     Abdominal   or   bladder   mass     Raised   plasma   creatinine   level     Septicaemia     Failure   to   respond   to   treatment   within   48   hours     Non- E.   coli   UTI

EDLIZ   2025  332  Acute   pyelonephritis   in   adults  Diagnosed   when   a   UTI   is   accompanied   by   nausea,   vomiting,   fever,   rigors   and   loin  pain.   Dysuria   may   be   absent.   Treat   for   2   weeks.     Mild   acute   pyelonephritis  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  amoxicillin/clavulanic   acid  po  B   N   625mg   2   times   a   day   2   weeks     Acutely   ill   patients:   use   IV   antibiotics   until   apyrexial,   and   then   change   to   oral  therapy   as   stated   above  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ceftriaxone   iv   C   V   1g   once   a   day   7   days  or   gentamicin*   iv   C   V   4-7mg/kg   once   a   day   7   days  *Remember   gentamicin   toxicity   is   manifested   after   7-10   days   of   use.   Check  gentamicin   levels   where   possible.   Avoid   the   nephrotoxic   medicines   such   as  gentamicin   and   nitrofurantoin   in   renal   dysfunction.  A CUTE   K IDNEY   I NJURY  What   is   causing   the   kidney   injury?   Try   and   classify   by   cause.   The   majority   of  cases   of   acute   kidney   injury   (AKI)   are   due   to   ischaemic   or   toxic   injury   to   the  kidney   and   are   reversible   if   treatment   is   instituted   promptly   i.e.   within   hours   not  days .  Pre-Renal   Cases  This   is   the   most   common   cause   of   acute   kidney   injury   and   is   most   amenable   to  therapy   if   treatment   is   instituted   early.   Usually   have   a   history   of   hypovolaemia   or  hypotension   e.g.   bleeding,   vomiting,   diarrhoea   and   are   usually   oliguric.   Rapid  recovery   of   renal   failure   is   to   be   expected   with   prompt   treatment.  Acute   kidney   Injury  Consider   hypovolaemia,   hypotension,   sepsis,   malaria,   acute   glomerulonephritis,  acute   tubular   necrosis,   multiple   myeloma,   nephrotoxic   medicines   such   as  gentamicin   and   NSAIDs,   and   other   causes   such   as   acute-or-chronic   renal   failure.  As   a   minimum,   get   urine   microscopy   and   an   ultrasound   of   the   kidneys   for   size.  Are   the   kidneys   normal   sized   (small,   enlarged   or   obstructed?)

EDLIZ   2025  333  Obstructive   Uropathy  Continuous   bladder   catheterisation   is   required   until   the   obstruction   is   relieved.  Relief   of   obstruction   can   result   in   polyuria.   Therefore,   rehydrate   with   IV   fluids.  Aim   to   keep   up   with   the   urine   output.   Sodium   and   potassium   supplements   may   be  required.   Scan   kidneys   to   exclude   hydronephrosis.   Refer   to   a   urologist   for  definitive   management.  Exclude   prostatic   enlargement   /   prostatic   cancer   in   males   and   cancer   of   the   cervix  in   women.  Management   of   Acute   Kidney   Injury     First   line:   Exclude   dehydration   in   all   cases.   Give   adequate   rehydration.   Try  fluid   challenge   with   preferably   Ringer ’ s   Lactate   but   sodium   chloride   0.9%   can  also   be   used.   Should   show   response   within   hours   (not   days).   Aim   for   a   visible  jugular   venous   pressure   (JVP)   first   and   then   consider   giving   IV   furosemide   if  urine   output   is   low   to   encourage   diuresis.     Second   line:   If   the   patient   fails   to   respond   to   adequate   rehydration   and   fluid  challenge   with   Ringer ’ s   Lactate   or   sodium   chloride   0.9%   [ not   dextrose   5%]  within   24   hours   and   condition   is   deteriorating,   referral   for   dialysis   may   be  indicated.   If   this   is   not   possible,   then   aim   to   support   patient   until   the   kidneys  recover   [may   take   up   to   6-8   weeks   or   more].   Monitor   fluid   (input   and   output  charting)   carefully.   Check   electrolytes   regularly   and   maintain   nutrition.   Watch  for   infections.     Third   line:   Start   dialysis   or   consult   dialysis   team   sooner   rather   than   later   so   that  they   can   help   monitor   the   patient.   Late   referrals   contribute   to   the   mortality   of  acute   kidney   injury   and   end-stage   renal   disease.   If   you   suspect   your   patient   will  need   dialysis   chronically   i.e.   entry   into   the   chronic   dialysis   programme,   discuss  the   patient   with   the   dialysis   team.   Acute   dialysis   e.g.   peritoneal   dialysis   or  haemodialysis   should   be   offered   to   those   that   need   it   resources   permitting.  Fluid   balance:   Measure   weights   before   breakfast   on   a   daily   basis.   Aim   for   no  weight   gain   if   dealing   with   established   renal   failure.   Previous   day ’ s   losses   (urine,  vomit   etc)   +500mls   =day ’ s   fluid   intake.  Electrolytes:   Ideally   measure   urea   and   electrolytes   at   least   on   alternate   days.  Monitor   potassium   levels.   Consider   getting   an   ECG   to   determine   severity   of   the  hyperkalaemia.

EDLIZ   2025  334  ⬥   To   lower   potassium   levels   in   acute   hyperkalaemia,   give:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  calcium   gluconate  (or   chloride)   10%  B   V   10mls   once  plus   short   acting  insulin   with  glucose  B   V   Intravenous   50mls  of   50%   glucose   +  10   units   of   short  acting   insulin  over   10  min  plus   salbutamol  nebulised  B   V   10mg   2   times   a  day  Review   daily  plus   calcium   resonium ®   A   N   45gm   as   rectal   enema.   Leave   enema   in   for   as  long   as   possible  Note:   Consider   IV/oral   furosemide   and   fluid   replacement   to   manage   total   body  potassium   once   emergency   has   been   treated.  General   measures   in   the   management   of   acute   kidney   injury     Catheter:   Insert   a   urinary   catheter   but   remove   it   once   sustained   diuresis   has  occurred   or   if   oliguria   persists   and   the   patient   is   started   on   dialysis   or  conservative   treatment.   Avoid   long   catheterisation   periods   -   increases   risk   of  catheter   related   infection.     Urine:   Ward   urinalysis   test   -   check   for   haematuria,   proteinuria   and   glycosuria.  Send   urine   for   microscopy   (for   cells,   casts),   culture   and   sensitivity.     Diet:   High   calorie   and   normal   or   high   protein   diet   with   low   sodium   and   low  potassium.   Ask   for   dietician ’ s   help.   For   low   potassium   diet,   advise   to   avoid   e.g.  oranges,   bananas,   mangoes,   potatoes.     Anaemia:   If   oliguric   do   not   transfuse   unless   there   is   significant   bleeding,   as  there   is   a   high   risk   of   fluid   overload.   Can   transfuse   safely   when   patient   is   on  dialysis.     Monitor   BP   regularly(   e.g.   4   hourly)     Monitor   fluid   input/output   strictly.     Avoid   nephrotoxic   medicines   such   as   gentamicin   and   NSAIDs     Watch   for   and   treat   underlying   infections.     Check   weight   daily.

EDLIZ   2025  335  A CUTE   N EPHRITIC   S YNDROME  Rapidly   progressive   glomerulonephritis   (RPGN)   is   a   renal   emergency!!!   It   is   a  disease   of   the   kidney   characterized   clinically   by   a   rapid   decrease   in   the   glomerular  filtration   rate   (GFR)   of   at   least   50%   over   a   short   period,   from   a   few   days   to   3  months.  Presence   of   features   of   glomerulonephritis   (i.e.   facial   or   generalised   oedema,  haematuria   plus   oliguria   and   hypertension)   and   rapid   deterioration   of   kidney  function   should   be   managed   as   RPGN   with   immediate   initiation   of   intravenous  steroids   and   urgent   referral   to   a   tertiary   hospital.  Medication   to   give:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  methylprednisolone   iv   A   V   10mg/kg   once   a   day   3   days  then   prednisolone   po   B   V   2mg/kg  Max   60mg/day  once   a   day   2   weeks  plus   calcium   po   B   E   500mg   once  plus   vitamin   D   po   B   E   400iu   once  plus   omeprazole   po   B   E   20-40mg   daily  P OST   INFECTIOUS   N EPHRITIS   -  Usually   presents   with   facial   or   generalised   oedema   plus   oliguria   and   hypertension.  Check   urine   microscopy   for   active   sediment   and   do   U   &   Es   daily.   There   may   be   a  recent   history   of   tonsillitis,   arthralgia,   skin   rashes/   infection.     Promote   diuresis   with   small   doses   of   oral   furosemide   (40-80mg   once   daily).   If  response   obtained   put   on   a   regular   dose.     If   post-streptococcal   infection   is   suspected   give:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  amoxicillin   po   C   V   500mg   8hrly   10   days     Treat   hypertension   conventionally.   Children   need   early   intervention   for   elevated  blood   pressure.     Restrict   fluid   if   oliguric   and   carefully   maintain   fluid   balance.     If   renal   function   is   deteriorating,   refer   early   to   nephrologist   /specialist   physician  or   paediatrician.   NOTE:   If   the   patient   has   signs   of   nephritic   syndrome   and  U&Es   monitoring   is   not   available,   REFER

EDLIZ   2025  336     Once   a   diagnosis   of   nephritic   syndrome   is   suspected,   discuss   with   physician/  paediatrician/nephrologist   whilst   monitoring   renal   function   DAILY.   If   renal  function   is   deteriorating,   REFER.  N EPHROTIC   S YNDROME   IN   ADULTS  Diagnosed   where   there   is   generalised   oedema,   hypoalbuminaemia   and   proteinuria  (>3gm/day).   Dipstick   should   show   at   least   protein   ++.   To   quantify   the   proteinuria,  you   can   request   a   urine   albumin:   creatinine   ratio.   Check   urine   microscopy   and  U&Es.   Estimate   the   GFR   (creatinine   clearance).   Weigh   patient   at   each   review.  Exclude   SLE,   HIV   and   Hepatitis   B   or   C   or   diabetes.   Renal   biopsy   should   be  considered   where   available   Consult   a   nephrologist.   The   nephrotic   syndrome   may  respond   to   an   ACEI   and   may   be   steroid   sensitive.     Promote   diuresis   using:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  frusemide   po   B   V   40   –   80mg   once   a   day,   5   days  or   frusemide   iv   B   V   40   –   200mg   twice   a   day   until   resolution  Caution:   Excessive   use   of   frusemide   may   precipitate   renal   failure   and   large  doses   of   frusemide   may   cause   hearing   loss.   Therefore,   check   U&Es   regularly.  Measure   urea   and   electrolytes.   Restrict   fluid   to   1   litre   per   day   until   diuresis  occurs.   If   oedema   is   gross   and   no   response,   consider   adding   prednisolone  particularly   if   the   urine   sediment   is   benign   (i.e.   no   red   cells   or   casts).  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  prednisolone   po   B   V   1mg/kg   once   a   day  [mornings]  2   months  and   enalapril   po   B   V   5-10mg   once   a   day   review     Aim   to   tail   off   steroid   dose   to   zero   during   the   3 rd   month.   Stopping   early   may  result   in   a   relapse.     Give   an   ACEI   for   the   proteinuria   even   if   BP   is   normal   e.g.   a   small   dose   of   e.g.  enalapril   early   unless   contraindicated.   This   may   be   increased   as   the   condition  allows.     Refer   if   there   is   failure   to   reduce   oedema   within   two   weeks   on   high   dose  steroids.     If   patient   is   responding   to   steroids,   you   will   need   to   reduce   the   diuretics.

EDLIZ   2025  337     Anticoagulate   if   immobile:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  heparin   sc   B   V   5000   units   3   times   a   day   until   mobile     Search   for   underlying   cause   -e.g.   Diabetes,   SLE,   Hepatitis   B/C,   HIV,   syphilis.     Restrict   dietary   salt   intake   but   leave   on   normal/usual   protein   intake.     If   oedema   is   not   resolving   after   2   weeks   of   treatment,   refer   to   Central   Hospital.  Nephrotic   syndrome   in   children  Recent   history   of   oedema,   hypoalbuminemia   (albumin   <2.5g/dl)   and   nephrotic  range   proteinuria   (urine   protein   3+   or   4+,   urine   protein-creatinine   ratio   in   first  morning   urine   sample   of   >2mg/mg.  First   episode   of   nephrotic   syndrome   in   children   >3   months   of   age   –   initiate  Prednisolone   2mg/kg   (maximum   60mg)   for   4-6   weeks   then   1.5mg/kg   daily  (maximum   40mg)   on   alternate   days   for   4-6   weeks.     Add   Calcium   500mg   daily   and   vitamin   D   400iu   to   prevent   steroid   induced   bone  disease.     Add   Omeprazole   0.4-0.8mg/kg   daily   to   prevent   gastritis.  Children   <3   months   with   nephrotic   syndrome   should   be   referred   to   a   nephrologist  for   investigations   and   management.  Frusemide   1-2mg/kg   daily   or   twice   daily   may   be   used   to   manage   severe   oedema  BUT   with   close   monitoring   as   this   may   cause   intravascular   volume   loss.  All   cases   of   relapsing   nephrotic   syndrome   should   be   referred   to   a   nephrologist.  Renal   tract   conditions   in   children  Rapidly   progressive   glomerulonephritis  Rapidly   progressive   glomerulonephritis   (RPGN)   is   a   Renal   emergency!!!.   It   is   a  disease   of   the   kidney   characterised   clinically   by   a   rapid   decrease   in   the   glomerular  filtration   rate   (GFR)   of   at   least   50%   over   a   short   period,   from   a   few   days   to   3  months.

EDLIZ   2025  338  Presence   of   features   of   glomerulonephritis   (i.e.   facial   or   generalised   oedema,  haematuria   plus   oliguria   and   hypertension)   and   rapid   deterioration   of   kidney  function   should   be   managed   as   RPGN   with   immediate   initiation   of   intravenous  steroids   and   urgent   referral   to   a   tertiary   hospital. EDLIZ   2025  344  Antibiotics  zidovudine   Reduce   dose   especially   if   on   dialysis  lamivudine   Consider   reducing   does   but   may   not   be   feasible   given  our   ficed   dose   combinations  efavirenz   No   need   to   adjust   doses  Tenofovir   (TDF)   300mg   following   dialysis   once   a   week   but   better   to  switch   to   ABC  lopinavir/ritonavir   Use   usual   dose  Medicines   and   Dialysis  Dialysis   may   remove   significant   quantities   of   some   medicines   e.g.   penicillins,  aminoglycosides,   cephalosporins,   chloramphenicol,   metronidazole,   methyldopa,  anti-TB   therapy,   quinine.   Therefore,   give   supplementary   doses   following   a  haemodialysis   session.   The   dialysis   team   will   advise   on   supplementary   doses.  E ND   S TAGE   R ENAL   D ISEASE /C HRONIC   D IALYSIS  End   Stage   Renal   Disease:   Stage   5,   GFR   <15ml/min:   This   is   said   to   have   occurred  if   a   patient   needs   dialysis   for   at   least   3   months   and   thus   the   need   for   chronic  dialysis   may   be   more   likely.  Chronic   dialysis   clients   have   to   be   selected   carefully   as   renal   replacement   therapy  is   expensive   and   not   easily   accessible.   Clinical   and   psychosocial   selection   criteria  for   the   chronic   dialysis   programme   are   applied   and   each   individual   patient   should  be   discussed   with   the   dialysis   team.   Some   psychosocial   issues   may   exclude   the  patient   from   the   chronic   dialysis   programme.   The   goals   of   providing   this   therapy  should   be   addressed   adequately   with   patient   and   relatives.   Current   dialysis   does  entail   some   out-of-pocket   expenses   for   the   patient   and   family.   Thus,   ensure   there  has   been   adequate   pre-counselling   prior   to   starting   patient   on   the   chronic   dialysis  progra m .  Chronic   peritoneal   dialysis   and   haemodialysis   may   be   available   in   your   city   or  town   but   discuss   with   the   s pecialist   before   transferring   patient   to   the   di alysis   unit .  Renal   transplantation  There   is   currently   no   renal   transplantation   in   Zimbabwe,   but   some   patients   have  had   live   donor   related   renal   transplantation   outside   this   country   at   their   own  expense   or   that   of   their   medical   aid   society.

EDLIZ   2025  348     In   the   elderly   patients,   those   on   diuretics,   or   those   with   impaired   renal   function,  allopurinol   should   be   started   at   the   lower   daily   dose   of   50   mg   and   increased  cautiously   if   necessary.   Can   increase   the   allopurinol   by   50mg   every   4   weeks   up  to   300mg.     Allopurinol   should   not   be   introduced   during   an   acute   attack.     D uring   the   period   when   allopurinol   is   being   introduced   an   active   drug   for   acute  gout,   like   colchicine   or   NSAIDs,   should   be   used   until   a   normal   level   of   uric   acid  is   attained:     Concurrent   anti-inflammatory   therapy   should   be   given   for   the   first   3   months   of  allopurinol   therapy:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  diclofenac   po   B   E   50mg   3   times   a   day   3   months  Dietary   management   of   gout  Choice   of   foods   aims   to   control   the   amount   of   purine   in   the   diet.     Reduce   weight   (limit   fats   and   refined   carbohydrates).     Alcohol   should   be   avoided   or   reduced   drastically     Avoid   dehydration.  These   foods   should   be   avoided   or   eaten   in   moderation.     offals,   red   meat   e.g.   beef.  These   foods   are   permissible:     eggs,   milk   products,   carbohydrates,   fruit,   vegetables,   chicken   and   fish.  R HEUMATOLOGICAL   C ONDITIONS  General   Guidelines     Refer   patients   with   suspected   rheumatic   disease   for   S pecialist   diagnosis   and  help   with   treatment.   Simple   analgesia   alone   is   not   enough!     NSAIDs   should   be   used   cautiously   in   kidney   disease,   pregnancy,   the   elderly,  and   in   patients   with   asthma     NSAIDs   should   be   avoided   in   patients   with   a   history   of   peptic   ulcer   disease.  NSAIDs   should   be   taken   with   food.     If   dyspeptic   symptoms   develop   in   a   patient   on   chronic   NSAIDs,   try   adding   a  proton   pump   inhibitor   e.g.   omeprazole.     Physiotherapy   or   occupational   therapy   is   a   useful   adjunct   treatment   especially  after   acute   inflammation   has   subsided.

EDLIZ   2025  349  Systemic   Connective   Tissue   Diseases  Refer   all   cases   of   chronic   polyarthritis   for   a   definitive   diagnosis.   This   group   shares  several   pathogenic   and   aetiological   factors   related   to   autoimmunity.  Rheumatoid   Arthritis   and   Juvenile   Chronic   Arthritis   (Juvenile  Rheumatoid   Arthritis)  To   avert   the   erosive   damage   of   progressive   rheumatoid   arthritis,   early   diagnosis  and   initiation   of   treatment   with   NSAIDs,   Disease   Modifying   Anti-Rheumatic  Medicines   (DMARDs)   (hydroxychloroquine   or   chloroquine ,   methotrexate   and  sulphasalazine),   and   low   dose   steroids   in   the   presence   of   severe   inflammation   or  vasculitis   is   necessary.   Disease   modifying   medicines   and   biologics   are   the  mainstay   of   treatment   to   minimise   erosions   and   deformities.   Biologics   should  only   be   prescribed   by   Specialists   as   there   will   be   need   for   close   monitoring   of   the  patient.   Screen   for   TB,   HIV,   hepatitis   B   and   C   prior   to   starting   DMARDs.  ■   Manage   with:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ibuprofen   po   C   N   200-  400mg  3   times   a   day   review  (Paed   7-14mg/kg)  or   diclofenac   po   B   E   25   -50mg   3   times   a   day   review  Disease   modifying   anti-rheumatic   medicines   should   be   started   early:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  methotrexate   po   S   E   5-25mg   once   a   week   review  or   hydroxychloroqui  nine  S   N   200-400mg   once   a   day   review  or   chloroquine   po   S   N   150mg   base   once   a   day   continual/review  Referral   to   an   ophthalmologist   is   strongly   advised   after   9   months   of  continuous   treatment   with   chloroquine.   Eye   checkups   should   be   every   6  months-   should   include   visual   field   testing.   Treatment   should   be   discontinued  if   a   patient   complains   of   visual   disturbance   whilst   on   chloroquine. D IABETES ,   ENDOCRINOLOGY   AND  METABOLISM  DIABETES   MELLITUS   35 3  TYPES   OF   INSULIN   35 5  ORAL   ANTI-DIABETIC   AGENTS   35 8  DIABETIC   DIET   35 9  SPECIAL   PROBLEMS   IN   DIABETICS   36 2  SURGERY   36 3  HYPERGLYCAEMIC   COMA   &   PRE-COMA   (ADULTS)   36 4  HYPERGLYCAEMIC   COMA   AND   PRE-COMA   (CHILDREN)   36 4  THYROID   DISEASE   3 70  GOITRE   37 1  HYPERTHYROIDISM   37 1  HYPOADRENALISM   37 1

EDLIZ   2025  353  D IABETES   M ELLITUS  Clinically,   there   are   four   main   classifications   of   diabetes   mellitus:  1.   Type   1   diabetes   (T1DM)  Usually   under   30   years   but   can   present   at   any   age,   presents   acutely,   with   weight  loss   and   ketonuria:   Require   insulin.   Provide   dietary   advice.   Diabetic   ketoacidosis  (DKA)   is   a   medical   emergency   of   type   1   diabetes   mellitus   and   should   be   managed  in   hospital.  2.   Type   2   diabetes   (T2DM).  Usually   diagnosed   in   people   with   overweight,   obesity,   positive   family   history   of  diabetes   and   occurs   more   frequently   in   much   older   individuals   but   can   occur   in  younger   people.  3.   Gestational   Diabetes   Mellitus   (GDM).  This   is   diabetes   that   is   first   diagnosed   in   the   second   trimester   or   third   trimester   of  pregnancy.  4.   Other   types   of   diabetes  Many   other   diabetes   or   hyperglycaemic   clinical   entities   that   require   specialized  diagnoses.   Hence   early   referral   to   Diabetologists/Endocrinologists   is  recommended.  Dietary   control   and   weight   loss   plays   an   important   part   in   the   management   of  diabetes   mellitus.   Many   type   2   diabetics   are   overweight.   Reducing   body   weight  through   careful   control   of   energy   intake   and   physical   activity   like   walking   helps   to  control   the   symptoms   of   diabetes.  Criteria   to   diagnose   diabetes   mellitus  Generally,   there   are   four   recommended   criteria   to   diagnose   diabetes:  1)   Random   Blood   Glucose   (RBG)   levels  2)   Fasting   Blood   Glucose   (FBG)   levels  3)   Oral   Glucose   Tolerance   Test   (OGTT)  4)   The   glycated   haemoglobin   test,   known   as   HbA1c  General   notes:  • If   RBG   is   greater   than   11.1   mmol/l,   refer   to   at   least   District   level.  • T1D   is   usually   diagnosed   from   plasma   blood   glucose   levels   in   age-  appropriate   individuals  • For   T1D   patients,   use   the   HbA1c   to   monitor   treatment,   rather   than   for  diagnostic   purposes.

EDLIZ   2025  354  T2D   diagnostic   pathway  Key:   2h   =   2   hours.   To   convert   mg/dL   to   mmol/L   of   blood   glucose   divide   by   18

EDLIZ   2025  355  T YPES   OF   I NSULIN  Insulin  type  Onset   Peak  activity  [hrs]  Duration  [hrs]  Type   of   insulin  e.g.  Bolus   or  meal-time  Insulins  Rapid  Acting  (analogue)  5-15  mins  1-1.5   2-4   Aspart(NoVo   rapid  ™ ),   Lispro  (Humalog   TM)  Glulisine(Apidra   ™ )  Short   acting  (human  Insulin)  30mins   2   –   4   5-8   Regular   or   soluble  (Actrapid   ™ )  Basal  Insulin  (Intermediate  and   long-  acting  insulins)  Intermediate  acting  (Human  insulin)  2-4   hrs   4   –   12   12-   18   Neutral   Protamine  Hagedorn  (NPH),Isophane   ,  (Protaphane   ™ )  Long-acting  Analogue  2   hrs   3-9   6-24   Detemir   (Levemir  ™ )  Long   Acting  Analogue  2hrs   None   (flat  profile)  20->24   Glargine   (Lantus   ™ )  Ultra   long  acting  analogue  30-90  mins  none   (flat  profile)  Up   to   48  hours  Insulin   Degludec  (Tresiba   ™ )  Pre   mixed   or  Biphasic  Insulins  Biphasic  Human  Insulin  (BHI)  30mins   1st   peak  at   2-4hrs   ,  2nd   peak  at   4-12  hrs  12-18   hrs   Regular   30%   +   NPH  70%   per   unit  (Actraphane   ™ )  Biphasic  Insulin  aspart  30(BIAsp30  )  (Analogue)  15mins   1st   peak  at   1-  1.5hrs   ,  2nd   peak  None   (flat  profile)  20-24hrs   Insulin   Aspart.   30%  +70%   Insulin   aspart  protamine  (Novomix30   ™ )  Key:   TM   denotes   trade   name   or   branded   product.   EDLIZ   recommends   generic  prescribing   however   with   insulins,   brand   names   are   easier   remembered   than   the  generic   names,   and   indeed,   switch   between   brands   may   upset   on-going  glycaemic   control.

EDLIZ   2025  356  General   Insulin   dosage   guidance   and   monitoring:     At   the   point   of   insulin   initiation,   refer   to   a   specialist   Physician/   Endocrinologist  or   consult   via   Telemedicine.     In   type   1   diabetes,   when   initiating   insulin   treatment,   the   starting   dose   is   0.25-  0.4   units   /kg/day.   In   most   T1D   patients   the   basal/bolus   regimen   is   the   most  flexible   and   physiologic   way   of   delivering   insulin.   This   is   also   called   Multiple  Daily   Injections   of   insulin   (MDII).     On   calculating   the   Total   Daily   Dose   (TDDI)   of   insulin   required   based   on   body  weight,   a   50:50   basal/bolus   dose   split   is   usually   recommended.  Basal   Insulin   (50%   of   Total   daily   dose   of   insulin)  Long   acting   subcutaneously   (SC)   once   (10pm   for   analogue   insulin)   but   for   the  human   insulin   NPH   (protaphane),   doses   may   need   to   be   split   into   two   equal   doses  12   hours   apart  Basal   insulins   are   normally   injected   independent   of   meals,   hence   the   10pm   dosing  recommendation.     Analogue   insulins   are   associated   with   easier   handling,   user-friendly   delivery  systems,   less   weight   gain,   less   glycaemic   variability   and   less   hypoglycaemia  risk.  Bolus   Insulin:   (50%   of   the   TDDI)  Dedicated   to   the   bolus   or   meal-time   insulin   is   then   divided   into   three   premeal  doses   to   cover   the   three   recommended   main   meals,   breakfast,   lunch   and   supper.  Can   use   rapid   acting   (Actrapid)   30   minutes   before   meals   or   analogue   insulins   eg  Insulin   Aspart   (Novorapid)   15   minutes   before   meals.     If   however,   a   meal   is   to   be   skipped   for   whatever   reason,   the   bolus   insulin   should  NOT   be   injected.   So,   NO   MEAL,   NO   BOLUS!  A   compromise   to   the   MDII   regimen   or   basal/bolus   regimen   is   use   of   premixed   or  biphasic   insulins.   The   TDD   of   insulin   should   be   calculated   based   on   body   weight  as   given   above.  Premixed   regimens   are   a   less   flexible   and   a   less   physiologic   way   of   delivering  insulin   in   T1D   patients.

EDLIZ   2025  357  Monitoring   insulin   therapy   in   diabetes   management  Self-monitoring   of   blood   glucose   (SMBG)   using   hand-held   blood   glucose   device  is   mandatory   for   all   T1D   patients   on   insulin   therapy     The   8-point   SMBG   daily   profile   is   a   useful   tool     The   3-day   profiling   of   SMBG   gives   actionable   data   much   as   a   7-day   profiling  would   do  Self-Monitored   Blood   Glucose   (SMBG)   profile   testing   (the   8-point   SMBG  template).  Key:   BG   =   blood   glucose  Time   Targets  BG   test   at   0600   hrs   OR   before   breakfast   4-7   mmol/L  BG   test   2   hrs   after   breakfast   4-   Less   than   9mmol/L  BG   test   immediately   before   lunch   4-7   mmol/L  BG   test   2   hrs   after   lunch   4-   Less   than   9mmol/L  BG   test   immediately   before   supper   (dinner)   4-7   mmol/L  BG   test   2   hrs   after   supper   (dinner)   Less   than   9   mmol/L  BG   test   at   bedtime   OR   at   2200   hrs   4-9   mmol/L  BG   test   at   3AM   >4mmol/L  Pharmacologic   management   of   T2D   patients.  Patients   diagnosed   with   T2D   should   be   offered   dietary   counselling   and   lifestyle  modification   strategies   from   the   time   of   diagnosis.   However,   these   non-  pharmacologic   measures   although   good.  All   T2D   first   line  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  metformin   po   B   V   500mg   to  1000mg  2   times   a   day   indefinitely  [max   2g/   day]  Contraindications   to   Metformin   use:   severe   renal   failure   (eGFR   <   30   ml/min),  suspected   or   confirmed   lactic   acidosis,   severe   liver   disease,   intolerable  gastrointestinal   (GI)   upsets   even   after   switch   to   extended   release   (ER)   Metformin  and   dose   reduction.

EDLIZ   2025  359  Monitoring   Glycemic   control   in   T2DM.  The   goals   of   diabetes   care   include   achieving   and   maintaining   the   HbA1c   at   an  individualized   target.  Targets:  Group   Target  Most   people   HbA1c   <   7%  Younger   T2D-   with   no   complications,   no  hypoglycaemia   risk,   good   support   system   and   are  motivated  HbA1c   <   6.5%  Older   T2D   individuals   with   no   complications   7-7.5%  Older   individuals   with   significant   complications  (hypoglycaemia   risk,   longer   duration   of   diabetes,   age-  related   incapacities,   polypharmacy)  HbA1c   7.5-   8.5%  Patients   with   heart   failure,   coronary   artery   disease   and   chronic   kidney   disease  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  dapagliflozin   po   B   V   10mg   once   daily   Long   term  D IABETIC   D IET  Ideally   a   dietician   should   calculate   dietary   requirements   for   individual   patients.  Aim   of   diet:   to   reduce   the   blood   sugar   to   normal   and   to   maintain   a   constant   blood  sugar   level.     45-50%   of   energy   intake   should   be   in   the   form   of   carbohydrates;   the   amount   of  carbohydrates   should   be   consistent   from   day   to   day.     Complex   carbohydrates   are   preferable   to   simple   sugars.     Carbohydrates   and   calories   should   be   evenly   distributed   throughout   the   day.  Meals   must   not   be   missed .   A   diabetic   on   insulin   may   have   snacks   between  meals.     An   adequate   intake   of   fibre   is   important.     Alcohol   is   NOT   RECOMMENDED   in   Diabetics.     Sugar   and   sugar-containing   food/drinks   should   be   totally   avoided.   The   only  exceptions   are   when   a   patient   feels   faint   or   is   ill   and   cannot   eat   normally.

EDLIZ   2025  360     Exercise   should   be   encouraged.   A   snack   should   be   taken   before   and   after  playing   sport.     Unrefined   carbohydrate,   e.g.   Roller   Meal,   wholemeal   flour,   is   preferable   to  refined   starches.     Special   preparations   for   diabetics   are   safe   but   not   “ diet ”   drinks.   100%   fruit  juices   and   diet   sodas   should   be   totally   avoided   in   Diabetics.  G ENERAL   A DVICE   FOR   D IABETICS  All   diabetic   patients   should   have   a   "medic-alert"   bracelet   or   necklace  Syringes   /   Insulin   Storage:     Store   syringes   dry.     Do   not   sterilize   insulin   syringes     use   insulin   syringe   only   once   to   guarantee   sterility     Insulin   should   be   stored   in   a   cool   place.  Injection   technique     Clean   and   dry   skin.   Inject   subcutaneously   at   right   angles   90   degrees   to   the   skin  counting   up   to   6-10   seconds   before   removing   the   needle.     The   site   of   injection   should   be   varied   (abdomen   and   thighs,   shoulders,   gluteal  region   and   back   of   arm   are   the   most   suitable   sites).  .  Foot   Care   for   Diabetics:     Advice   about   foot   care   is   important:   keep   feet   clean   and   dry,   wear   well-fitting  shoes,   and   take   care   to   avoid   blisters   and   burns.   Healthcare   service   provider   to  screen   for   diabetic   foot   at   each   review   visit.  Ophthalmological   Examinations:     At   least   annually   from   the   time   of   diagnosis;   monitor   and   record   acuities   (each  eye   separately).   If   acuity   drops,   look   for   cataracts.   Refer   to   an   eye  hospital/specialist.     Pregnant   women   should   be   screened   for   retinopathy   in   each   trimester   and  preconception   because   retinopathy   can   get   worse   in   pregnancy.

EDLIZ   2025  361  Blood   pressure   control:     Good   BP   control   is   essential   and   is   more   effective   at   preventing   complications  than   good   glycaemic   control.   Use   combinations   of   medicines,   preferably  including   an   ACEI,   target   BP   <140/80.   If   Chronic   Kidney   Disease   (eGFR   <  60ml/hr   or   proteinuria)   target   BP   <   120/80.  Aspirin   and   diabetes     Use   for   secondary   prevention   (coronary   artery   disease,   stroke,   peripheral  vascular   disease,   high   cardiovascular   risk,   chronic   kidney   disease,   peripheral  neuropathy)  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  aspirin   po   C   E   75mg   once   a   day   indefinitely  For   those   that   are   allergic   to   Aspirin   or   have   an   intolerance:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  clopidogrel   po   B   E   75mg   once   a   day   indefinitely  Lipid   control  All   type   2   diabetes   should   be   on   a   statin   e.g.   Atorvastatin   20mg   oral   daily   for   the  management   of   hyperlipidaemia.  Type   1   diabetics   with   complications   or   above   40   years   should   be   started   on   a   statin.  Smoking :     Patients   with   diabetes   should   avoid   smok ing .  Sexual   Dysfunction:     Patients   ( m ales   and   f emales)   MUST   always   be   asked   about   sexual   dysfunction  and   referred   accordingly,   since   it   is   a   marker   of   vascular   disease.     They   can   be   offered   sildenafil   or   tadalafil   tablets   after   evaluation   for   endocrine  causes   that   test   for   HbA1c,   prolactin,   TSH   and   fasting   morning   total  testosterone.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  sildenafil   po   B   E   25mg   once   a   day   when   necessary

EDLIZ   2025  362  Oral   Care:     Good   oral   h ygiene   should   always   be   encouraged,   and   patients   should   have  annual   dental   check- ups   with   a   dentist   or   dental   therapists .  Gastrointestinal   upset:     e.g.   vomiting,   diarrhoea   or   constipation   must   be   checked   often   as   they   are   an  indicator   of   complications.   Metformin   can   cause   GIT   upset   and   can   be   replaced.  Diabetic   Clinics     Are   useful   to   focus   care   even   at   District   Hospital   level.   Six   monthly   reviews  should   include   eye   checks,   checking   for   peripheral   neuropathy,   checking   for  foot   problems,   oral   care,   sexual   dysfunction   and   BP.  S PECIAL   P ROBLEMS   IN   D IABETICS  Pregnancy     Oral   anti-diabetic   agents   can   be   used   (with   S pecialist   guidance)   and   very   strict  glycaemic   control   is   necessary.   See   the   chapter   on   Obstetric   and   Gynaecological  conditions.  Infections   and   Other   Major   Illnesses     Both   types   of   diabetics   (Type   1   and   Type   2)   may   need   to   be   given   an   increased  dose   of   infusion   or   Basal   Bolus   Insulin   (see   insulin   therapy   in   adults).   In   Type   1  diabetes   NEVER   stop   insulin,   even   if   the   patient   is   unable   to   eat .   After   a  hypoglycaemic   event   correct   with   20mls   of   50%   dextrose   and   restart   the   insulin  at   the   appropriate   dose.   Do   not   stop   insulin   in   a   type   1   diabetic.     Insulin   as   a   BASAL   BOLUS   REGIMEN   is   the   anti-hyperglycaemic   treatment  of   choice   in   patients   admitted   to   Hospital.   There   is   no   role   for   subcutaneous  sliding   scale   in   inpatient   diabetes   management.   For   critical   patients   in   HDU   and  ICU   a   variable   rate   intravenous   insulin   infusion   (VRIII)   can   be   used,  Hypoglycaemia   and   Hypoglycaemic   Coma     Always   educate   patients   and   document   in   the   notes   about   hypoglycaemic  symptoms   (hunger,   sweating,   irritability,   etc). .  Clinical   presentation:  Adrenergic   features:     s weating,   pallor,   tremor,   palpitations,   tachycardia   and   hunger

EDLIZ   2025  363  Neuroglycopaenic   features     c onfusion,   s eizures   &   c oma     Patients   on   oral   hypoglycaemic   agents   and   insulin   must   carry   sweets   or   glucose  tablets.   The   patient ’ s   close   relatives   must   also   be   instructed   in   the   management  of   hypoglycaemic   attacks.   Metformin   cannot   cause   hypoglycaemia.  D EFINITION   OF   HYPOGLYCAEMIA  Capillary   glucose   <   3.9   mmol/L  Management   of   hypoglycaemia:  In   type   1   diabetics,   administer   glucagon   1mg   IM   stat.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  glucagon   im   A   V   1mg   stat   once  If   level   of   consciousness   is   depressed,   give   50   mL   of   50%   dextrose   by   bolus  intravenous   injection.   Check   blood   glucose   every   15   minutes.   Repeat   25   mL   of  50%   dextrose   intravenously,   every   15   minutes   until   blood   glucose   is   4   mmol/L.  5%   dextrose:   infused   slowly   (50-60   mL   per   hour)   after   injection   of   50%   dextrose  and   titrated   according   to   capillary   plasma   glucose   levels.   Sulfonylurea-induced  hypoglycaemia   may   be   prolonged   and   glucose   infusions   may   be   needed   for   2-3  days.   The   patient   should   take   oral   carbohydrate   as   soon   as   possible   after   the   initial  management   with   50%   dextrose.     In   the   event   of   confusion   or   coma   in   a   diabetic   on   treatment   and   in   the   absence  of   reliable   blood   sugar   readings,   there   should   be   no   hesitation   in   administering   a  trial   injection   of   intravenous   dextrose.  If   the   above   are   unavailable,   small   quantities   of   sugar   or   preferably   glucose,   may  be   placed   inside   the   cheeks   and   will   be   eventually   swallowed   or   absorbed   through  the   buccal   mucosa.  If   awake   and   normal   conscious   level   give   fast   acting   sugars   eg   glucose   tablets,  100%   juices,   sweets   or   consider   giving   IV   dextrose   as   above.  S URGERY  Diabetic   patients   requiring   surgery   SHOULD   ALWAYS   BE   THE   FIRST   ONES  ON   THE   SURGERY   LIST   and   are   best   cared   for   by   S pecialists.   Refer   wherever  possible.  In   the   case   of   diabetic   patients   on   oral   agents ,   stop   the   oral   medicines   and  commence   Basal   Bolus   Regimen.

EDLIZ   2025  364  If   NIL   BY   MOUTH  ■   Basal:   50%   of   Total   daily   dose   insulin   (TDDI)     Long-acting   insulin   at   bedtime   or   morning     Or   Intermediate   insulin   twice   a   day   (50/50   or   2/3   am   and   1/3   pm)     Or   Insulin   INFUSION   (preferred   if   prolonged   NPO,   ICU   or   ketosis   prone)  ■   Correction     Rapid   acting   insulin   sc   every   4   hours     Or   Regular   insulin   sc   every   6   hours     check   blood   sugar   -   if   less   than   5   mmol/L   set   up   5%   dextrose   infusion   to   run  slowly,     Do   hourly   sugar   checking   intraoperatively     check   blood   sugar   post-operatively   and   if   nil   per   mouth   administer   Basal   and  Correction   Insulin   only.  Hyperglycaemic   Coma   &   Pre-coma   (Adults)     Pass   a   nasogastric   tube   and   allow   free   drainage   in   the   unconscious   or  semiconscious   patient. F LUID   R EPLACEMENT   (A DULTS )     Diabetic   Ketoacidosis   DKA-   always   give   insulin   first   at   0.1   units   per   kg  followed   by   fluids.     Hyperosmolar   hyperglycaemic   state   (HHS)-   always   give   fluids   first   followed   by  insulin   at   0.05   units   per   kg   per   hour     Sodium   chloride   0.9%   is   the   recommended   fluid   (Ringers   lactate   can   be   used   as  well);   as   much   as   8   litres   may   be   required   in   24   hours:   The   schedule   given   is   a  guide.   Be   flexible  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose –   fluid   rate  sodium   chloride   0.9%   iv  infusion  C   V   first   litre  second   litre  third   litre  fourth   litre  fifth   litre  over   1   hour  over   2   hours  over   4   hours  over   6   hours  over   8   hours  Give   subsequent   litres   of   sodium   chloride   0.9%   every   8   hours.   Monitor   closely  during   the   period   of   infusion   and   modify   accordingly,   e.g.   take   into   account   skin  turgor,   peripheral   perfusion   and   urine   output.

EDLIZ   2025  365  CAUTION:   Fluid   overload   is   dangerous   in   elderly,   heart   failure   and   CKD  patients.     The   above   regimen   may   need   to   be   modified   depending   on   the   state   of   hydration  or   the   cardiovascular   status   of   the   patient.     Beware   of   hypernatremia   by   monitoring   electrolytes   and   be   prepared   to   change  to   a   hypotonic   solution,   e.g.   5%   dextrose   if   appropriate,   or   half   sodium   chloride  0,9%   (prepared   by   diluting   normal   saline   by   50%   with   water   for   injection).     When   blood   sugar   falls   to   1 4 mmol/L   change   to   dextrose   5%   (or   10%)   (or   if  urinary   ketones   can   be   measured),   set   up   5%   dextrose   (or   10%)   infusion   if  ketones   moderate   or   strong   and   blood   sugars   <1 4 mmol/L.  Potassium   Replacement     In   conditions   where   blood   potassium   levels   cannot   be   determined,   add   to  intravenous   fluid:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose  add   potassium   chloride   iv  infusion  B   V   20mmol   with   every   litre   after   the   first   litre.  Increase   to   40mmol   /   litre   given   over   8hrs.     Where   serum   potassium   levels   are   available   start   replacement :  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose  potassium   chloride   iv  infusion  B   V   20mmol   /   litre   as   soon   as   insulin   has   been  started.     Assess   serum   potassium   regularly   and   adjust   replacement   as   needed   to  maintain   potassium   at   4.0-5.0mmol/per   litre.     Continue   with   oral   replacement   for   one   week   if   not   in   renal   failure:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  potassium   chloride   po   B   V   600   –  1200mg  twice   a   day   7   days  DKA   I NSULIN   MANAGEMENT   (A DULTS )  Patients   preferentially   to   be   managed   with   protocol   1   in   a   High   Care   ward,   with  appropriate   monitoring.  PROTOCOL   1:   -   continuous   intravenous   infusion   (fixed   rate   intravenous   insulin  infusion):     Give   a   bolus   intravenous   injection   of   0.1   units/kg   short   acting   insulin   followed  by   a   continuous   intravenous   infusion.

EDLIZ   2025  366     Mix   50   units   short   acting   insulin   in   200   mL   isotonic   saline   -   thus   4   mL   solution  contains   1   unit   of   insulin     Initial   infusion:   0.1   unit/kg/hr   (usually   5-7   units   per   hour:   20-28   mL/hr)     When   plasma   glucose   <   14   mmol/L,   change   the   IV   fluid   to   5%   dextrose   (or   10%  if   available).   Aim   to   get   rid   of   ketonuria.   Once   ketones   are   no   longer   there,   the  insulin   doses   can   be   reduced   as   the   blood   glucose   is   also   controlled.   NOTE :  Ketonemia   takes   longer   to   clear   than   hyperglycaemia   and   combined   insulin   and  glucose   (and   K + )   are   needed   to   ensure   clearance   of   ketonemia.   Avoid   focusing  on   glucose   control   alone!  PROTOCOL   2 :   -If   no   access   to   insulin   infusion,   hourly   intramuscular   or  intravenous   bolus   injections:     Hourly   doses:   0.1   unit/kg   either   by   intramuscular   or   intravenous   injection   with  hourly   bedside   capillary   glucose   level   until   ketones   negative.   Change   IV   fluids  to   10%   dextrose   once   sugar   less   than   14   mmol/L  PROTOCOL   3:     Initially   given   by   intramuscular   injection   (be   careful   not   to   inject   into  subcutaneous   fat,   use   intramuscular   needles   and   in   very   obese   patients   use   the  deltoid   region),   see   below:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  soluble   insulin   im   B   V   10units   immediately,   then  5units   hourly   until   blood   sugar   down   to  14mmol/L     When   the   blood   sugar   is   14mmol/L   or   less   and   the   clinical   condition   shows   clear  improvement,   change   to   subcutaneous   administration   but   continue   to   monitor  blood   sugar   hourly   until   the   level   ceases   to   fall   (the   intramuscular   injection   may  continue   to   act   for   some   hours   through   a   depot   effect).   Then   give   insulin  according   to   Basal   Bolus   regimen.  Common   problems   with   using   the   Sliding   Scale   Only   (No   longer   recommended.  Basal   Bolus   Regimen   Preferred)  1-   Is   reactive   rather   than   proactive  2-   Often   mismatched   with   changes   in   patient ’ s   insulin   sensitivity  3-   It   does   not   meet   the   physiologic   needs   of   the   patient  4-   Leads   to   insulin   stacking

EDLIZ   2025  367  ⮩   Blood   Sugar  [mmol/L]  Soluble   Insulin  >16   12   units  >12-16   8   units  >8-12   4   units  <8   0.5   units  Sliding   scales   using   URINE   glucose   tests   are   unreliable   -   avoid.  Metabolic   conditions   in   children  Hypoglycaemia   in   Children  Common   causes   of   hypoglycaemia   include   excessive   insulin   dose,   skipping   meals,  and   exercise.   In   children   who   are   awake-   give   100%   juice,   glucose   tablet   or   a  sweet,   to   increase   the   glucose   fast.   Thereafter   maintain   glucose   level   with   a  carbohydrate   e.g.   sandwich  If   unconscious   give:  Medicine   Codes   Paed   dose   Freq   Duration  10%   dextrose   iv   A   N   2mg/kg/hr  or   glucagon   iv   A   V   <25kg   -   0.5mg  >25kg-   1mg  stat   once  Maintain   with   a   dextrose   containing   fluid   until   the   child   is   able   to   eat   or   drink.  Check   glucose   level   every   20   min   until   normal.  C hildren   with   Hyperglycemic   States  Hyperglycaemic   Coma   and   Pre-coma   (Children)  NB.   Refer   to   the   appropriate   local   guideline   used   by   paediatricians   for   Diabetic  ketoacidosis   (DKA)   and   hyperosmolar   hyperglycaemic   state   (HHS)   at   the  quaternary   hospitals.  Priorities:     Fluid   replacement     Electrolyte   /   acid-base   monitoring     Insulin   therapy     Blood   glucose   monitoring  Use   blood   sugar   reagent  strips   or   glucometer  readings.   Do   not   rely   totally  on   these   readings-   also   use  clinical   judgement.

EDLIZ   2025  368  Fluid   Replacement     Approximately   200   ml/kg   in   24   hours   is   required   for   rehydration.     Start   with   rapid   infusion   of:  Medicine   Codes   Rate  sodium   chloride  0.9%   iv   infusions  Total   volume   =  200ml/kg   in   24   hours  C   V   20ml/kg   fast,   then  ½   the   remaining   volume   in   8hrs,   then  ½   the   remaining   volume   in   16hrs.  and   potassium   chloride  infusion  B   V   add   20mmol/L   after   the   initial   20mg/kg  fast   infusion.     Monitor   glucose   levels   hourly:   when   the   blood   sugar   is   less   than   15mmol/l  change   to:  Medicine   Codes   Rate  half   strength   Darrows  with   5%   dextrose   iv  infusion   *  C   V   see   section   on   iv   fluid   replacement  and   potassium   chloride   iv   B   V   20mmol   per   litre   of   the   ½   Darrows/   dextrose  solution  *   Made   up   by   adding   50mls   of   50%   dextrose   to   1   litre   ½   Darrow ’ s   with   2.5%  dextrose.     Monitor   U/E   2-4   hourly   watching   the   potassium   levels.  Insulin   Therapy   (Children)  Medicine   Codes   Dose   Rate  soluble   insulin   iv  (initial   –   continuous  infusion)*  B   V   0.1units/  kg/   hr  until   blood   sugar   falls   below  15mmol/L,   then  (*e.g.   make   up   infusion   of  insulin   in   normal   saline)  0.05units/  kg/   hr  until   condition   stabilises,   then  soluble   insulin   sc  (maintenance)  B   V   0.75   –   1unit/kg/day   in   3   divided   doses   before  meals   for   one   day,   then  soluble   +   isophane   sc   B   V   apply   the   rule   of   thirds,   (2/3   of   the   total   daily  dose   in   the   morning   and   1/3   in   the   evening).  e.g.   30kg   child  Initial   fluid   requirement   =   (30   x   200mls)   =   6   Litres

EDLIZ   2025  369  ▪   initial   bolus   of   600mls   normal   saline   fast  ▪   followed   by   (5.4L   /   2)   =   2.7L   in   next   8   hrs   and   2.7L   in   next   16hrs  Initial   insulin   =   (30   x   0.1)   =   3   units   iv   continuous   infusion,  ▪   slowing   to   (30   x   0.05)   =   1.5units/hr   when   glucose   <   15   mmol/L  ▪   when   stable   ( +   0.75   –   1   unit   x   30kg   /   day)   e.g.   24   units   per   day   (24/3)   =  8   units   3   times   a   day   before   meals  ▪   then   after   24hrs,   24   units   isophane   and   soluble   insulin/day:   2/3   isophane:  10   units   am6   units   am  1/3   soluble:   6   units   am;   4   units   am  Honeymoon   period  In   the   months   after   initial   diagnosis   insulin   requirements   may   decline   to   less   than  0.5   unit/kg/day   as   the   pancreas   continues   to   produce   some   endogenous   insulin.  Requirements   invariably   revert   to   higher   doses   as   endogenous   insulin   levels  decline.   Explain   the   concept   to   the   patient   or   relatives.  Note:   Diet   is   important   in   children   but   attempts   at   too   rigid   control   may   prove   to  be   counterproductive.   The   child   living   with   diabetes   should   be   allowed   to   indulge  in   normal   activities   at   school.   Teachers   need   to   be   informed   about   the   condition  (Loco   parentis   should   apply).  Endocrinological   Conditions.  NB.   In   general   most   endocrinology   patients   are   best   referred   or   discussed   on   a  telemedicine   platform   with   Endocrinology   Specialists.  Endocrine   diseases   include   young   hypertension   patients   below   44   years,   pituitary  disease,   thyroid   diseases,   parathyroid   gland,   adrenal   diseases,   testicular   and  ovarian   failure,   calcium   disorders,   obesity,   neuroendocrine   tumours,   other  electrolyte   problems   such   as   hyponatraemia,   hypokalaemia   and   puberty   and  growth   failure.  Thyroid   Disease  Anyone   with   Goitre   needs   referral   for   work   up.  Points   in   Management  A   patient   with   a   visible   goitre   will   need   a   complete   history   and   examination.  Investigations   include   a   TSH.   Ultrasound   of   the   thyroid   by   a   qualified   thyroid  ultrasonographer   and/or   radiologist   will   be   useful   to   exclude   features   suspicious   of  cancer.

EDLIZ   2025  370  The   initial   treatment   for   thyrotoxicosis   includes   propranolol   while   awaiting   full  thyroid   results   (TSH,   FT4   and   FT3).  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  propranolol   po   B   E   40   – 160   mg   3   times   a   day   -  Graves ’   disease     Treat   initially   with   anti-thyroid   medicines:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  carbimazole   po   B   E   20   -60mg  [0.5   mg/kg]  after   initiating   treatment   refer   to  specialist   for   titration  CAUTION:   Carbi mazole   induce   bone   marrow   suppression;   advise   patient   to  report   sore   throat   or   other   signs   of   infection.   Stop   medicine   immediately   if  neutropenic.   Minor   rashes   are   not   an   indication   to   stop   treatment.  Check   thyroid   function   at   5-6   weeks   and   if   normalised,   gradually   reduce   the   dose  to   the   lowest   that   will   maintain   euthyroidism.   Continue   carbimazole   for   one   year  from   time   of   stabilisation.   If   poor   response,   relapse   or   clinically   very   severe,   refer  for   radioiodine   or   surgery.  NB:   after   radio-iodine   therapy   for   Graves ’   disease,   long-term   follow   up   is  essential   to   detect   late   hypothyroidism   that   might   otherwise   remain   neglected   and  untreated.  Toxic   Nodular   Goitre   [including   toxic   adenoma]     Where   available   Radioiodine   at   the   appropriate   centre   is   recommended,   particularly  in   older   patients   and   those   with   other   medical   problems.     Surgery   is   particularly   suitable   for   those   with   a   large   goitre.   As   radioiodine   may   take  three   months   or   longer   to   produce   a   clinical   effect,   propranolol   may   be   continued  uninterrupted   and   carbimazole   may   be   restarted.   Patients   must   be   rendered   euthyroid  prior   to   thyroidectomy   by   use   of   anti-thyroid   medicines.     Aqueous   iodine   oral   solution   may   be   administered   for   10-14   days   before  thyroidectomy:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  Aq.   iodine   solution  (Lugol ’ s   iodine)  130mg   iodine/ml  A   N   0.1   –   0.3ml  diluted   in  milk  3   times   a  day  10-14   days   before  surgery

EDLIZ   2025  371  Hypothyroidism  Except   in   iodine   deficient   areas,   this   is   treated   by   thyroid   hormone   replacement  whatever   the   cause:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  thyroxine   po   B   V   50   -   100mcg  initially  once   a   day   4   weeks,   then  increase   by   25   -   50mcg   every   four   weeks   as   necessary   until   euthyroid     Start   at   25mcg/day   in   the   elderly   or   those   with   heart   disease.     Typical   adult   replacement   dose   is   1.6mcg/kg/day   [i.e.   100mcg   daily].     Larger   doses   are   needed   in   infancy   [10   -   15mcg/kg/day]   and   childhood  [4mcg/kg/day].     Close   monitoring   of   clinical   response   including   weight   monitoring   and   thyroid  function   tests   (T4,   TSH)   is   essential.  Hypoadrenalism  May   be   primary   (Addison ’ s   disease)   or   secondary   to   pituitary   failure,   e.g.   as   a  result   of   surgical   or   irradiation   ablation   of   the   pituitary   gland.  Requires   specialist   physician/endocrinologist   investigation   and   advice   on   how   to  treat   the   patient.  Surgery   or   illness   necessitates   an   increase   in   corticosteroid   cover   generally   in   the  form   of   hydrocortisone   parenterally   in   the   acute   phase,   followed   by   oral  prednisolone   in   a   higher   than   usual   dosage   as   the   condition   improves.   Patients   on  long   term   corticosteroid   who   develop   infection   or   are   subjected   to   surgery   also  require   additional   steroid   cover   as   above.  Adrenal   Crisis   (Systolic   BP   less   than   100mmHg),  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  hydrocortisone   inj   C   V   100mg   stat  0.9%   Normal   Saline   1   litre   in   first   hour,   check   supine   and   erect   BP

EDLIZ   2025  373  I NFECTIONS   OF   THE   NERVOUS   SYSTEM  The   usual   presentation   of   meningoencephalitis   is   with:     headache   and   fever     and/or   altered   level   of   consciousness     and/or   neck   stiffness     and/or   focal   neurological   signs     and/or   seizures.  In   infant ’ s   irritability,   lethargy   and   failure   to   suck   are   important   signs .   Neck  stiffness   is   an   unreliable   sign   under   one   year   of   age,   but   the   detection   of   a   bulging  fontanelle   supports   the   diagnosis.  Differential   diagnosis  Headache   and   fever   only:   look   for   cause   of   fever   in   other   systems   (for   example,  chest,   respiratory   tract;   urinary   tract,   etc.).   Always   exclude   malaria  If   there   is   altered   level   of   consciousness,   neck   stiffness,   focal   signs   or   seizures   in  the   presence   of   fever,   or   fever   with   lethargy   and   failure   to   suck   in   infants:     Give:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  benzylpenicillin   iv/im   C   V   3g   (5MU)   one   dose   refer  Note:   for   dose   in   children   see   chapter   on   Paediatric   conditions     Transfer   urgently   to   a   secondary   care   centre.  M ENINGITIS  Management   of   suspected   meningitis   (fever   +   headache   +   altered   alertness )   at  District   level   (or   higher):     Urgent   lumbar   puncture.     Blood   slide   for   malaria   parasites.     If   diagnosis   is   in   doubt   DO   NOT   perform   a   lumbar   puncture.   Refer   to   a  higher   level.     Contraindications   to   lumbar   puncture :   deeply   unconscious   +   focal   signs;   one  pupil   large   and   unresponsive;   papilledema   (if   fundoscopy   available);   rapidly  falling   level   of   consciousness.   These   are   indications   for   referral   to   a   tertiary   care  centre.     Lumbar   puncture   should   be   considered   mandatory,   and,   preferably,   when   the  condition   is   first   suspected   since   Cryptococcal   meningitis   must   always   be  excluded.

EDLIZ   2025  374     Tuberculous   meningitis   should   always   be   suspected. Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ceftriaxone   im   C   V   500mg   once   only   single   dose  Note:   for   dose   in   children   see   chapter   on   Paediatric   conditions  Further   management  The   combination   of   fever   and   focal   neurological   signs   is   an   indication   for   referral  to   a   central   hospital   and   CT   scan   of   the   head.  The   differential   diagnosis   includes   cerebral   abscess,   cryptococcal   meningitis,  tuberculoma,   toxoplasma   encephalitis,   and   other   parasitic   infection.  If   a   focal   contrast-enhancing   lesion   or   multiple   lesions   are   present   on   scan   and   the  patient   is   known   to   be   HIV   infected   or   is   suspected   to   be   infected   on   clinical  grounds,   start   treatment   for   toxoplasmosis:
