This   is   a   rare   but   potentially   fatal   side   effect .   Discontinuation   of   the  antipsychotic   is   essential   and   an   emergency   referral   made   to   a   physician   at   a  central   hospital.   During   the   transferring   of   the   patient,   there   is   need   to   take   care   of  rehydration   of   the   patient,   nutritional   and   fever   control.   Renal   failure,   hypoxia   and  acidosis   should   be   managed   at   a   referral   centre.   Bromocriptine   may   be   used   in  doses   of   2-3mg/kg   body   weight   maximum   40mg/day   and   not   for   more   than   10days.

EDLIZ   2025  410  MOOD   (AFFECTIVE)   DISORDERS  Bipolar   Disorders  It   is   a   condition   characterised   by   elation   (mania)   and   low   mood   (depression).  Treatment   depends   on   presentation.   If   acutely   disturbed   use   of   antipsychotic  medication   for   rapid   tranquilization   is   recommended.   (as   per   section   on  psychosis).   Thereafter   use   antipsychotics   as   for   section   on   antipsychotics   but  avoid   Sulpiride   and   Clozapine   for   this   group   of   patients.  Treatment   is   as   for   other   psychoses   i.e.   with   antipsychotics   but   add   mood  stabilisers.  Use:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  carbamazepine   po   B   V   100-400mg   3   times   daily   continual  or   sodium   valproate   po   B   V   200-500mg   2   times   daily   continual  or   lamotrigine   po   B   E   50-200mg   2   times   a   day   continual  &/or   lithium   carbonate   po   B   V   250mg-1g   At   night   continual  ▪   For   HIV/AIDS   patients   use:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  sodium   valproate  po  B   E   200   –   500mg   2   times   daily   continual  or   lamotrigine   po   B   E   50-200mg   2   times   daily   continual  Note:   Avoid   carbamazepine   in   HIV   and   AIDS   patients.  In   manic   patients   with   psychoses,  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  risperidone   S   E   1   –   2mg   2   times   a   day   Continual  or   olanzapine   po   S   E   2.5   –   5mg   2   times   a   day   Continual  or   quetiapine   po   S   E   50-200mg   O nce   a   day   Continual  HIV   Induced   Mood   Disorders  For   rapid   tranquilization,   avoid   chlorpromazine,   use   Haloperidol   and  benzodiazepines   as   described   above

EDLIZ   2025  411  Blood   tests   for   FBC,   U&E,   Thyroid   function   and   Pregnancy   test   are   essential  before   commencing   mood   stabilizers.   These   medicines   should   be   used   with  caution   during   pregnancy   especially   within   the   first   trimester.   Lithium   levels   are  mandatory   for   pregnant   patients.  Carbamazepine   may   induce   liver   enzymes   and   hence   causing   more   rapid  metabolism,   and   therefore   reduced   efficiency   of   co-administered   medicines   e.g.  ARV ’ s   and   Oral   Contraceptives.  Lamotrigine   is   associated   with   skin   rashes-   discontinue   treatment   if   this   occurs.  Lithium   toxicity   can   occur   with   dehydration,   diarrhoea   and   vomiting.   Hence   the  need   to   discontinue.   At   toxic   levels   this   may   cause   tremor,   in-coordination,   ataxia,  coma   and   death.   If   toxicity   occurs   Lithium   should   be   stopped   immediately   and   a  saline   drip   started   –   1   litre   fast,   then   4   hourly   -   and   the   patient   should   be   referred   to  a   central   hospital.  Depression  Assess   severity   and   duration,   identify   stressors,   and   carry   out   risk  assessments   for   suicide.     Depressive   Episode   (Mild)  Counsel,   follow   up   and   help   individual   to   deal   with   stressors.   Commence  patient   on   antidepressants   preferably   with   Selective   Serotonin   Re-Uptake  Inhibitors   (SSRIs).     Major   Depression  Use   of   anti-depressants   and   close   monitoring   of   the   patient   are   key.  First   Line   Medicines:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  fluoxetine   po   C   V   20-60mg   once   daily   in  morning   with   food  assess   response  after   2   weeks  or   citalopram   po   B   V   10-40mg   once   daily   morning   assess   response  after   2   weeks

EDLIZ   2025  412  Alternate   First   Line  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  or   amitriptyline  po  C   V   Start   at   50mg   and  increase   by   25mg  every   2   nights   up  to   150mg  Once   at   night  1   hour   before  sleep  Assess   progress  after   2   weeks  or   imipramine  po  B   V   Start   at   50mg   and  increase   by   25mg  every   2   nights   up  to   150mg  Once   at   night  1   hour   before  sleep  Assess   progress  after   2weeks  Second   Line   Medicines:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  venlafaxine   po   S   E   75mg   Once   daily   in   the  morning   with   food  continual  or   duloxetine   po   S   E   30mg   Once   daily   in   the  morning   with   food  continual  or   mianserin   po   S   E   15-40mg   Once   at   bedtime   continual  Caution :   History   of   Epilepsy,   history   of   Mania,   cardiac   disease,   Diabetes   Mellitus,  close   angle   glaucoma,   bleeding   tendency   or   anticoagulant   therapy,   hepatic   or   renal  impairment   and   breast   feeding.  Side   Effects :  “ First   flood   effect ”   with   increased   restlessness   or   agitation   (This   may   be   managed  by   reduced   dosage   or   with   short   term   usage   of   a   Benzodiazepine.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  diazepam   po   C   V   5mg   [up   to   10mg]   once   a   day   max   2   weeks  or   clonazepam   po   A   E   0.5mg   upto   3   times   a   day   max   2   weeks  Caution:   Do   not   prescribe   for   more   than   two   weeks.   If   agitation   persists,   refer   to  specialist.  Other   side   effects   include :  Gastro-intestinal   upsets;   appetite   reduction;   reduced   libido.   Some   patients   may  have   a   hypersensitivity   reaction   with   skin   rash.   In   general,   medicine   should   be  stopped   if   any   of   these   untoward   events   occur  “ Serotonin   Syndrome ”   is   a   toxic   over-activity   of   serotonin   which   may   rarely   occur  with   therapeutic   dosage   of   an   SSRI   but   occurs   more   commonly   because   of   usage

EDLIZ   2025  413  of   more   than   one   medicine   acting   on   the   serotonin   system.   Symptoms   of   varying  severity   include:  Autonomic   effects   –   shivering,   sweating,   raised   temperature,   high   blood   pressure,  tachycardia,   nausea   and   diarrhoea.  Motor   effects   –   myoclonus   or   muscle   twitching,   brisk   tendon   reflexes   and   tremor.  Cognitive   effects   –   restlessness,   hypomania,   agitation,   headache   and   coma.  Management   involves   immediate   cessation   of   the   offending   medicine/s,   usage   of   a  benzodiazepine   for   agitation   and   supportive   care.  Medicines   may   cause   reduced   libido.  SSRIs   cause   insomnia   -   always   take   the   dose   in   the   morning;   where   there   is   sleep  disturbance,   limited   use   of   benzodiazepines   like   clonazepam   1-   2mg   at   night   or  lorazepam   0.5   –   1mg   can   be   given   at   night   for   a   maximum   of   2   weeks.  Patients   with   Bipolar   Affective   Disorder   in   the   depressive   phase   may   need   both   an  antidepressant   and   a   mood   stabiliser   (Refer   to   section   under   mania   above)     For   depression   with   psychomotor   retardation   use:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  fluoxetine   po   C   V   20   –   60mg   once   only   continual  For   depression   with   psychomotor   agitation   give:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  amitriptyline   po   B   V   50   – 75mg   once   at   night   *14-   21days   then  review or   imipramine   po   A   E   50   – 75mg   once   at   night  *It   may   take   up   to   14   -   21   days   before   therapeutic   effect   occurs.  Do   not   issue   large   quantities   of   antidepressant   medicines;   tricyclic  antidepressants   can   be   fatal   in   overdose!  CAUTIONS :   Avoid   both   amitriptyline   and   imipramine   in   patients   with   history   of  heart   disease,   urinary   retention;   glaucoma   and   epilepsy   [refer   such   patients   to   a  specialist].   In   elderly   patients,   start   with   25-50   mg/day.   Imipramine   is   less   sedating  than   amitriptyline.  Side   effects:   Common   side   effects   include   dry   mouth,   blurring   of   vision,   postural  hypotension,   appetite   increase   and   constipation.  With   Venlafaxine   usage,   an   EEG   is   recommended   prior   to   initiating   the   medicine  and   blood   pressure   needs   careful   monitoring.

EDLIZ   2025  414  T REATMENT   OF   A LCOHOL   USE   DISORDERS  Mild   to   Moderate   Alcohol   Use   disorder  Alcoholism   is   the   excessive   and   recurrent   use   of   tobacco   despite   medical,  psychological,   social   or   economic   problems   and   includes   tolerance   and   withdrawal  symptoms.   Counsel   patient;   involve   family   or   others   as   appropriate.   Referral   to  support   groups   such   as   Alcoholic   Anonymous   are   recommended.   The   person   must  stop   alcohol   use   OR   reduce   consumption   to   not   more   than   14   units   per   week   [men]  or   not   more   than   7   units   per   week   [women].   These   units   should   ideally   be   spread  out   over   the   week,   not   drunk   all   at   once.   Drinking   heavily   on   one   occasion   is  called   binge   drinking   and   it   is   hazardous   to   one ’ s   health.   One   should   not   drink  every   day,   ideally   one   should   have   2   to   3   alcohol   free   days   a   week  1   unit   of   alcohol   =   200ml   of   beer   (5%   weight/volume),   or   one   glass   of   wine  (250mls),   or   one   tot   (25mls)   of   any   spirit  OR   use   formula:   number   of   units   =   volume   in   ml   X   percentage   alcohol  1000  Severe   Alcohol   use   disorder  Manage   physical   and   social   complications.   Counsel   patient,   with   family  involvement.   Alcohol   use   must   be   stopped   preferably   while   the   patient   is   admitted  for   close   monitoring.   If   the   last   alcoholic   drink   was   taken   in   the   last   7   days,   a   fixed  dose   diazepam   reducing   regimen   should   be   considered.   In   addition,   vitamin  supplementation   should   also   be   considered.

EDLIZ   2025  415  Detoxification  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  diazepam   iv   C   V   10mg   once   only   then  diazepam   po   C   V   10mg  3   times   a  day   [reduce  by   5mg  every   other  day]  discontinue  within   7   –  14days  or   c hlordiazepoxide   A   N   50mg   3   times   a  day   and  taper   off  then  discontinue  over   7   to   14  days  and   multivitamins   po   C   N   2   tablets   once   a   day   review  or   thiamine   po   A   N   100mg   once   a   day   review  and   Vit   B   Complex  im  C   V   1ml   daily   for   3  days  These   vitamin   replacements   protect   against   the   development   of   Wernicke ’ s  encephalopathy   (ophthalmoplegia,   ataxia,   confusion   and   altered   level   of  consciousness).   Care   should   continually   be   made   to   ensure   that   there   are   no   signs  of   withdrawal.   If   such   should   occur,   the   dose   of   oral   diazepam   should   be   increased  and   titrated   to   effect.   The   total   doses   of   diazepam   should   not   exceed   60mg   per   day.  Patients   with   hepatic   problems   or   comorbidities   may   be   given   the   shorter   acting  drug   lorazepam   instead   of   diazepam   or   chlordiazepoxide.  General   supportive   Care  Patients   in   alcohol   withdrawal   should   preferably   be   treated   in   a   quiet   room   with  low   lighting   and   minimal   stimulation.   All   patients   with   seizures   or   delirium  tremen s   (DT)   should   have   immediate   intravenous   access   for   administration   of  drugs   and   fluid s.   Fluid   and   electrolyte   imbalances   must   be   promptly   corrected.  Adequate   nutrition   must   be   ensured,   and   all   patients   should   receive   supplemental  thiamine.

EDLIZ   2025  416  Management   of   moderate   to   severe   alcohol   withdrawal   syndrome  a.   Without   seizures   or   DT  A   fixed   dose   regimen   described   above   may   be   used.   Continued   monitoring   for  signs   of   withdrawal   should   be   done   and   if   present,   appropriate   top-up   doses   should  be   given   as   need   arises.  b.   Severe   alcohol   withdrawal   with   delirium   tremens  An   initial   dose   of   10   mg   diazepam   is   given   intravenously   and   prompt   transfer   to   an  ICU   setting   made   where   frequent   intravenous   doses   of   diazepam   are   given  according   to   the   attending   intensivist   assessment.  Alcoholic   hallucinosis  This   is   a   unique   form   of   withdrawal   related   psychosis   which   can   begin   even   while  the   person   is   continuing   to   use   alcohol   or   begins   after   he   stops   alcohol.  Hallucinations   occurring   in   clear   sensorium   are   the   hallmark   of   this   disorder.   The  patient   may   be   given   low   doses   of   antipsychotics   like   chlorpromazine   100-200  mg/day   or   risperidone   1-3   mg/day   to   control   severe   agitation   due   to   hallucinations.  The   hallucinations   last   about   a   week   in   most   cases   but   may   last   up   to   1   month   in  some   patients   after   which   the   antipsychotic   can   be   stopped.  Wernicke   Encephalopathy  Parenteral   thiamine   should   be   given   at   500mg   for   3-5   days   is   advised.  Aversion   Drugs  1.   Disulfiram  Disulfiram   is   used   as   an   aid   in   the   management   of   chronic   alcohol   use   disorders   in  patients   motivated   to   stop   taking   alcohol.   This   helps   such   patients   to   benefit  immensely   from   the   longer-term   psychotherapy.   When   used   alone,   disulfiram   is  not   a   cure   for   alcohol   use   disorder   there   is   need   for   proper   motivation   and  supportive   therapy   to   have   substantive   effect   on   the   drinking   pattern   of   the   patient.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  disulfiram   po   A   E   500mg   once   only   1-2   weeks  then   125   –   250mg   once   daily   until   patient   no   longer  in   need   of   adjunctive  to   abstain

EDLIZ   2025  417  Warning  ●   Disulfiram   should   not   be   taken   with   metronidazole   or   paraldehyde,   or   with  any   foods   or   products   that   contain   alcohol   (mouthwash,   cough   medicine,  cooking   wine   or   vinegar,   certain   desserts,   and   others)   due   to   the   severe  painful   aversion   reaction   that   may   occur   (nausea,   vomiting,   headaches,   chest  pain,   weakness,   difficulty   breathing,   anxiety).  ●   Antabuse   should   never   be   given   to   a   person   without   his   or   her   knowledge   of  taking   the   medicine.  2.   Naltrexone  Naltrexone   reduces   the   pleasure   one   normally   gets   from   alcohol.   Without   this  satisfying   feeling,   people   with   alcohol   use   disorder   may   be   less   likely   to   drink  alcohol.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  naltrexone   po   A   E   50mg   once   only   as   advised   by   psychiatrist  3.   Acamprosate  Indicated   for   maintenance   of   alcohol   abstinence   in   patients   who   are   abstinent   at  treatment   initiation.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  acamprosate   po   A   E   666mg   three   times   a  day  as   advised   by   a  psychiatrist  ●   For   renal   impairment   reduce   to   333mg   po   tds.  Substance   induced   psychosis  To   be   considered   substance-induced   psychosis,   the   hallucinations   and   delusions  should   be   more   than   those   that   typically   accompany   simple   substance   intoxication  or   withdrawal,   although   the   patient   may   also   be   intoxicated   or   withdrawing.   The  precipitating   substances   include   alcohol,   cannabis,   amphetamines   (e.g   Crystal  Meth),   opioids,   cocaine,   hallucinogens,   and   phencyclidine   (PCP).  Antipsychotics   and   benzodiazepines   may   be   used   as   outlined   under   the   section   on  psychoses.  Dementia  The   goal   of   treatment   is   to   manage   behavioural   problems   and   focus   less   on  memory   losses.   This   may   just   involve   psychosocial   support   and   improving  adaptation   of   the   patient   to   their   environment.

EDLIZ   2025  418  Presenting   symptoms   of   dementia   are   similar   to   those   of   depression   therefore   there  is   need   to   rule   out   depression.  Normally   patients   who   are   able   to   perform   daily   activities   but   have   memory  retardation   are   most   likely   suffering   from   depression   and   pseudo   dementia   (false  dementia).   Treatment   of   depression   as   outlined   in   the   depression   section   (avoiding  use   of   Tricyclic   Antidepressants   like   Amitriptyline)   can   reverse   the   symptoms   of  memory   loss   and   overall   function.  Associated   symptoms   of   psychosis   and   bizarre   behaviour   can   be   managed   with   a  low   dose   antipsychotic   like   Sulpiride;   Quetiapine   or   Haloperidol   as   recommended  in   the   section   on   Psychoses.   Benzodiazepines   (low   dose)   like   Bromazepam   3mg  note   or   Clonazepam   0.5mg   PRN   may   also   help   when   the   symptoms   are  intermittent   or   are   disruptive.   Diazepam   must   be   avoided   as   it   causes   heavy  sedation   and   is   addictive.   Mood   stabilizers   e.g.   Carbamazepine   and   Sodium  Valproate   are   also   useful.  Comorbid   physical   conditions   and   sensory   deficits   need   to   be   treated.   When   there  is   establishment   of   the   cause   of   the   neurocognitive   dysfunction   there   are   better  chances   of   recovery   and   symptoms   can   be   reversed.  Delirium  Control   of   acute   onset   abnormal   behaviour   is   paramount   as   failure   to   do   so   can  result   in   patient   death.  The   principles   of   care   remain   the   same.   This   section   is   mainly   covered   under  neurological   conditions   which   is   a   separate   (page- ... .)   subtopic   and   under   organic  psychosis

EDLIZ   2025  419  C HILD   AND   A DOLESCENT   M ENTAL   D ISORDERS  Developmental   delays   and   disorders  Assess   all   domains   –   motor,   cognitive,   social,   communication,   emotional   and  adaptive  COMMON   PRESENTATIONS   OF   CHILD   &   ADOLESCENT   MENTAL   &  BEHAVIOURAL   DISORDERS   BY   AGE   GROUP  Age   Developmental  Disorders  Behavioural  Disorders  Emotional   Disorders  Infants  and  children  <5  Delayed   milestones   for  appropriate   age   (smiling,  sitting   interaction   with  others;   or   regression   of  milestones,   delayed  speech,   lack   of   imitative  play   interacting   with  others,   walking,   talking,  toilet   training,   repetitive  stereotypical   behaviours  and   interests,   resistance  to   change  Also   consider;   nutritional  deficiency,   including  iodine   deficiency,  anaemia,   malnutrition,  lack   of   stimulation,  hearing   impairment   in  cases   of   delayed   speech,  acute   or   chronic  infectious   illness,  including   ear   infection  and   HIV/AIDS   as   causes  of   delayed   development  (from   age   4   to   18)  Symptoms   of  Attention   Deficit  Hyperactivity  Disorder   (ADHD)  Overactivity  (excessive   running  around,   extreme  difficulties   remaining  seated,   excessive  talking)  Inattention   (absent  mindedness,  difficulty   completing  tasks)  Impulsivity  (frequently   doing  things   without  forethought)  Symptoms   present   in  multiple   settings   and  lasting   for   at   least   6  Excessive   crying,  clinging   to   caregiver,  “ freezing ” ,   extreme  shyness,   milestone  regression,   Diminished  initiation   of   play   and  social   interaction,  Difficulty   sleeping,  eating

EDLIZ   2025  420  months   with  considerable  difficulty   with   daily  functioning   in  personal,   family,  social,   educational,  occupational   or   other  areas.  Physical   conditions  that   can   resemble  ADHD .  Thyroid   diseases   –  Acute   or   chronic  infectious   illness,  including   HIV/AIDS  –   Uncontrolled   pain  e.g.,   from   an   ear  infection,   sickle   cell  disease  Disruptive  behavioural  disorders  Repeated   behaviour  that   disturbs   others  (unusually   frequent  and   severe   tantrums,  cruel   behaviour,  persistent   and   severe  disobedience,  stealing)  Sudden   changes   in  behaviour;  withdrawal,   anger  (maybe   suggestive   of  emotional   disorders  such   as   depression   or  trauma   related  disorders)  Middle  Childhoo  d   (6-12  years)  Delay   in   reading   and  writing,   self-care  Assess   the   child   for  visual   and/or   hearing  impairment:  Is   child   able   to   follow   a  moving   object   with   the  head   and   eyes,   grab   an  object   –   recognize  familiar   people?  Does   the   child   turn   head  to   see   someone   behind  them   when   they   speak   or  show   reaction   to   loud  noise?  Recurrent   unexplained  physical   symptoms   (e.g  stomach-ache,  headache,   nausea),  reluctance   or   refusal   to  go   to   school,   extreme  shyness,   milestone  regression  Adolesce  nts   (13-  18   years)  Poor   school   performance,  difficulty   understanding  instructions,   difficulty   in  social   interaction   and  adjusting   to   change  Problems   with   mood,  anxiety/worry/  irritability,   frustration/  depressed   mood/  extreme   or   rapid  changes   in   mood/  emotional   outbursts  Deterioration   in  functioning   (difficulty  concentrating,   poor  school   performance,  social   withdrawal)  All   ages   Difficulty   carrying   out  age-appropriate   daily  activities,   difficulty   in  social   interactions   and  adjusting   to   change,  difficulties   or   oddities   in  communication,  restricted   or   repetitive  behaviour   patterns  Excessive  fear/anxiety/avoidance  of   situations   or   objects  Changes   in   sleeping   or  eating   habits  Diminished   interest   or  participation   in  activities  Oppositional/attention  seeking   behaviour  (Modified   from   the   WHO   mhGAP   version   2.0)

EDLIZ   2025  421  These   generally   do   not   need   pharmacological   management.   Provide  psychoeducation   and   support,   connect   family   with   available   community   resources  then   refer   to   specialist   for   further   assessment   and   management   plan .  Treatment   of   ADHD  Offer   an   ADHD-focused   group   parent-training   behavioural   modification  programme   to   parents   or   carers   of   children   under   5   years   with   ADHD   as   first-line  treatment.  F or   children   aged   5   years   and   over   and   young   people   with   ADHD   a   combination  of   medications   and   behavioural   modification   techniques   can   be   used.  First   Line   Medicines:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  methylphenidate   po   S   V   5-30mg   twice   daily   continual  or   methylphenidate  SR   po  S   V   18-54mg   (children)  18-72   mg  (adolescents)  once   daily  once   daily  continual  continual  or   dextroamphetamine  po  S   V   5-20mg   twice   daily   continual  Second   line  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  atomoxetine   po   S   V   5-40mg   twice   daily   continual  All   medication   for   ADHD   should   only   be   initiated   by   a   healthcare   professional  with   training   and   expertise   in   diagnosing   and   managing   ADHD .  Baseline   assessment   and   monitoring  1. Current   medications  2. History   of   cardiovascular   disease  3. Height   and   weight   (Methylphenidate   may   cause   growth   retardation)  4. Pulse   and   blood   pressure   (methylphenidate   may   cause   tachycardia   and  hypertension)  5. Sleep   pattern   (methylphenidate   can   cause   severe   insomnia)  Comorbidities   in   child   and   adolescent   mental   health  Quite   often   comorbidity   in   children   and   adolescents   is   the   rule   rather   than   the  exception.   Behavioural   disorders   are   common   in   children   with   epilepsy.   ADHD

EDLIZ   2025  422  often   co-occurs   with   epilepsy   and   developmental   disorders   such   as   autism  spectrum   disorder   and   intellectual   disability   (formerly   mental   retardation)  Autism   spectrum   disorders   (ASDs)  Core   features   of   autism   include   deficits   in   social   communication   and   social  interaction   such   as   delayed   or   absent   speech,   echolalia;   repetitive   stereotypical  behaviours   such   as   repetitive   motor   movements,   restricted   repetitive   or   ritualistic  behaviours   interests   or   activities   and   resistance   to   change.   In   addition,   there   may  be   aggression,   irritability   and   self-injurious   behaviour  Management   of   ASDs  General   principles  Assessment   management   and   coordination   of   care   should   be   done   by   specialists.  Interventions   are   aimed   at   improving   communication   and   activities   of   daily   living  and   consist   mainly   of   behavioural   modification,   social   skills   training,   speech  therapy   and   educational   interventions.  Medications   in   ASDs  Psychotropic   medications   such   as   risperidone   and   aripiprazole   are   sometimes   used  to   manage   uncontrollable   irritability,   hyperactivity,   self-mutilation   and   repetitive  motor   stereotypes.   Psychotropics   should   only   be   considered   after   behavioural   and  environmental   interventions   have   been   inadequate   or   the   child   poses   a   safety   risk.  Medications   are   mainly   prescribed   for   comorbid   conditions   such   as   epilepsy   and  ADHD.   However   psychotropic   medications   such   as   risperidone   can   be   used  Medication   Code   Initial   dose   Titration   Recommended  dose  risperidone   S   V   0.25  mg/day  (<20kg)   or  0.5mg/day(  >20kg)  Increase   by  0.25mg/day  (,20kg)  Or   0.5mg   /day  (>20kg)   after   4  days   and   every   2  weeks   thereafter  0.5mg/day-  3mg/day   given  once   daily   or   in   2  divided   doses  or   aripiprazole   S   V   2mg/day   Increase   to   5mg  /day   after   7   days  then   increase   by  5mg   every   7  days  5mg-10mg/day  given   once   a   day

EDLIZ   2025  423  Conduct   disorders   (CD)  Conduct   disorders   are   characterized   by   repetitive   persistent   patterns   of   antisocial  aggressive   and   defiant   behaviour   that   results   in   significant   and   persistent   violation  of   age-appropriate   social   expectations   and   rules.  Management   of   CD  The   mainstay   management   approaches   are   psychosocial   and   behavioural  interventions   mostly   focused   on   parent   training,   anger   management,   problem  solving   techniques   and   primary   prevention   measures.  Pharmacological   interventions   are   used   mainly   to   target   comorbid   conditions  such   as   ADHD   and   epilepsy.   Antipsychotics   such   as   risperidone   and   aripiprazole  can   be   used   to   manage   aggressive   behaviour   not   responding   to   psychosocial   and  behavioural   approaches.   Please   refer   to   the   dosing   guide   above.  Mood   stabilizers   such   as   carbamazepine   can   also   be   used   for   the   management   of  aggression   in   CD.  Treatment   of   Depression   in   children   and   adolescents  Focus   Action   Medications  Mild   depression   Individual   Cognitive  Behavioural   Therapy  (CBT)   ,   group   CBT,   group  Interpersonal   Therapy   (IPT)  None  Moderate   to   severe  depression  Family-based   IPT,   family  therapy,   CBT  +/-   Fluoxetine  Depression  unresponsive   to  treatment/recurrent  depression/psychotic  depression  Intensive   psychological  therapy  +/ –   fluoxetine,  sertraline,   citalopram,  augmentation   with   an  antipsychotic  Antidepressant   medications  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  fluoxetine   po   C   V   2.5-40mg   once   at   least   6   months  or   citalopram   po   B   V   10-20mg   once   at   least   6   months  or   sertraline   po   B   E   25-200mg   once   at   least   6   months

EDLIZ   2025  424  Psychosis   in   children   and   adolescents  For   management   of   psychosis   in   adolescents,   please   refer   to   the   section   on  psychosis   in   children   Although   uncommon,   psychosis   can   occur   in   young   children  as   young   as   5   years   old,   and   pre-adolescent   children.   Because   of   the   complexity   in  presentation   of   psychosis   in   children,   these   children   should   be   referred   for  assessment   and   management   by   mental   health   specialists.  Elimination   disorders  Enuresis  Elimination   disorders   involve   the   inappropriate   elimination   of   urine   (enuresis)   or  faeces   (encopresis),   that   are   usually   first   diagnosed   in   childhood   or   adolescence.  Enuresis   is   defined   as   repeated   voiding   of   urine   into   the   bed   or   clothes   whether  involuntary   or   intentional,   at   least   twice   per   week   for   at   least   three   consecutive  months   in   a   child   who   is   ≥   5   years   of   age,   resulting   in   significant   distress.   It   can  occur   during   daytime   (diurnal)   or   during   nighttime   (nocturnal).   Primary   enuresis   is  bed   wetting   that   occurs   in   a   child   who   has   never   achieved   dryness.   Secondary  enuresis   is   onset   of   enuresis   after   previous   achievement   of   dryness   for   at   least   6  months.  Evaluation   and   management   approaches   for   enuresis  A   careful   history   should   be   obtained   and   a   thorough   physical   examination   done,  urinalysis,   urine   culture.   Urinary   system   ultrasonography   and   urodynamic   studies  should   be   performed   where   indicated   to   exclude   medical/surgical   causes.   If   a  medical   condition   is   suspected   the   patient   should   be   referred   to   the   appropriate  specialist   discipline.   Psychiatrists   are   mainly   involved   in   the   treatment   of   enuresis  where   medical   causes   have   been   excluded.   Behavioural   approaches   are   the   first  line   treatment   for   uncomplicated   enuresis   due   to   non-medical   causes.  Urotherapy   and   behavioural   approaches   in   the   management   of   enuresis  Standard   urotherapy   involves   educating   families   regarding   enuresis   and   its  treatment,   offering   suggestions   for   voiding   patterns   and   frequency,   limiting   fluid  intake,   and   treating   constipation   when   present.   Nocturnal   enuresis   is   best   treated  with   positive   reinforcement   and   enuresis   alarms.   Offer   an   alarm   as   the   first-line  treatment   to   children   and   young   people   whose   bed   wetting   has   not   responded   to  advice   on   fluids,   toileting   or   an   appropriate   reward   syste m

EDLIZ   2025  425  N.   B.   Given   its   higher   success   rate   and   lower   relapse   rate,   an   alarm   system  should   be   considered   as   first-line   treatment   for   many   children  What   to   do   How   to   do   it  Psychoeducation   of  parents   /   caregivers  Prevalence   of   enuresis,   reassurance,   support,   and  avoidance   of   punishment   and   humiliation   (emphasize  the   involuntary   nature   of   bed   wetting)  Maintain   voiding   diary  record  Measurement   of   voiding   volume  Fluid   intake   regulation   Decrease   fluid   intake   especially   caffeinated   beverages  Awakening   child   at   night  to   void  Involves   awakening   child   at   regular   times   to   void,   pre-  empting   the   time   of   bed   wetting  Double   voiding   before  bedtime  Double   voiding   is   urinating   at   the   beginning   of   the  bedtime   routine   and   then   again   just   before   falling   asleep  Positive   reinforcement   e.g.,   the   child   puts   stickers   on   a   chart   or   earns   points   for:  1.   drinking   recommended   levels   of   fluid   during   the   day  2.   using   the   toilet   to   pass   urine   before   sleep  3.   engaging   in   management  (e.g.   taking   medication   or   helping   to   change   sheets  Once   a   certain   number   of   stickers   or   points   have   been  earned,   the   child   is   given   a   prize  Enuresis   alarms  (Have   the   highest   cure  rate   and   lowest   relapse  rate   compared   to   other  behavioural   approaches  and   pharmacological  approaches,   although  response   is   generally  slower   than  pharmacological  approaches.   It   may   take  up   to   one   to   two   months  to   see   an   improvement.)  Fixed   bell   and   pad   or   portable   transistorized   alarms  worn   by   the   child.   Response   is   not   immediate   and  treatment   should   be   continued   for   2-3   months   or   until  the   child   is   dry   for   14   consecutive   nights

EDLIZ   2025  426  Pharmacological   management   of   Enuresis  First   line:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  desmopressin   po   S   V   0.2mg  titrate   to   0.4mg  max   0.6mg  once   at   night  Contra-indicated   in   renal   impairment,   hyponatraemia   and   known   hypersensitivity  Second   Lin e:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  imipramine   po   A   E   25mg  (6-12years)  titrate   up   to   50mg  25mg  titrate   up   to   75mg  or   (12-18   years)  1   hour   before  bed  at   night  Contra-indicated   in   children   <6   years.   Maximum   dose   can   be   2.5mg/kg  Encopresis  Encopresis   is   the   repeated   passage   of   stool   in   inappropriate   places   for   at   least   3  months   after   the   age   of   4   years.   It   can   be   divided   into   constipation   associated   or  non-retentive   encopresis   and   classified   as   primary   (faecal   continence   never  achieved)   or   secondary.  Assessment  An   extensive   medical   history   should   be   carried   out,   including   the   history   of   rectal  or   anal   surgery   due   to   possible   malformations   or   congenital   disabilities.  Management  Health   workers   should   evaluate   for   a   history   of   psychological   trauma   or   new-onset  life   stressors   such   as   bullying,   losing   close   family   members,   or   parental   separation.  Psychiatrists   can   be   called   upon   to   manage   encopresis   in   causes   where  medical/surgical   causes   have   been   excluded.  Treatment   of   encopresis   includes   dietary   changes,   behavioural   interventions,   and  medications.   For   medical   management   of   encopresis   refer   to   the   relevant   sections.

EDLIZ   2025  427  Behavioural   interventions   include   positive   feedback   and   reinforcement   from  parents,   addressing   coexisting   behavioural   problems   and   psychological   referrals   if  needed.  HIV/AIDS   and   Mental   Health  There   is   a   complex   interlink   between   HIV   and   Mental   Health   problems     HIV   can   have   a   direct   neurobiological   effect   on   the   brain   and   thus   increases   the  risk   of   certain   mental   health   disorders   (e.g.   depression,   anxiety,   psychosis)     HIV   also   has   a   psychological   effect   on   patients   as   a   chronic   illness   requiring  lifelong   treatment,   health   anxiety,   stigma,   issues   of   guilt   and   self-blame   can   also  be   prominent     Some   mental   health   conditions   can   also   increase   risky   behaviours   and   increase  risk   of   contracting   HIV   (e.g.   disinhibition   in   mania,   intellectual   disability)     Some   HIV   medications   may   also   affect   mental   health   (e.g.   Zidovudine,  Efavirenz)     Mental   health   problems   can   affect   adherence   to   HIV   medications   and  compromise   HIV   outcomes  Assessment   for   and   management   of   mental   health   problems   is   integral   to   holistic  HIV   care     Pre   and   post   Test   Counselling   is   vital   in   providing   psychosocial   support   from  the   start   of   the   treatment   journey     Discuss   the   reason   for   testing   and   assess   risk   behaviour   and   discuss   risk  reducing   interventions     Discuss   the   meaning   of   and   possible   reactions   to   a   positive   result     Address   fears   and   anxiety     Interpret   the   result   clearly     Discuss   prevention   of   transmission   and   disclosure     Explore   available   psychosocial   support   were   indicated     Screening   for   common   mental   health   disorders   using   standardized   tools   should  be   part   of   the   routine   assessment   in   HIV   care   clinics   (e.g.   PHQ   9,   SSQ)   with  appropriate   referral   were   indicated     Common   mental   health   problems   that   can   occur   in   HIV

EDLIZ   2025  428  Condition   Symptoms   in   HIV   setting   Medication   Concerns   in   HIV  setting  Adjustment  disorders/  Acute   Stress  Disorder  Transient   anxiety   symptoms   that  can   occur   at   diagnosis   or   during  any   other   crisis   lasting   for   hours  to   days.   May   include   emotional  numbing,   panic   symptoms,  somatic   symptoms .  Provide   psychosocial   support   and  reassurance .  Usually   resolves   without  m edication .  Anxiety   Symptoms   similar   to   non-HIV  settings.   Health   related   anxiety  may   be   prominent  Refer   to   section   on   treatment   of  anxiety.  *Consider   lower   doses   of   SSRIs .  Depression   Symptoms   similar   to   non-HIV  settings.   Physical   health  problems   may   mask   somatic  symptoms   of   depression .  Refer   to   section   on   treatment   of  depression.  *Consider   lower   doses   of   SSRIs  HIV   related  psychosis  Symptoms   similar   to   non-HIV  settings.  Increased   risk   of   extrapyramidal  side   effects   and   neuroleptic  malignant   syndrome   with   high  doses   of   typical   antipsychotic  drugs .  **Consider   low   doses   of   atypical  antipsychotics   e.g.   Risperidone  HIV   related  mania  Similar   to   non-HIV   setting .   Can   be   the   result   of   direct  neurobiological   effect   of   HIV   but  can   also   result   from   medications  used   in   HIV   and   OI   care   (e.g.  zidovudine,   isoniazid,  ganciclovir) .  Refer   to   section   on   treatment   of  mania   however  Avoid   typical   antipsychotics .  ***Avoid   Carbamazepine .  **Use   low   doses   of   atypical  antipsychotics   were   indicated .  Delirium   Similar   to   non-HIV   setting .   Refer   to   section   on   treatment   of  delirium  Use   low   doses   of   atypical  antipsychotics   +/-   low   doses   of  benzodiazepines****  Substance   Use   Can   often   occur   due   to   self-  medication   for   pain,   depression  and   anxiety .  Screen   for   these   as  comorbidities .  Refer   to   section   on   treatment   of  substance   use   disorders .  ****Use   low   doses   of  benzodiazepines   where   indicated .

EDLIZ   2025  429  HIV  Associated  Neurocognitiv  e   Disorders  (HAND)  Similar   to   non-HIV   setting   but  with   marked   physical   symptoms  such   as   psychomotor  retardation,   tremor,   impairment  of   rapid   repetitive   movements  and   eye   coordination,   poor  balance   and   ataxia,   hypertonia  and   hyperreflexia .  Early   treatment   with   ARVs,  reducing   viral   load   may   help   slow  down   progression .  Protease   Inhibitors,   Non-Nucleotide   Reverse   Transcriptase   Inhibitors   and   to   lesser  extent   Nucleotide   Reverse   Transcriptase   Inhibitors   inhibit   metabolism   by  cytochrome   P450   liver   enzymes   therefore:  *Some   ARVs   may   increase   levels   of   SSRIs   increasing   risk   of   serotonin   syndrome  **Some   ARVs   may   increase   serum   levels   of   antipsychotics   increasing   risks   of  EPSEs   and   NMS  ***   Carbamazepine   may   induce   liver   enzymes   and   hence   causing   more   rapid  metabolism,   and   therefore   reduced   efficiency   of   co-administered   medicines   e.g.  ARV ’ s  ****   Some   ARVs   may   decrease   clearance   of   benzodiazepines   increasing   risk   of  respiratory   depression   and   oversedation  Anxiety   Disorders  Mild   to   Moderate  Use   of   psychosocial   interventions   like   relaxation   exercises,   breathing   exercises  and   talk   therapy   by   a   trained   person.  Severe  Acute   Onset   Symptoms  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  diazepam   iv/pr   C   V   5-10mg   nocte   5-10days  or   promethazine   im   C   V   25-50mg   stat  or   lorazepam   im   B   E   1mg  1mg  stat  nocte  then  3-5   days  or   clonazepam   po   A   E   0.5mg  0.5mg  stat  PRN  (not   >4doses   in  24hours)  3-5   days  or   propranolol   po   B   V   40mg  40mg  stat  once   daily  then  5-10   days

EDLIZ   2025  430  Intermediate   to   long   term/chronic   anxiety   treatment  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  amitriptyline   B   V   25-50mg   nocte   3-6months  or   imipramine   po   B   E   25-50mg   nocte   3-6months  or   fluoxetine   po   C   V   20-40mg   once   daily  (morning   dose)  6-12   months  or   citalopram   po   A   E   20-40mg   once   daily  (morning)  6-12   months  or   sulpiride   po   B   V   50-200mg   once   daily  (Nocte)  6-12   months  Trauma   Related   Disorders  Treatment  Use   of   talk   therapy   by   a   trained   perso nel.  Medication   use   also   depends   on   presentation   (acute   or   chronic)   as   outlined   above  Obsessive   compulsive   and   related   Disorders  Treatment   is   like   long   term   treatment   for   anxiety   disorders.   In   addition,   long   term  psychotherapy   is   also   needed   to   change   the   behaviour   in   the   long   term  Sleep   Disorders  Sleeping   medications   can   be   addictive   and   if   given,   care   should   be   taken   on   the  duration   they   are   given.   It   is   generally   not   recommended   to   rely   on   prescription  sleeping   pills   for   more   than   2   weeks,   and   other   remedies   should   be   used   instead.  These   include   sleep   hygiene,   treatment   of   any   underlying   mental   health   issues,   use  of   other   psychological   services,   and   avoidance   of   alcohol   and   other   drugs   of   abuse.  Examples   of   medications   which   can   be   used   include:  ●   Melatonin   C-DRUG  ●   Antihistamines   C-DRUG  A   psychiatrist   should   assess   the   patient   and   determine   the   doses   of   medication   to  be   given .

EDLIZ   2025  431  Temporal   Lobe   Epilepsy  Please   read   the   Psychosis,   Depression   and   Epilepsy   section   before   reading   this.  These   are   seizures   that   often   present   with   episodic   mood,   anxiety,   and   behavioural  changes   whose   management   is   covered   in   the   above   sections.   The   patient  experiences   varying   levels   of   loss   of   consciousness,   and   memory   loss,   for   the  event   afterwards.   Please   note   some   of   the   medication   will   reduce   seizure   threshold  like   Chlorpromazine   and   Amitriptyline.   Anti-epileptic   medicine   are   enzyme  inducers   so   please   note   their   drug   interaction   with   other   medication   e.g.,   Anti-  retroviral,   hormonal   contraception   etc.  First   line   treatment  Health   workers   who   have   undergone   training   in   the   recognition   and   management  of   epilepsy   may   initiate   treatment   at   primary   care   (C)   level.   Otherwise   refer   to  District   level.     If   two   or   more   typical   seizures   in   the   past   12   months   in   a   patient   over   2   years  Plus     normal   physical   examination,   no   neurological   signs,   start:  Medicine   Codes   Adult  dose  Frequency   Duration  sodium   valproate  po  B   V   200mg   twice   a   day,   at  night  2   weeks  until  review  or   carbamazepine   po   B   V   200mg   twice   a   day,   at  night  2   weeks  until  review  Review   after   2   weeks   to   consider   increasing   the   dose.   Check   compliance   and   side  effects   (very   sleepy,   loss   of   balance,   rash).   If   side-effects,   reduce   doses   Review  again   after   4   weeks.  Please   note   in   pregnancy,   do   not   use   sodium   valproate   but   lamotrigine   is   first   line  treatment.  Second   line   treatment  For   the   patient   who   fail   to   tolerate   the   above   e.g.   elderly,   pregnant   and   sexually  active   women   consider   changing   to   Lamotrigine.

EDLIZ   2025  432     Second   Line   and   special   groups:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  lamotrigine   po   B   V   50mg   once   a   day,   at  night  1   weeks   until  review  Increase   Lamotrigine   by   50mg   after   every   7   days.   Lookout   for   a   rash   and   Steven  Johnson   syndrome.   If   rash   appears   STOP   lamotrigine   and   refer.

C OMMON   E YE   C ONDITIONS  PREVENTION   OF   BLINDNESS   43 4  MANAGEMENT   OF   EYE   CONDITIONS   43 4  DIAGNOSIS   OF   EYE   CONDITIONS   43 5  ATRAUMATIC   EYE   CONDITIONS   43 5  XEROPHTHALMIA   /VITAMIN   A   DEFICIENCY   43 6  CONJUNCTIVITIS   OF   THE   NEWBORN   4 40  TRAUMATIC   EYE   CONDITIONS   44 1  PENETRATING   INJURY   44 1  CORNEAL   FOREIGN   BODIES   44 1  CORNEAL   ABRASION   44 2  CHEMICAL   BURNS   44 2  IRITIS/   UVEITIS   44 2  CORNEAL   ULCERS   44 2  GLAUCOMA   (CHRONIC   OPEN   ANGLE)   44 3  CATARACT   44 5  EYE   CONSUMABLES   4 4 5

EDLIZ   2025  434  P REVENTION   OF   B LINDNESS  80%   of   blindness   can   be   prevented   by:     proper   diet   (Vitamin   A   and   proteins)     personal   and   environmental   hygiene     measles   immunisation     early   treatment   of   eye   diseases   by   qualified   health   personnel   including   cataract  surgery   and   early   diagnosis   and   treatment   of   glaucoma     appropriate   management   of   STIs   in   pregnant   mothers   and   their   sexual   partners     early   referral   of   serious   eye   diseases   and   injuries     tetracycline   eye   ointment   in   the   new-born   child ’ s   eyes   (Crede   Prophylaxis)     early   detection   and   appropriate   management   of   diabetes   –   stringent   control   of  blood   sugar.   All   diabetic   patients   should   have   screening   of   eye   complications   by  an   ophthalmologist   regularly   starting   from   diagnosis   of   the   disease.     early   detection   and   appropriate   management   of   hypertension     creation   of   community   awareness   on   the   dangers   of   using   herbal   medicines   in  the   eye     persistent   conjunctival   growths   should   be   referred   to   an   ophthalmologist   early,  in   case   it   is   due   to   Ocular   Surface   Squamous   Neoplasia     HIV   positive   patients   must   be   screened   for   eye   complications   by   an   ophthalmic  nurse   or   ophthalmologist   if   symptomatic     patients   with   allergic   conjunctivitis   must   be   discouraged   from   rubbing   eyes   and  appropriate   treatment   should   be   used   e.g.   antihistamines.   Avoid   use   of   topical  steroids   since   they   can   cause   glaucoma   in   steroid   responders   and   early   cataract  formation.  “ Healthy   bodies,   healthy   eyes! ”  Organic   headaches   such   as   migraine   and   cluster   headaches   do   NOT   occur  because   of   eyestrain.   See   the   chapter   on   Nervous   System   Conditions   for  management   of   these   conditions.  Excessive   use   of   eyes   does   NOT   harm   them,   and   “ bad   eyes ”   do   NOT   result   from  overuse.  M ANAGEMENT   OF   E YE   C ONDITIONS  Diagnose   and   refer   urgently   most   of   the   following   conditions:     unexplained   vision   loss;   or   visual   disturbance     glaucoma:   high   pressure   in   the   eye

EDLIZ   2025  435     any   perforating   eye   injury     any   white   pupil   in   children   ( retinoblastoma   or   retinopathy   of   prematurity   or  congenital   cataract)     eye   burns     orbital   cellulitis     red   eyes     retinal   detachment     congenital   or   developmental   squint     conjunctivitis   persisting   after   one   week   of   treatment     trachoma   if   eyelashes   touch   the   cornea     proptosis:   (protrusion   of   the   eyeball )  Important:   Avoid   the   use   of   steroid   eye   preparations;   conditions   requiring   them  need   confirmation   by   a   specialist   -   refer .   Steroids   may   lead   to   worsening   of  infective   processes   like   trachoma,   increased   intraocular   pressure,   cataracts,  delayed   healing   and   worsening   of   corneal   ulcers   of   viral   origin.   Never   use   local  anaesthetic   drops   for   painful   corneal   conditions.   Only   specialists   should   prescribe  atropine   eye   drops/ointment.  D IAGNOSIS   OF   E YE   C ONDITIONS  Testing   vision   is   the   single   most   important   test,   and   every   health   worker   must  know   how   to   use   an   eyesight-testing   chart   e.g.   Snellen   Chart   or   LOGMAR   Chart  for   both   adults   and   E-Chart   or   Landolt   rings   or   pictorial   charts   for   children   and  illiterate   patients.   Visual   acuity   testing   should   be   done   in   a   well   light   room   at  measured   distance   from   the   testing   object.   Use   of   pinhole   can   quickly   give   an   idea  of   patients   whose   vision   is   likely   to   improve   with   use   of   spectacles.  With   a   simple   torch,   most   of   the   external   eye   diseases   can   be   diagnosed.   However,  the   use   of   a   slit   lamp   should   be   standard   practice.  A TRAUMATIC   E YE   C ONDITIONS  See   table   21.1   for   differential   diagnosis  Acute   Glaucoma     Patients   may   present   with   headaches,   severe   eye   pain,   nausea,   and   vomiting.  Examination   shows   ocular   injection,   corneal   haze   and   poor   vision.   Patients   may  see   rainbow-coloured   halos   around   lights.     Refer   immediately   to   the   hospital   (delay   increases   risk   of   visual   loss).

EDLIZ   2025  436     At   hospital:   Patients   suspected   of   having   acute   angle   closure   glaucoma   must   be  started   on   pilocarpine   2%   or   4%   drops   in   both   eyes   four   times   a   day.     Other   measures   which   can   be   taken   include:   application   of   central   corneal  pressure   with   muscle   hook   or   gonioprism.   This   can   abort   recent   onset   of   acute  angle   closure   glaucoma.     Instil   topical   beta   blocker   e.g.   Timolol   0.5%  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  acetazolamide   po   A   N   250-500mg   stat,   then  8   hourly  review     Refer   to   an   Eye   S pecialist   within   24   hours.  X EROPHTHALMIA /   V ITAMIN   A   DEFICIENCY  Preventive   measures   include   promotion   of   breast-feeding,   measles   immunisation,  Vitamin   A   supplementation,   foods   rich   in   Vitamin   A   [carrot,   mango,   pumpkin,  and   paw-paw].   To   all   children   with   signs   and   symptoms   of   xerophthalmia   and/or  measles,   give   and   refer:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  vitamin   A   po   C   V   200,000   iu   [<1yr  =   100,   000   iu]  single   dose   Day   1,   Day   2   and  repeat   after   2  weeks     Nutritional   rehabilitation   is   indicated.

EDLIZ   2025  439  C ONJUNCTIVITIS   ( INCLUDING   TRACHOMA )  Table   21.2:   Differential   Diagnosis:   Causes   of   conjunctivitis  Signs/  symptoms  Acute  bacterial  Viral   Allergic   Chronic,  endemic  trachoma  Discharge?   ✔ Purulent   ✔ Watery   /  none  Mucoid   None   /   purulent  Itching?   None   None   ✔ Marked   None  One   or   both  eyes?  One   or   both   One   or   both   Both   Usually   both  Recurrences?   Unusual   Unusual   Usually   ✔ Chronic  Note:  ✔ Bold   lettering   indicates   distinguishing   feature.  Treatment   of   conjunctivitis:     ▪   Eyelids   hygiene   using   baby   shampoo   or   clean   water   is   important   as   part   of  management   of   these   conditions.     Acute   bacterial   conjunctivitis :  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  tetracycline   1%   eye  ointment  C   V   apply   3   times   a   day   one   week  or   chloramphenicol   1%  eye   ointment  C   V   apply   4   times   a   day   one   week     Viral   conjunctivitis:  No   medicine   treatment   as   this   is   a   self-resolving   infection.   If   in   doubt,   treat   as   for  acute   bacteria   and   refer.     Allergic   conjunctivitis:  Educate/   reassure.   Apply   cold   compresses   and   wear   a   sun   hat   whenever   outdoors.   If  no   relief   of   symptoms   refer.   A   night-time   dose   of   an   antihistamine   may   relieve  symptoms.  NB:   Steroids   are   contraindicated   in   allergic   conjunctivitis

EDLIZ   2025  440  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  olopatadine   (patanol)   1%)  eye   drops  C   V   apply   twice   daily   2   weeks  If   no   improvement   in   the   three   conjunctivitis-   refer  Trachoma:     If   left   untreated,   the   cornea   becomes   permanently   and   irreversibly   damaged.     Apply  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  tetracycline   1%   eye  ointment  C   V   apply   4   times   a   day   for   6   weeks     If   in   turned   eye   lashes   (trichiasis,   entropion)   present,   perform   epilation   (pull   out  the   lashes)   and   refer   the   patient   to   the   eye   hospital.     Provide   education   in   personal   and   environmental   hygiene   for   prevention   of  trachoma,   with   emphasis   on   face   washing,   not   sharing   towels,   hand   washing,  provision   of   safe   water   supplies   and   basic   sanitation.  C ONJUNCTIVITIS   OF   THE   N EWBORN   (O PHTHALMIA   N EONATORUM )  See   also   the   section   on   Sexually   Transmitted   Diseases   and   the   STI   Module.  This   is   defined   as   conjunctivitis   with   discharge   occurring   in   a   neonate   within  the   first   month   of   life.   The   condition   is   commonly   caused   by   gonococcal,  chlamydial   and   bacterial   infection   and   the   newborn   acquires   this   infection  from   an   infected   birth   canal   during   delivery.   The   condition   is   preventable   by  detecting   and   treating   maternal   and   partner   gonococcal   and   chlamydial  infection   during   pregnancy   and   by   swabbing   with   normal   saline   wet   cotton  swab   both   eyes   as   soon   as   the   baby ’ s   head   is   out   followed   by   carefully  instilling   1%   tetracycline   eye   ointment   (Crede   Prophylaxis)   into   the  conjunctival   sacs   of   every   newborn   baby   as   soon   as   possible   after   birth.  Ophthalmia   Neonatorum   is   treated   as   follows:     Collect   pus   swab   for   culture   and   sensitive   before   initiation   of   antibiotic  treatment     Eye(s)   Irrigation   with   warm   Normal   Saline   until   all   the   pus   is   removed   and  intermittently   as   long   as   pus   is   still   present.

EDLIZ   2025  441  Instilling   Antibiotic   Eye   Drops   (Ofloxacin   /   Fortified   Gentamicin   0.3%)   hourly   as   long  as   the   eyes   are   still   discharging   and   red   during   the   day   and   1%   Tetracycline   eye  ointment   at   night   until   infection   is   cleared.  Treatment:  Medicine   Codes   Paed   dose   Frequency   Duration  kanamycin   im   C   V   25mg/kg   once   single   dose  and   erythromycin   po   C   V   16mg/kg   6   hourly   14   days  Treat   the   parents   and   the   baby   for   gonococcal   and   chlamydial   infection   as  described   above.   Also   provide   health   education   and   counselling   to   the   parents.  T RAUMATIC   EYE   CONDITIONS  Penetrating   Injury  Treatment:   Put   an   eye   shield   and   ensure   NO   pressure.   Refer   urgently   to   an   eye  hospital.  Administer   the   following   medicines   before   referral:  M edicine   codes   adult   dose   frequency   duration  tetanus   vaccine   im   C   V   0.5mls   once   single   dose  and   paracetamol   po   C   N   500mg   4   times   a   day   if   required  and   amoxicillin   po   C   V   500mg   3   times   a   day   5   days  Corneal   Foreign   Bodies  Gently   attempt   removal   of   foreign   body   with   cotton   wool   tipped   orange   stick.   Use  topical   anaesthetic   like   lignocaine   1   or   2   %   x   2   drops   to   the   eye   to   prevent  discomfort   during   removal   of   corneal   foreign   body.   If   unsuccessful   –   refer   to   eye  hospital .  If   successful:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  tetracycline   1%  eye   ointment  C   V   apply   under   an   eye   pad  for   24hrs,   then   review  3   times   a   day  Chloramphenicol   1%   eye   ointment   may   be   used   instead   of   tetracycline   eye  ointment   above.  If   worse   after   24   hours   –   refer   to   eye   hospital.

EDLIZ   2025  442  Corneal   Abrasion  Apply   an   eye   pad   with   tetracycline   eye   ointment   or   chloramphenicol   1%   eye  ointment   stat   and   advise   bed   rest   for   24   hours,   then   review.   If   worse,   refer   to   eye  hospital  If   improving,   continue   with:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  tetracycline   1%   eye  ointment  C   V   apply   3   times   a   day   4   days  or   chloramphenicol   1%  eye   ointment  C   V   Apply   4   times   a   day   5   days  Chemical   Burns  Refer   after   doing   the   following:  Consider   this   to   be   a   medical   emergency   -   prompt   action   can   save   vision.  Irrigate   the   eye   and   surrounding   areas   thoroughly   using   tap   water   and   a   10ml  syringe   (without   the   needle)   for   30   minutes.   Remove   any   debris   or   foreign   bodies  from   the   eye   if   present.  Then:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  tetracycline   1%   eye  ointment  C   V   apply   under   an   eye   pad   for   24hrs,   then  review  or   chloramphenicol   1%  eye   ointment  C   V   apply,   pad   eye   and   refer  Iritis/   Uveitis  Refer   to   eye   specialist.  Corneal   Ulcers  ( Refer )   NB:   corneal   sensation   must   always   be   tested   with   a   cotton   tip   to   exclude  herpetic   cause   of   corneal   ulcer   which   would   be   treated   with   antiviral   drugs  like   acyclovir

EDLIZ   2025  443  Treatment:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  tetracycline   1%   eye  ointment  C   V   apply   3   times   a   day   5-7   days  or   chloramphenicol   1%  eye   ointment  C   V   apply   4   times   a   day   5   days  G LAUCOMA   (C HRONIC   O PEN   A NGLE )  Glaucoma   is   defined   as   a   progressive   optic   neuropathy   characterised   by   retinal  ganglion   cell   loss   of   the   retinal   fibre   layer   resulting   in   characteristic   changes   in   the  optic   nerve   head.   Features   include   loss   of   visual   field,   cupping   of   optic   disc   and  plus   or   minus   elevation   of   intraocular   pressure.   Types   include   Primary   open   angle  glaucoma   (POAG)   which   is   usually   the   commonest   where   aqueous   drainage   is  obstructed   despite   open   angles,   its   asymptomatic   and   has   a   familial   and   racial  tendency,   ocular   hypertension   and   low-tension   glaucoma.   Diagnosis   is   based   on:  Elevated   intraocular   pressure   (IOP),   Cupping   of   Optic   Disc,   Visual   Field   loss   and  Gonioscopy.   Risk   factors   include   race   /   ethnicity,   increasing   age,   positive   family  history,   thin   central   cornea   etc.  Medical   treatment   comprises   the   following   groups   of   drugs:     Prostaglandin   Analogues,     Adrenergic   Drugs,     Carbonic   Anhydrase   Inhibitors,     Parasympathetic   Agents,     Rho   Kinase   Inhibitors,     Combined   Medications   and     Hyperosmolar   Agents.  Mechanisms   of   action   differ.  A   specialist   must   first   confirm   the   diagnosis   of   glaucoma.   The   treatment   can   then  be   repeated   according   to   the   specialist ’ s   prescription,   provided   the   patient   is  referred   to   the   specialist   every   3   months   for   review.   The   specialists   should   tailor  therapy   for   POAG   to   the   individual   needs   of   the   patient   including   the   target   IOP,  medicine   effectiveness,   cost   and   likelihood   of   compliance.   Starting   with   a   single  medicine   first   is   always   recommended   unless   IOP   is   extremely   elevated   in   which  case   two   or   more   complimenting   medicines   can   be   used.   First   line   therapy   may  include:   Prostaglandin   Analogue,   Beta   Blocker,   Alpha   Adrenergic   Agonist   and  Topical   Carbonic   Anhydrase   Inhibitor.

EDLIZ   2025  444  Prostaglandin   Analogues   preferred   because   of   superior   efficacy,   once   daily   dose,  favourable   safety   profile   when   compared   to   others.  At   hospital:   Chronic   open   angle   glaucoma:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  latanoprost   50  micrograms/mL   eye  drops  B   V   1   drop   in  each   eye  daily   continual  or   latanoprost   50  microgram/mL   &  timolol   5mg/mL  (Xalacom)   eye   drops  B   V   1   drop   in  each   eye  daily   continual  or   timolol   maleate   0.5%  eye   drops  B   V   1   drop   in  each   eye  twice   daily   continual   as   per  recommendation  by   specialist  If   intra-ocular   pressure   is   more   than   40mmHg:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  timolol   maleate   0.5%  eye   drops  B   V   1   drop   twice   daily   continual  or   travoprost   0.004%  eye   drop  B   V   1   drop   daily   continual  or   latanoprost   50  micrograms,   timolol  maleate   5mg/ml  (xalacom )  A   V   1   drop   daily   continual  or   levobunolol  hydrochloride   0.5%  eye   drops  A   N   1   drop   twice   daily   continual  and   *acetazolamide   po   A   N   0.25-1g   divided  doses/day  few   weeks  *NOTE:   This   medicine   should   NOT   be   used   for   more   than   3   continuous   months  without   review   by   a   specialist   and   potassium   chloride   600   mg /day   orally   should   be  given   when   a   patient   is   on   acetazolamide.  Timolol   should   not   be   prescribed   in   Asthma   and   other   Chronic   Obstructive   Airway  Diseases   (COAD)

EDLIZ   2025  445  C ATARACT  Cataract   is   defined   as   the   opacity   of   the   lens.   Causes   may   be   classified   as   acquired  and   congenital.   Aging,   trauma,   inflammation,   drugs   and   metabolic   disorders   like  diabetes   mellitus   are   the   leading   acquired   causes   while   intrauterine   infections,  inheritance   and   congenital   abnormalities   are   the   leading   congenital   causes   of  cataracts.   Treatment   for   cataract   is   surgical   except   for   galactosemic   cataract   which  reverses   when   the   neonate   is   given   a   lactose   free   diet.   Cataracts   present   with   a  white   pupil   in   children,   loss   of   vision   of   various   degrees,   squint   and   rarely   painful  blind   eye   if   neglected   and   complicated   with   glaucoma   and   lens   induced   uveitis.  Mydriatic   Drugs :  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  tropicamide   0.5%   or   1%  eye   drops  B   V   1-3   drops   stat   pre-op  or   cyclopentolate   0.5%   or  1%   eye   drops  B   N   1-3   drops   stat   pre-op  and   phenylephrine   2.5%   eye  drops  B   V   1-3drops   stat   Pre-op  atropine   0.5%   or   1%   B   V   1-2   drops   2-3   times/day   1   week  Corticosteroid   drugs   used   post   cataract   surgery:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  dexamethasone  0.1%   neomycin  0.35%,   polymyxin  B   sulphate  6000units/L   and  ointment  B   V   1   drop   6   hourly   6   weeks  or   dexamethasone  0.1%,   neomycin  0.35%   eye   drops  and   ointment  B   V   1   drop   6   hourly   6   weeks  or   betamethasone  0.1%   eye   drops  B   V   1   drop   6   hourly   6   weeks  or   prednisolone  acetate   1%   eye  drops  A   E   1   drop   1-2   hourly   2-3   weeks

EDLIZ   2025  446  Antibacterial   drugs   for   eye   infections  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  gentamicin  0.3%   eye  drops  C   V   1   drop   2-6   hourly   14   days  or   ciprofloxacin  0.3%   eye  drops  B   V   1   drop   1-2   hourly   2   days   then   reduce  dose   to   4   hourly   for  12   days  or   ofloxacin  0.3%   eye  drops  A   V   1   drop   2-4hourly   2   days   then   reduce   to  4   hourly   for   10   days  Antivirals   agents   for   eyes  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  acyclovir   eye   ointment  (For   corneal   ulcers)  A   V   1   cm  ointment  5   times   a   day   14   days  acyclovir   200mg   tablet  (For   herpes   zoster  ophthalmicus)  C   V   800mg   5   times   a   day   7   days  or   famciclovir   250mg   tablet  (For   herpes   zoster  ophthalmicus)  A   V   500mg   3   times/day   7   days  Treatment   for   Cytomegalovirus   retinitis:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ganciclovir   500mg  vial  A   V   5mg/kg   infusion   12   hourly   14-21   days  and   foscanet   24mg/ml  IVI  A   V   90mg/kg   infusion   12   hourly   2-3   weeks  Topical   anaesthetics   and   diagnostic   preparations:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  oxybuprocaine   hydrochloride  0.4%   eye   drops  B   V   1   drop   stat   repeat   if  necessary  or   tetracaine   hydrochloride  0.5%   eye   drops  B   V   1   drop   stat   repeat   if  necessary

EDLIZ   2025  447  Diagnostic   preparations  Fluorescein   strips  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  fluorescein   strips   C   V   1   strip   single   use   single   use  fluorescein   solution   1%   or  2%  A   V   1   Vial   single   use   single   use  Diabetic   Retinopathy   /   Age   Related   Macular   Degeneration   (ARMD)   and  Retinal   Vein   Occlusion  The   above   are   the   commonest   conditions   for   which   anti-vascular   endothelial  growth   factor   (anti-VEGF)   medicines   are   used.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ranibizumab   S   E   0.05ml   single   use   Monthly/bimonth  ly   X   3   months  or   dexamethasone  intravitreal   injection  S   E   700mcg   single   use  NB:   Dexamethasone   intravitreal   is   indicated   for   treatment   of   diabetic   macular  oedema   in   patients   who   are   pseudophakic   or   aphakic   or   scheduled   for   cataract  surgery

EDLIZ   2025  449  O RAL   PROBLEMS  Oral   lesions   are   quite   common   especially   amongst   HIV   positive   patients.   At   any  encounter   with   a   healthcare   worker,   the   patient   should   have   their   mouth   examined  for   various   lesions   such   as   the   following:     Oral   thrush   or   candidiasis/angular   cheilitis     Herpes   simplex   labialis     Kaposi ’ s   sarcoma     Gum   infections     Salivary   gland   disorders   e.g.   parotid   gland   enlargement     Dental   caries     Cancrum   oris     Enlarged   nodes   such   as   submandibular,   submental   and   cervical  lymphadenopathy     Ranula-   bluish   sublingual   swelling   especially   in   children     Oral   hairy   leukoplakia  Some   of   these   lesions   will   need   referral   to   a   dentist   for   biopsy   if   one   is   worried  about   malignancy   e.g.   with   Kaposi   sarcoma   and   lymphoma   or   infections   such   as  histoplasmosis.   Any   ulcer   or   swelling   that   fails   to   respond   to   treatment   within   14  days   should   be   biopsied,   preferably   by   an   experienced   oral   health   professional.  M ANAGEMENT   OF   O RAL   THRUSH /O RAL   CANDIDIASIS  This   will   be   one   of   the   commonest   abnormalities   found   in   the   mouth.   Diagnosed  when   whitish   patches   or   reddening   of   the   oral   mucosa   is   noted.   The   whitish  patches   are   not   adherent   but   can   be   wiped   off   leaving   an   erythematous   surface.  The   condition   may   be   associated   with   a   feeling   of   not   tasting   food   well   plus  odynophagia   (pain   on   swallowing).   Angular   cheilitis   is   ulceration   and   occasional  bleeding   at   the   corner   of   the   mouth.   Apart   from   suspecting   HIV - related   disease,  exclude   the   current   use   of   antibiotics,   steroids   or   the   presence   of   diabetes   mellitus.  Offer   provider-initiated   HIV   testing   and   counselling   ( PI TC).  Treat   with   topical   therapy   such   as:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  nystatin   suspension   B   E   200   000   units   5   times   a   day   7-   14days  or   nystatin   lozenges   B   E   sucked   5   times   a   day   7-   14   days  or   miconazole   oral   gel  2%  C   V   topically   4   times   a   day   7   –   14days-

EDLIZ   2025  450  If   there   is   odynophagia,   treat   as   oesophageal   candidiasis   as   follows:  O ESOPHAGEAL   C ANDIDIASIS  This   is   an   AIDS   defining   illness   (WHO   Stage   4   disease)   and   hence   the   patient  would   need   to   be   worked   up   for   referral   to   the   OI   Clinic   for   Antiretrovirals   therapy.  Offer   PITC   and   consider   giving   Cotrimoxazole   prophylaxis.  Treat   oesophageal   candidiasis   with   systemic   medicines   such   as:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  fluconazole   po   B   V   200mg   twice   a   day   7   –   14days     Nutritional   rehabilitation   is   indicated.     Exclude   other   OIs   such   as   TB/KS     Refer   to   OI   Clinic.  Herpes   Simplex   labialis  Usually,   the   lesions   are   found   on   the   lips,   buccal   mucosa   and   hard   palate.   They  cause   discomfort   and   pain   during   oral   feeds   and   on   swallowing.   These   lesions  normally   undergo   spontaneous   regression   within   14   days   and   symptomatic  management   and   patient-reassurance   is   recommended.  I f   lesions   are   extensive   e.g.   covering   all   the   lip   areas ,   consider   the   following:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  acyclovir   po   C   E   400mg   3   times   a   day   5   days  Kaposi   Sarcoma  The   purple-coloured   lesions   or   nodules   should   be   easy   to   see   especially   when   they  are   on   the   palate   but   may   be   more   difficult   to   diagnose   if   they   are   underneath   the  tongue.   Check   for   similar   lesions   elsewhere.   The   patient   should   be   offered   PI TC.  Assess   for   Cotrimoxazole   prophylaxis   and   refer   to   your   nearest   OI   clinic.  Gum   infections  These   are   most   common   in   those   who   do   not   brush   their   teeth   regularly.   Oral  hygiene   should   be   emphasised.   Refer   the   patient   to   an   oral   health   professional   as  they   may   require   professional   dental   cleaning.

EDLIZ   2025  451  Necrotizing   gingivitis/periodontitis/stomatitis   There   may   be   spontaneous   bleeding  of   the   gums   as   well   as   loosening   of   the   teeth  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  metronidazole   po   C   V   200mg   3   times   a   day   5   days  plus   amoxicillin   po   C   V   500mg   3   times   a   day   5   days  Antibacterial   mouthwashes   may   help   to   alleviate   the   infection   and   pain   e.g.  betadine   mouthwash,   chlorhexidine   mouthwash   with   or   without   benzydamine  tds  Dental   Caries  The   teeth   will   have   cavities   and/or   intrinsic   discolouration .   Oral   hygiene   is   needed  and   brushing   twice   a   day   with   fluoride   toothpaste   should   be   encouraged.   Limit  sweet   foods.   Regular   dental   examination   is   required.   Refer   the   patient   to   a   dental  clinic   for   definitive   management   (dental   restoration,   extraction   or   other   treatments).  Take   note   that   dental   caries   can   complicate   to   life-threatening   fascial   space  abscesses,   Ludwig ’ s   angina   and   necrotising   fasciitis.  Fascial   Space   Abscesses/Ludwig ’ s   Angina/Cervicofacial   Necrotising   Fasciitis  Risk   factors   include   dental   infection,   jaw   fractures   and   immunosuppression.   The  mainstay   of   management   is   surgical   source   control   (dental   extraction,   incision   and  drainage,   debridement)   and   intravenous   antibiotics.   Other   symptomatic  management   will   depend   on   the   patient ’ s   clinical   status   and   include   intravenous  fluids,   analgesics   and   steroids   if   the   airway   is   compromised.   Check   for   HIV  infection   and   diabetes   mellitus   in   the   patient   and   manage   accordingly.   Pus  microscopy,   culture   and   sensitivity   (MCS)   test   and   timeous   referral   to   the  maxillofacial   and   oral   surgeons   is   recommended.  Before   MCS   results   are   availed,   start   the   patient   on:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ceftriaxone   iv   C   V   1-2g   twice   daily   2-3   weeks  or   clindamycin*   iv   B   V   600-900mg   four   times   daily   2-3   weeks  and   metronidazole   iv   B   V   500mg   three   times   daily   2-3   weeks  or   gentamycin**   iv   B   V   80mg   1-   3   times   a   day

EDLIZ   2025  452  *In   hypersensitivity   to   cephalosporins,   use   clindamycin   iv  **Please   note   Gentamycin   is   added   for   non/poorly   resolving   infections*  *Treatment   duration   is   usually   2-3   weeks   based   on:  -   clinical   response  -   surgical   source   control  -   evolution   of   laboratory   markers   of   infection   *  In   case   of   airway   compromise   (raised   tongue,   saliva   drooling   and   other   respiratory  signs   and   symptoms),   start   the   patient   on:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  dexamethas  one   iv  B   V   8-12   mg  then  4-8mg  initially  then   3   times   a   day  48   hours.   Stop   steroids   as  soon   as   the   airway   is   no  longer   compromised  For   management   of   paediatrics   refer   to   the   Paediatric   conditions   section.  Persistent   Generalized   Lymphadenopathy   (PGL)  The   commonest   cause   of   generalized   enlarged   lymph   nodes   (>   1cm)   is   underlying  HIV.   Thus,   the   patient   should   be   considered   for   PI TC.   Evaluate   the   patient   for  possible   lymphoma   and/or   loco-regional   malignancies.  Oral   Ulcers  These   are   painful   ulcers   that   may   occur   anywhere   in   the   oral   mucosa.   They   may  cause   pain   or   discomfort   during   oral   feeds.   Apart   from   herpes   simplex,   most   are  treated   symptomatically   by   using   simple   analgesics.   Large   ulcers   may   need   biopsy  to   exclude   malignancy.  The   following   applied   to   the   mouth   area   may   help:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  0.2%   chlorhexidine  mouth   rinse  C   E   2 – 4   times   a   day  or   1%   povidone   iodine   C   E   4   times   a   day  or   triamcinolone  acetonide   in   orabase  A   N   3   times   a   day

EDLIZ   2025  453  Histoplasmosis  This   may   present   as   a   nodule   on   the   palate   and   sometimes   a   penetrating   lesion   i.e.  a   hole   in   the   palate.   Biopsy   should   confirm   the   diagnosis.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ketoconazole  po  B   N   200mg   twice   daily   months
