Suppositories   can   also   be   used   in   this  situation  Nausea   and   vomiting  Medicine   Codes   Paed   dose   Frequency   Duration  metoclopramide   po   C   N   0.1-  0.2mg/kg  3   times   a   day   review  or   promethazine   po*   B   N   0.025-  0.05mg/kg  3   times   a   day   review  *not   for   use   in   children   under   the   age   of   2yrs  C ARE   OF   OTHER   COMMON   PALLIATIVE   CARE   SYMPTOMS   IN  CHILDREN   AND   ADULTS  GIT  Nausea   and   vomiting  Metoclopramide   –   see   above   under   pain   management  Prochloperazine   –   see   above   under   pain   management  Haloperidol   –   see   above   under   pain   management  CNS

EDLIZ   2025  499  Muscle   spasm  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Paed   dose   Frequency   Duration  diazepam   po   B   V   2-10mg   0.04-  0.8mg/kg  every   6-8  hrs  review  or   baclofen   po   B   N   5-10mg   5mg   3   times   a  day  review  N EUROPATHIC   PAIN :   see   under   adjuvant   analgesics  R ESPIRATORY  Shortness   of   Breath  Medicine   Adult   dose   Paed   dose   Frequency   Duration  morphine   po   2.5-5mg   0.1-0.2mg/kg   every   4   hours   review  or   diazepam   po*   5-10mg   0.04-0.05mg/kg   once   a   day   review  or   dexamethasone   po   4-8mg   0.08-0.3mg/kg   once   a   day   review  or   prednisolone   po   5-20mg   0.1-2mg/kg   once   a   day   review  *diazepam:   frequency   can   be   increased   to   twice   or   thrice   a   day,   as   per  assessment  C ARE   OF   THE   T ERMINALLY   I LL  This   is   a   part   of   palliative   care   when   the   illness   has   reached   an   irreversible   state  and   soon   will   result   in   death   or   a   state   of   permanent   unconsciousness   from   which  the   patient   is   unlikely   to   recover.   Care   is   focussed   on   keeping   the   patient   as  comfortable   as   possible   and   curative   approaches   are   discontinued.   This   usually  requires   a   good   multidisciplinary   team   (MDT)   approach.   Good   palliative   care   can  greatly   relieve   the   mental   and   physical   suffering   of   terminally   ill   patients   and   their  families.  Psychological   support  A   full   explanation   of   the   illness,   the   treatment   and   expected   physical   symptoms  should   be   discussed   (often   on   several   occasions).   It   is   important   that   health  workers   be   available   to   provide   continuing   support.   Fear   and   anxiety   about   dying,  pain   and   other   distressing   symptoms   are   common,   and   patients   may   become  depressed.

EDLIZ   2025  500  Management   includes:     honest   answers   should   be   provided   to   all   questions   asked   about   the   illness,   and  delivered   with   compassion     taking   time   to   allow   patients   and   their   family   to   share   their   problems   and  concerns     proper   control   of   pain   and   other   symptoms  Management   of   physical   symptoms  Pain   control   –   see   text   above  Nausea   and   vomiting   –   see   text   above  Loss   of   appetite   -   may   be   due   to   many   causes   including   medications.   If   possible,  identify   and   treat   the   cause.   Good   oral   care   and   adequate   hydration   should   be  ensured,   using   simple   mouthwashes.  Difficulty   in   swallowing   –   may   be   due   to   pharyngeal   or   oesophageal   obstruction,  or   thrush.   Identify   &   treat   the   cause   if   possible.   A   feeding   tube   might   help.   Good  oral   hygiene   and   hydration.  Diarrhoea   –   may   be   due   to   constipation   with   overflow,   or   excessive   use   of  laxatives.   Use   loperamide,   codeine   or   morphine.  Shortness   of   breath   –   may   be   due   to   infections   or   pleural   effusions.   This   is   one   of  the   most   feared   symptoms   and   every   effort   should   be   made   to   alleviate   it.   The  cause   should   be   established   and   treated   if   possible.   A   calming   presence   of   a  relative/carer,   propping   the   patient   up,   and   the   use   of   low-dose   morphine   (2.5-5mg  every   4   hours   –   or   a   25%   increase   in   dose   currently   being   given   for   analgesia)   and  mild   sedatives   may   help.   Oxygen   is   only   indicated   if   there   is   cyanosis.  Terminal   restlessness   -   can   be   defined   as   agitated   delirium   with   cognitive  impairment   during   the   dying   process.  During   the   last   hours   of   a   person ’ s   life,   carers   should   focus   on   minimising   pain,  reducing   respiratory   secretions   (e.g.   atropine   0.6mg   sc   4hrly,   hyoscine   5-10mg   po  every   6-8hrs)   and   observing   for   urinary   problems   (retention   or   incontinence).  Medicines   should   be   rationalised,   stopping   all   but   those   relevant   to   terminal  symptoms/signs.   Intravenous   fluids   should   be   discouraged   (or   discontinued)   as  they   aggravate   terminal   respiratory   problems.   If   symptoms   and   distress   are   not  easily   controlled,   it   may   be   appropriate   to   use   terminal   sedation.   Families   should  always   be   informed   about   the   dying   process   and   consulted   on   medicine   and   fluid  rationalization.

EDLIZ   2025  501  Palliative   care   emergencies   (address   immediately   then   refer   appropriately   or  manage   as   per   MDT):     Spinal   cord   compression:   as   above   and   refer   appropriately     Malignant   bowel   obstruction:   refer   appropriately     Severe   bleeding     Superior   vena   cava   syndrome  N.B   Use   of   syringe   drivers   in   palliative   care   can   be   indicated   when   the   patient  cannot   tolerate   oral   medications   for   various   reasons   e.g.   continuous   vomiting.  Syringe   drivers   should   be   initiated   by   trained   staff.

EDLIZ   2025  502  29.   M EDICINES   AND   T HE   E LDERLY  GENERAL   NOTES   50 3  ANTI-HYPERTENSIVES   51 8  DIURETICS   51 8  DIGOXIN   51 9  ORAL   HYPOGLYCAEMICS   51 9  HYPNOTIC   SEDATIVES   51 9  MAJOR   TRANQUILLISERS   51 9  ANTI-   DIARRHOEALS   51 9  NON-STEROIDAL   ANTI-INFLAMMATORY   MEDICINES   5 20  NARCOTICS   5 20  STEROIDS   5 20

EDLIZ   2025  503  G ENERAL   N OTES  Due   to   physiological   changes   and   altered   pharmacodynamic   response   of   target  organs   the   elderly   are   more   susceptible   to   adverse   medicine   reactions.  Most   patients   over   70   years   old   have   an   average   of   5   medical   complaints   and  polypharmacy   is   common.   Therefore,   medication   should   be   reviewed   frequently  (every   3   months).  Principles   of   management   in   elderly     Provide   simple,   once   or   twice   daily   regimens   wherever   possible.     Give   clear   instructions   on   how   medicines   are   to   be   taken.     Where   possible   ask   relatives   to   supervise   medicine   administration.     Suppositories   or   liquid   formulations   should   be   prescribed   where   swallowing   is  difficult.     Use   reduced   doses     Avoid   nephrotoxic   medicines  Principles   of   prescribing   for   older   people  The   care   of   older   people   is   complex   for   various   reasons:     Physiological   complexity   secondary   to   frailty,   multiple   long-term   conditions,  and   polypharmacy     Diagnostic   uncertainty   secondary   to   non-specific   presentations   (see   geriatric  ‘ giants ’ )   and   atypical   signs/symptoms     Higher   prevalence   of   co-existing   psychological,   cognitive,   functional,   and   social  issues     Complex   ethical   considerations,   particularly   with   regards   to   autonomy   and  cognitive   capacity     With   advancing   age   and   chronic   conditions   comes   limited   prognosis,   requiring  individualised   ‘ Advance   Care   Planning ’   which   considers   each   patient ’ s   priorities  and   preferences   to   guide   their   care   and   treatment.  Such   complexity   requires   multi-domain   assessment   and   management   planning   –  this   is   often   termed   ‘ Comprehensive   Geriatric   Assessment ’   (CGA),   when  performed   in   full   by   a   multidisciplinary   team.

EDLIZ   2025  504  Frailty  Frailty   is   a   state   of   increased   vulnerability   and   functional   impairment   caused   by  cumulative   declines   across   multiple   body   systems.   Frailty   is   a   better   indicator   of  health   status   than   chronological   age.   Frailty   may   include   loss   of   muscle   mass   and  strength,   reduced   energy   and   exercise   tolerance,   cognitive   impairment,   and  decreased   physiological   reserve,   leading   to   poor   health   outcomes   and   a   reduced  ability   to   recover   from   acute   stress.  Warning   signs   such   as   falls,   delirium,   immobility,   weight   loss,   and   changes   in  chronic   conditions,   mood,   cognition,   and   function   should   prompt   frailty  assessment.   The   gold   standard   for   assessing   and   managing   frailty   in   older   adults   is  CGA   that   includes   a   medical   review,   medication   review,   and   discussion   with   the  older   person   and   their   carers   to   define   the   impact   of   illness   and   create   an  individualised   care   and   support   plan.   There   are   many   objective   assessment   tools,  the   most   common   being   the   Rockwood   Clinical   Frailty   Scale   (freely   available)   -   a  model   of   accumulated   deficits,   where   more   deficits   correlate   with   negative   health  outcomes,   i.e.,   frailty.  Multifactorial   management   of   frailty   includes   medication   adjustments,   exercise  programs   with   muscle   strengthening   and   resistance   training,   optimisation   of  nutrition,   cognitive   training   (e.g.   cognitive   stimulation   training),   and   social   support.  Pharmacokinetics   &   pharmacodynamics   in   older   people  Administration/Absorption:   Tooth   decay,   decreased   saliva   production,   and  decreased   oesophageal   motility   all   affect   an   individual ’ s   ability   to   swallow  medications.   The   gastrointestinal   mucosa   remains   relatively   intact;   therefore,   the  absorption   of   most   medications   is   unchanged   in   ageing   physiology.  Distribution:   With   ageing,   total   body   fat   increases,   increasing   the   distribution   of  fat-soluble   medications.   Conversely,   total   body   water   decreases,   reducing   the  distribution   of   water-soluble   drugs.   Serum   albumin   levels   decline   with   age,  increasing   unbound   levels   of   albumin-bound   molecules.  Clearance:   Age-related   decreases   in   liver   volume   (mass   and   blood   flow)   and  hepatic   enzyme   activity   mean   that   hepatic   metabolism   of   many   medications  decreases.   Similarly,   age-related   decreases   in   glomerular   filtration   rate   (decreased  renal   mass   and   blood   flow)   reduce   renal   excretion   of   medications.   To   prevent  toxic   accumulation   of   medications   in   older   people,   doses   should   be   reduced,   or  dosing   intervals   increased.

EDLIZ   2025  505  Two   good   examples   of   this   are:  Diuretics:   decreased   plasma   water   volume   and   lower   levels   of   aldosterone   alter  physiological   action   of   and   response   to   diuretics.   Dosages   considered   ‘ low ’   in  young   adults   can   be   too   aggressive   and   cause   problematic   hypotension   and  electrolyte   derangement   in   older   people.  Digoxin:   a   renally-excreted   drug   with   a   narrow   therapeutic   index,   often   requires  dose   reduction   in   older   people   (62.5-125   micrograms   daily).  Polypharmacy  Multiple   long-term   conditions,   also   known   as   multimorbidity   (the   presence   of   two  or   more   chronic   diseases   in   a   single   individual),   often   comes   with   polypharmacy  (the   prescription   of   more   than   four   medications).  Polypharmacy   can   result   in:     Increased   risk   of   adverse   effects     Increased   risk   of   drug-drug   interactions   (e.g.   proton-pump   inhibitors,   such   as  omeprazole,   decrease   activation   of   the   pro-drug,   clopidogrel,   thus   reducing   its  efficacy   when   co-prescribed)     Prescribing   cascades   (e.g.   diuretic   prescribed   to   treat   peripheral   oedema,   itself  the   side   effect   of   a   calcium-channel   blocker)     Increased   risk   of   medications   not   being   reviewed   thoroughly.  Anti-cholinergic   burden  Many   common   drugs   have   anti-cholinergic   properties   that   adversely   affect  cognitive   function   in   older   people   –   this   is   termed   ‘ anti-cholinergic   burden ’   (ACB).  Medications   with   ‘ high ’   ACB   increase   the   risk   of   falls,   dementia,   and   death   in  older   people.   There   are   many   freely   available   resources   that   can   help   assess   ACB  of   medications,   including   www.acbcalc.com.   Common   classes   of   drugs   with   high  ACB   include   anti-muscarinics   used   to   treat   urinary   incontinence   (e.g.   oxybutinin),  tricyclic   anti-depressants   used   to   treat   neuropathic   pain/insomnia   (e.g.  amitriptyline),   and   anti-histamines   used   to   treat   allergic   symptoms   (e.g.  chlorphenamine).

EDLIZ   2025  506  Falls-Risk   Increasing   Drugs   (FRIDs)  All   older   patients   should   have   their   drug   burden   reviewed   with   respect   to  likelihood   to   cause   falls.   Falls   can   be   caused   by   almost   any   drug   that   acts   on   the  brain   or   on   the   circulation.   Usually,   the   mechanism   leading   to   a   fall   is   one   or   more  of:  -   sedation,   with   slowing   of   reaction   times   and   impaired   balance,  -   hypotension ,   including   the   three   syndromes   of   paroxysmal   hypotension:  orthostatic   hypotension,   vasovagal   syndrome,   vasodepressor   carotid   sinus  hypersensitivity.  -   bradycardia,   tachycardia   or   periods   of   asystole .  Falls   may   be   the   consequence   of   recent   medication   changes   but   are   usually   caused  by   medicines   that   have   been   given   for   some   time   (and   not   reviewed).  High   Risk   Medicines   -   can   commonly   cause   falls   on   their   own   or   in  combination.  Medicine   Class   Examples  Benzodiazepines   Diazepam,   lorazepam  Z-drugs   Zopiclone,   zolpidem  Tricyclic   antidepressants   Amitriptyline,   imipramine  Other   antidepressants   Mirtazapine,   venlafaxine,   duloxetine  Monamine   oxidase   α   inhibitors   Phenelzine,   isocarboxazid  Drugs   for   psychosis   and   agitation   Chlorpromazine,   haloperidol  fluphenazine,   risperidone,   quetiapine  Opiate   analgesics   All   opiates   and   related   analgesia   –   e.g.  codeine,   tramadol  Antiepileptics   Parkinsons   Disease  Dopamine   agonists  Phenytoin,   carbamazepine  phenobarbitone  Ropinirole,   pramipexole,  bromocriptine,   cabergoline,   pergolide  Monoamine   oxidase   -   β   inhibitors   Selegiline  α   receptor   blockers   Doxazosin,   tamsulosin,   prazosin  Centrally   acting   α   2   receptor   agonists   Clonidine,   moxonidine

EDLIZ   2025  507  Medicine   Class   Examples  Thiazide   diuretics   Hydrochlorothiazide,   chlorthalidone  Angiotensin   Converting   Enzyme  Inhibitors  Lisinopril,   Ramipril,   enalapril,  captopril,   perindopril  Beta   Blockers   Atenolol,   sotalol   (renally   excreted  can   accumulate   in   renal  impairment),   bisoprolol,   metoprolol,  propranolol,   carvedilol,   timolol   eye  drops  Antianginals   GTN,   Isosorbide   mononitrate  Long   acting   oral   hypoglycaemics   Glimepiride,   glibenclamide  Moderate   Risk   Medicines   -   can   cause   falls,   especially   when   used   in  combination.  Medicine   Class   Examples  SSRI   antidepressants   Sertraline,citalopram,   paroxetine,  fluoxetine  Antiepileptics   Sodium   valproate,   gabapentin,  pregabalin  Muscle   relaxants   Baclofen  Loop   diuretics   Furosemide  Angiotensin   II   receptor   Blockers   Losartan,   candesartan,   valsartan,  irbesartan,   telmisartan

EDLIZ   2025  508  Calcium   channel   blockers   that   only  reduce   blood   pressure  (Dihydropyridines)   Amlodipine,  nifedipine,   lercanidipine,   nimodipine  Calcium   channel   blockers   which   slow  the   heart   and   reduce   blood   pressure  (Phenylalkylamines)   Verapamil  (Benzothiazepines)   Diltiazem  Other   antidysrhythmics   Digoxin,   amiodarone,   flecainide  Oral   hypoglycaemics   (Sulfonylureas)   Gliclazide,   glipizide  Possible   Risk   of   Falls   Medicines   -   possibly   cause   falls,   especially   when   used   in  combination,   or   in   certain   pre-disposing   medical   conditions.  Medicine   Class   Examples  Antiepileptics   Lamotrigine,   levetiracetam,  topiramate,   zonisamide  Vestibular   sredatives   Prochlorperazine,   cinnarizine  Sedating   antihistamines   for   allergies   Chlorpheniramine,   promethazine  Anticholinergics   acting   on   bladder   Oxybutynin,   duloxetine,   solifenacin  Dementia   drugs   (Acetylcholinesterase  inhibitors)  Donepezil,   rivastigmine  Proton   pump   inhibitors   Omeprazole,   lansoprazole

EDLIZ   2025  509  Anti-coagulation   for   atrial   fibrillation   in   older   people  Atrial   fibrillation   (AF)   confers   risk   of   clot   formation   in   the   atria,   which   can   travel  to   the   cerebral   circulation   causing   embolic   strokes.   To   minimise   this   risk,   anti-  coagulation   is   often   recommended,   generally   with   a   direct   oral   anti-coagulant  (DOAC,   e.g.   Apixaban,   Edoxaban,   Rivaroxaban),   and   where   these   are   not  available,   with   warfarin.   However,   anti-coagulation   creates   bleeding   risk.  Therefore,   the   stroke-risk   (quantified   using   the   CHA2DS2-VASc   score)   is  balanced   against   the   bleeding   risk.   Falls   risk   is   rarely   a   reason   not   to   anti-coagulate  as   the   substantial   benefits   largely   outweigh   the   small   risks.  CHA2DS2-VASc   score   for   AF   stroke   risk:     Age:   <65   (0   points)/65-74   (1   point)/   >75   (2   points)     Sex:   Female   (1   point)/   Male   (0   points)     History   of   heart   failure:   No   (0   points)/   Yes   (1   point)     History   of   hypertension:   No   (0   points)/   Yes   (1   point)     History   of   stroke/TIA/VTE:   No   (0   points)/   Yes   (2   points)     History   of   vascular   disease   (IHD,   PVD):   No   (0   points)/   Yes   (1   point)     History   of   diabetes:   No   (0   points)/   Yes   (1   point)  CHA2DS2-VASc   score   0=low   risk,   no   need   for   anti-coagulation  CHA2DS2-VASc   score   1=moderate   risk,   consider   anti-coagulation  CHA2DS2-VASc   score   >2=high   risk,   anti-coagulation   recommended  Analgesia  Older   people   can   be   prescribed   analgesia   according   to   the   WHO   analgesic   ladder,  with   a   few   key   considerations:     Reduce   the   dose   of   paracetamol   from   maximum   1000mg   QDS   to   500mg   QDS   if  weight   <50kg     Non-steroidal   anti-inflammatories   (NSAIDS,   e.g.   naproxen,   ibuprofen,  diclofenac,   indomethacin)   are   best   avoided,   and   if   used   should   be   used   with  extreme   caution,   due   to   substantially   increased   risk   of   GI   complications   and  nephrotoxicity.   Where   absolutely   necessary,   consider   co-prescription   of   a  gastro-protective   PPI   (e.g.   omeprazole,   lansoprazole),   and   monitor   renal  function.

EDLIZ   2025  510     Preferred   opiate   analgesics   are   morphine   (in   adequate   renal   function)   and  oxycodone   (in   reduced   renal   function).   Codeine   and   tramadol   should   be   avoided  due   to   higher   risk   of   delirium.   Opiates   should   always   be   co-prescribed   with  laxatives.  Medicines   Optimisation  1.   Obtain   an   accurate   and   complete   medication   history.  2.   Map   the   medication   list   to   the   past   medical   history   to   verify   an   appropriate  indication   for   each   drug,   and   identify   conditions   not   treated   optimally.  3.   Where   there   is   no   clear   indication   for   a   medication,   or   the   risks   have   come   to  outweigh   the   benefits,   discuss   with   the   patient   a   trial   without   that   medication  (known   as   ‘ de-prescribing ’ ).  4.   Simplify   the   administration   of   multiple   medicines   where   possible   (e.g.   choose  formulations   with   minimal   dosing   frequency,   when   prescribing   a   new  medication   consider   how   to   align   timing   with   existing   medications)   and   give  clear   written   instructions   on   why/how/when   each   medication   should   be   taken.  5.   In   acute   illness:     Ensure   essential   medicines   are   not   omitted   (e.g.   Parkinson ’ s   drugs,   anti-  epileptics),   including   review   of   appropriate   administration   route   where  applicable   (e.g.   if   the   patient   is   ‘ nil   by   mouth ’   then   switch   oral   tablets   to  parenteral   /   transdermal   forms).     Temporarily   hold   medications   that   are   likely   to   cause   further   harm   (e.g.   hold  diuretics   in   dehydration,   hold   nephrotoxic   in   acute   kidney   injury)Tools   that   can  help   in   the   process   of   medicines   optimization   and   aid   in   ‘ de-prescribing ’  decisions   include:   STOPP/START,   STOPPFall,   STOPPFrail ,   and  www.deprescribing.org .  Advance   Care   Planning  When   someone   is   reaching   the   last   year(s)   of   life,   it   is   important   to   revisit   the  goals   of   healthcare.   ‘ Advance   Care   Planning ’   (ACP)   describes   conversations  between   an   individual,   their   families,   caregivers,   and   healthcare   professionals  about   their   future   wishes   and   priorities   for   care.  The   goal   of   ACP   is   to   clarify   and   document   an   individual ’ s   wishes,   needs,   and  preferences,   thus   increasing   the   likelihood   of   these   being   known   and   respected   at  the   end   of   their   life,   such   that   their   final   days   are   spent   in   the   place   and   manner   of  their   own   choosing.

EDLIZ   2025  511  From   a   medical   perspective,   ACP   discussions   may   include   various   aspects   of  healthcare:     Documenting   a   decision   to   not   attempt   resuscitation   in   the   event   of  cardiopulmonary   arrest .     De-prescribing   medications   designed   for   longer-term   prognostic   benefit   that   are  no   longer   necessary   (e.g.   statins   to   reduce   cardiovascular   risk   in   someone   with  <2   years   to   live) .     Transitioning   from   management   with   curative   intent   to   management   of  symptoms,   with   many   aspects   of   monitoring   no   longer   required   (e.g.   oxygen  given   for   breathlessness,   oxygen   saturation   readings   are   no   longer   required) .     Documenting   a   decision   regarding   preferred   place   of   care,   and   the   circumstances  in   which   an   individual   would   and   would   not   wish   to   be   brought   to   hospital   for  treatment   (e.g.   preferred   place   of   care   is   at   home,   therefore   they   would   accept  oral   antibiotics   for   infection   but   not   conveyance   to   hospital   for   intravenous  antibiotics,   and   this   decision   is   made   understanding   the   risks)  Importantly,   just   as   many   geriatric   syndromes   present   non-specifically,   so   might  the   decline   towards   end   of   life   in   advanced   age.   Hence,   it   is   important   to   be  transparent   with   patients   about   prognosis/likely   outcomes   of   treatment,   and   begin  ACP   discussions   early.   Markers   of   general   decline   include:     Decreasing   levels   of   activity/increasing   amounts   of   time   spent   asleep     Increasing   personal   care   needs   (e.g.   unable   to   dress   without   help)     Multiple,   progressive   co-morbidities   with   declining   response   to   treatments     Progressive,   unintentional   weight   loss     Low   serum   albumin     Repeated,   unplanned   admissions   to   hospital   with   acute   illness   with   incomplete  recovery   after   each   event     Sentinel   events   (e.g.   serious   fall   with   a   fragility   fracture,   bereavement)  Key   conditions  The   geriatric   ‘ giants ’ ,   also   known   as   ‘ frailty   syndromes ’ ,   are   falls,   immobility,  delirium,   incontinence,   and   susceptibility   to   side   effects   of   medications.     Falls   and   immobility   are   often   multi-factorial,   requiring   considerations   across  multiple   body   systems   –   there   are   no   specific   medications   to   ‘ treat ’   or   ‘ prevent ’  falls,   hence   a   falls   assessment   must   therefore   be   comprehensive   and   holistic.   See  above   for   section   on   ‘ Falls   risk-increasing   drugs ’   and   below   for   section   on  ‘ Osteoporosis   and   fragility   fractures ’   (given   that   fragility   fractures   usually  follow   a   fall).

EDLIZ   2025  512     See   ‘ susceptibility   to   side   effects   of   medications ’   in   ‘ Polypharmacy ’   above.  Osteoporosis   and   Fragility   Fractures  Osteoporosis   is   a   progressive,   systemic   skeletal   disease   characterized   by   low   bone  mass   and   microarchitectural   deterioration   of   bone   tissue,   with   a   consequent  increase   in   bone   fragility   and   fracture   risk.   A   fragility   fracture   is   a   fracture  resulting   from   a   fall   from   standing   height   or   less   trauma.  Vertebral   fractures   are   the   most   common   fragility   fracture;   they   can   be   an  incidental   finding   often   overlooked   on   radiological   imaging   performed   for   other  indications.  Fracture   risk   assessment:  1.   Assess   fracture   risk   in   any   postmenopausal   woman,   or   man   age   ≥ 50   years,   with  a   clinical   risk   factor   (CRF)   for   fragility   fracture.   Examples   of   CRFs   include:  glucocorticoid   treatment,   malnutrition,   malabsorption,   diabetes,   HIV,  neurodegenerative   diseases   (e.g.   stroke,   Parkinson ’ s   disease),   systemic  inflammatory   disease   (e.g.   lupus,   rheumatoid   arthritis),   chronic   pulmonary  disease,   hyperthyroidism,   CKD   3a-5,   hypogonadism/premature   menopause,  alcohol   excess,   smoking,   and   certain   medications   (e.g.   phenytoin,  carbamazepine,   TDF).  2.   Perform   lateral   (AP   not   needed)   lumbar   and   thoracic   spine  X-rays   in   postmenopausal   woman,   or   man   age   ≥ 50   years,   with   acute   onset   back  pain,   height   loss,   or   kyphosis   (especially   if   glucocorticoid   therapy).  3.   Always   examine   vertebrae   on   routine   radiographs,   e.g.   CXRs,   looking   for  incidental   vertebral   fractures.  4.   Investigate   fragility   fractures   for   underlying   causes   of   fracture/osteoporosis  (e.g.   malignancy,   hyperthyroidism,   nutritional   status).  5.   Assess   falls   risk   in   patients   with   fragility   fractures   and   offer   those   at   fall   risk  exercise   to   improve   balance   and   muscle   strength.  6.   The   online   FRAX   tool   (fraxplus.org/calculation-tool)   can   be   used   to   assist  fracture   risk   assessment.  7.   Of   note,   most   people   who   sustain   a   fragility   fracture   have   a   bone   mineral   density  (BMD)   T-Score   >-2.5,   because   low   BMD   contributes   to,   but   is   not   the   only  cause   of   fragility   fracture.   If   DXA   scanning   is   available,   BMD   should   be  measured   where   identifying   a   T-Score   <-2.5   would   cause   you   to   give   an   anti-

EDLIZ   2025  513  resorptive   medication   that   you   would   otherwise   not   give,   or   if   a   baseline  measurement   is   needed   against   which   to   compare   future   BMD   measurements.  Lifestyle   advice:  1.   Recommend   a   healthy,   balanced   diet   (including   protein),   minimize   alcohol   and  avoid   smoking.  2.   Ensure   sufficient   dietary   calcium   (e.g.   kapenta   fish,   dairy,   green   cabbage,   green  beans).  3.   Ensure   skin   is   regularly   exposed   to   the   sun   (for   vitamin   D).  4.   Encourage   a   combination   of   regular   weight-bearing   and   muscle   strengthening  exercise.  Anti-resorptive   treatment:  Any   postmenopausal   woman,   or   a   man   age   ≥   50   years   who:  1.   Has   had   a   fragility   fracture   (especially   a   vertebral,   humerus   or   hip   fracture);   start  anti-resorptive   treatment   promptly   (because   re-fracture   risk   is   highest  immediately   after   a   fracture).  2.   Is   starting   ≥ 5   mg/day   Prednisolone   or   equivalent   over   the   next   3   months   (i.e.  ≥ 450mg   in   total   over   a   3-month   period),   start   concurrent   anti-resorptive  treatment.  3.   Offer   antiresorptive   therapy   with   oral   Alendronate   or   intravenous  Zoledronate   as   first-line   treatments   to   reduce   fragility   fracture   risk.  4.   Consider   Hormone   replacement   therapy   (HRT)   for   the   treatment   of  postmenopausal   fragility   fracture   risk   in   younger   post-menopausal   women   (aged  ≤   60   years)   who   have   low   risk   for   adverse   malignant   and   thromboembolic  events.  5.   Regularly   review   patients'   tolerance   of,   and   adherence   to,   oral   drug   treatments.  6.   Long-term   treatment   is   often   required,   because   osteoporosis   is   a   long-term  condition   for   which   there   is   currently   no   cure.   Plan   to   prescribe   oral  alendronate   for   at   least   5   years,   or   intravenous   zoledronate   for   at   least   3  doses   and   then   re-assess   fracture   risk.

EDLIZ   2025  514  Longer   durations   of   treatment   will   be   needed   in   those   who   are   older   (age   ≥ 70  years),   have   had   a   hip   or   vertebral   fracture,   are   on   high-dose   oral   glucocorticoids  [ ≥ 7.5   mg/day   of   prednisolone   or   equivalent   over   3   months],   or   have   a   further  fragility   fracture   durin g   treatment.  HRT   Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  or   p remarin   (Conjugated  oestrogens   )   po  S   N   0. 625mg   Once   a   day  or   n orethisterone   po   B   N   2.5mg   once   a   day   Variable  plus   a lendronate   po   S   N   70mg   once   a   week   3-   5   years  or   z olendronic   acid   IV   S   N   5mg   Every   12   -18months   3   -   10   years  A.   Alendronate ,   First   thing   in   the   morning,   on   an   empty   stomach,   tablets   should  be   swallowed   whole   with   a   glass   of   plain   water   (~200ml)   while   the   patient   is  sitting   or   standing   in   an   upright   position.   Patients   should   remain   upright   and   not  consume   anything   other   than   water   for   at   least   30   mins   after   taking   the   tablet  (ideally   60   minutes).   This   maximizes   absorption   and   minimises   oesophageal  irritation.  B .   Zolendronic   acid   IV ,   If   5mg   is   unavailable,   use   4mg   preparation.  Delirium  Delirium   is   a   syndrome,   not   a   disease.   It   is   defined   in   the   Diagnostic   and   Statistical  Manual   of   Mental   Disorders   (DSM   IV)   as   having   four   key   features:  1.   Disturbance   of   consciousness,   characterised   by   inattention .  2.   Change   in   cognition   or   perception   not   accounted   for   a   pre-existing   or   evolving  dementia .  3.   Development   acutely   over   a   short   period   (usually   hours   to   days),   and   fluctuating  over   the   course   of   each   day .  4.   A   history   supportive   of   an   organic   underlying   cause,   such   as   a   medical   illness,  infection,   metabolic   derangement,   medication   initiation   or   withdrawal,   pain,   or  constipation.   Environmental   factors   can   also   precipitate   a   delirium,   especially  when   the   individual   is   already   at   risk   (e.g.   they   have   dementia,   frailty,   and/or  sensory   impairment).

EDLIZ   2025  515  With   delirium,   patients   can   be   hypo   or   hyperactive,   and   this   can   fluctuate.  Disorientation,   reduced   concentration,   dysphasia,   dysarthria,   visual   hallucinations  are   all   possible   features.   There   are   multiple   simple   tools   to   assess   for   delirium,  including   the   Confusion   Assessment   Method   (CAM),   and   the   4   As   Test   (4AT).  The   mainstay   of   treatment   for   delirium   is   to   treat   the   underlying   cause(s)   and   calm  re-orientation.   Nutrition   and   hydration   are   important.   Non-pharmacological  management   can   include   involving   familiar   relatives   in   the   patient ’ s   care,  provision   of   orientation   aids   (e.g.   clock,   hearing   aids,   spectacles).   It   is   important   to  note   that   delirium   is   a   distressing   state   for   patients   and   their   relatives   and   should  therefore   be   carefully   explained,   including   the   risk   of   recurrence.  Only   once   all   non-pharmacological   management   has   failed,   or   the   patient   is   at  immediate   risk   of   harm   to   themselves   or   others,   should   sedative   medications   be  considered.   Sedatives   should   only   be   prescribed   ‘ as   required ’   and   not   regularly,  always   beginning   with   the   lowest   effective   dose   possible   to   avoid   harm.  Haloperidol   is   generally   considered   first-line,   except   in   people   with   movement  disorders   in   which   haloperidol   is   contra-indicated.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  h aloperidol   po/IM   B   V   5mg   PRN   once   a   da y   Review   every   24  hours,   discontinued  as   soon   as   possible l orazepam   po   B   E   0.5-1mg  PRN   once   a   day.  maximum   dose   is  2mg/24hrs  Dementia  Dementia   is   a   syndrome   comprising   impairment   of   memory   and   other   higher  cortical   functions   leading   to   progressive,   irreversible   decline   in   function.   The  underlying   diseases   and   clinical   features   vary   according   to   dementia   subtype.  Dementia   is   a   clinical   diagnosis,   in   which   a   collateral   history   from   a   relative  and/or   caregiver   is   key,   made   by   ruling   out   reversible   causes,   and   can   be   assisted  with   assessment   tools   (e.g.   MoCA,   RUDAS).   No   biochemical   or   imaging   test   can  diagnose   dementia.  Pharmacological   treatment   can   be   trialed   in   persons   with   Alzheimer ’ s   Dementia  (AD),   which   may   slow   the   progression   of   disease,   but   is   not   curative   (e.g.  Donepezil).   Such   medications   require   specialist   prescription   and   monitoring  (generally   in   liaison   with   psychiatry,   neurology,   or   private   practice).   If   after   an  initial   trial   period   of   2-3   months   there   is   no   effect   at   the   maximum   tolerated   dose,  it   should   be   discontinued.   If   benefit   appears   to   have   occurred   (clinical   assessment

EDLIZ   2025  516  +/-   cognitive   testing),   the   medication   can   be   continued   indefinitely   at   the  established   dose;   however,   periodic   withdrawal   (every   18   months)   should   be  considered   to   establish   whether   benefit   continues.   The   evidence   of   benefit   in  advanced   AD   is   weak,   therefore   donepezil   is   generally   stopped   when   the   patient  reaches   this   stage  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  d onepezil   po   A   E   s tart   5mg   po.  Increase   to   10mg  after  once   day   Increase   dose   after   4  weeks.  Trial   at   full   dose   for   2-3  months.   Stop   if   no  benefit   after   trial   period.  Incontinence  There   are   four   types   of   urinary   incontinence:   stress   incontinence   (due   to   weakness  of   structures   around   the   urinary   outlet,   often   following   childbirth),   urge  incontinence   (due   to   high   bladder   pressure,   often   caused   by   overactive   bladder  syndrome),   overflow   incontinence   (due   to   bladder   outlet   obstruction,   which   causes  an   overfull   bladder   to   overflow   in   an   uncontrolled   manner),   and   mixed  incontinence   (mixed   pathology   –   usually   a   combination   of   stress   and   urge  incontinence).  Non-pharmaceutical   management   should   include:     Consideration   of   hyperglycemia   and   hypercalcemia   as   causes   of   polyuria,     Medication   review,   especially   of   diuretic   use,     Provision   of   advice   on   fluid   intake   with   avoidance   of   salty,   caffeinated,   and  fizzy   drinks,     In   both   men   and   women,   pelvic   floor   retraining   exercises,   bladder   training,  double   voiding,   and   approaches   to   reduce   sleep   disturbance,     In   men,   techniques   to   empty   the   urethral   bulb   to   limit   terminal   dribbling.  If   available   through   private   prescription,   mirabegron   is   the   medication   with   the  lowest   anti-cholinergic   burden   –   trial   for   2-3   months   and   stop   if   improvement   of  symptoms.   Avoid   oxybutynin   and   other   similar   anti-muscarinics   (listed   in   FRIDs  table   above).

EDLIZ   2025  517  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  m irabegron  (modified   release)  po  S   E   50mg   once   daily   Trial   for   2-3   months.  Stop   if   no   benefit   after   trial  period.  Constipation  Constipation   describes   harder   than   normal   stools,   infrequent   or   increased   time  between   bowel   movements,   and/or   difficulty/pain   on   defecation.  Constipation   risk   increases   with   age,   as   bowel   motility   slows.   Immobility   is   an  important   risk   factor   for   constipation,   as   are   other   chronic   conditions   (e.g.  hypothyroidism,   parkinsonism).   Many   commonly   prescribed   medications   can  contribute   to   constipation   in   older   people   (e.g.   opiate   analgesia,   iron   supplements,  calcium-channel   blockers).   Paradoxically,   constipation   can   become   so   severe   that  it   causes   faecal   impaction   with   overflow   diarrhoea,   which   is   the   most   common  cause   of   faecal   incontinence   in   older   people   –   hence,   anti-diarrhoeal   medications  should   be   avoided   in   older   people.  Due   to   the   multi-factorial   nature   of   constipation,   management   must   be   equally  multi-faceted,   including   hydration,   mobilisation,   high-fibre   diet,   and   medication  review.   Where   pharmacological   intervention   is   required,   this   can   be   achieved   with  oral   stimulant   laxatives   (e.g.   senna,   bisacodyl),   or   treatment   per   rectum   (e.g.  glycerin   suppositories,   enemas).  Anti-hypertensives  Prescribe   with   caution   due   to   increased   risk   of   postural   hypotension,   side   effects,  cognitive   dysfunction   and   falls.   The   general   treatment   guidelines   on   hypertension  should   be   followed   but   it   is   appropriate   to   start   with   lower   doses   and   build   up.   Re-  evaluate   therapy   every   6-12   months   because   blood   pressure   may   decrease   as   a  result   of   progression   of   atherosclerotic   disease.  Diuretics  Since   the   elderly   have   a   decreased   plasma   volume   and   lower   levels   of   aldosterone,  aggressive   diuretic   therapy   to   reduce   BP   is   not   indicated.   Even   low   doses   may  precipitate   hypotension,   falls,   hyponatraemia   and   hypokalaemia.   Gravitational  oedema   will   respond   to   simple   mechanical   measures   such   as   raising   legs   and   does  not   usually   warrant   use   of   diuretics.

EDLIZ   2025  518  Digoxin  Lower   maintenance   doses   e.g.   0.0625   to   0.125   mg   (paediatric   elixir   formulation)  should   be   used   owing   to   reduced   renal   function   and   increased   sensitivity.   Signs   of  digoxin   toxicity   are   nausea,   vomiting,   anorexia,   visual   disturbances   and   headache.  Where   there   is   no   evidence   of   heart   failure   and   if   the   heart   is   in   normal   sinus  rhythm   digoxin   may   be   safely   withdrawn   but   the   patient   should   be   monitored   for  atrial   fibrillation   if   discontinuation   is   attempted.  Oral   hypoglycaemics   ( see   D iabetes   section   in   Metabolic   and   Endocrine  Conditions   Chapter)  The   elderly   are   at   an   increased   risk   of   hypoglycaemia   with   G libenclamide   and  should   be   prescribed   G liclazide.  Hypnotic   /   Sedatives  Benzodiazepines   (e.g.   D iazepam)   significantly   impair   cognitive   function   and  should   not   be   used.   Sedation   should   be   achieved   with:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  l orazepam   po   B   E   0.5-1mg   at   night   intermittently  Major   tranquillisers  It   is   essential   to   define   and   remove   the   underlying   cause   of   agitation   e.g.   infection  or   hypoxia.   Once   this   is   done   and   if   tranquillisation   is   still   considered   necessary,  the   options   are:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  h aloperidol   po   B   V   0.5-2mg   twice   daily   review  or   h aloperidol   im   B   V   1-5mg   twice   daily   review  Always   start   with   the   lower   dose   if   possible.   Usually   haloperidol   0.5   mg   twice  daily   which   is   often   enough.   Avoid   chlorpromazine   and   fluphenazine   decanoate  where   possible   as   major   irreversible   side   effects   may   occur.

EDLIZ   2025  519  Anti - diarrhoeals  The   elderly   are   prone   to   spurious,   or   overflow   diarrhoea   from   chronic   faecal  impaction.   History   of   ‘ warning ’   symptoms   or   drug   side   effects   is   important.   No  diarrhoea   in   the   elderly   should   be   treated   with   anti-diarrhoeal   medicines   before   an  adequate   physical   examination   i.e.   per   rectal   examination,   has   excluded   impaction.  In   such   cases   a   high   fibre   diet,   regular   enemas   and   a   stimulant   such   as   senna   or  bisacodyl   will   relieve   the   problem.  Non-steroidal   Anti-inflammatory   medicines  These   should   be   used   with   caution   as   the   elderly   are   particularly   susceptible   to  gastrointestinal   complications   (erosions   and   bleeding)   and   renal   complications   (e.g.  interstitial   nephritis).   Paracetamol   is   a   more   appropriate   analgesic   in   older   adults.  For   management   of   chronic   pain,   regular   use   of   safer   medications   such   as  paracetamol,   with   adjuvant   ibuprofen   is   safer   than   intermittent   dosing   of   more  potent   drugs   which   have   more   side   effects.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  i buprofen   po   C   N   200mg   <   3   times   a   day   as   required  Narcotics  Elderly   patients   are   prone   to   neurologic   side   effects   of   narcotics   (codeine  phosphate,   tramadol,   morphine   and   others)   which   include   sedation,  confusion/delirium.   Prescribe   with   caution   and   follow   guidelines   in   this   book,  adjusting   dose   for   frail/elderly   patients.  Steroids  The   known   side   effects   of   steroids   occur   more   rapidly   and   are   accentuated   in   the  elderly.   Use   with   caution   and   monitor   for   side   effects.

EDLIZ   2025  520  30. EDLIZ   2025  521  GENERAL   NOTES  A NAEMIA  This   is   defined   as   a   decrease   in   the   concentration   of   haemoglobin   (<13.5   g/dl   in  men   and   <11.5   g   /dl   in   women)   and   haematocrit   (<0.42   in   men   and   <0.36   in  women).   Use   of   red   blood   cell   indices   and   careful   examination   of   a   peripheral  blood   smear   may   indicate   the   likely   cause   of   anaemia.   If   in   doubt   contact   a   central  hospital   laboratory   for   assistance   (peripheral   blood   films   and   where   possible   bone  marrow   films   can   be   sent   for   comment).  Avoid   blood   transfusion   before   establishing   the   cause   of   anaemia   or   at   least   taking  samples   for   performing   appropriate   investigations.   Further,   avoid   transfusions   in  cases   correctable   by   hematinics   or   other   therapy,   unless   the   patient   has   life  threatening   symptoms.   Avoid   polypharmacy   (giving   multiple   haematinics   without  establishing   the   cause   of   the   anaemia).  Iron   deficiency   anaemia  Note:   parenteral   iron,   which   is   neither   faster   acting   nor   more   effective   than   oral  iron,   is   rarely   indicated.   The   cause   of   Iron   deficiency   anaemia   must   be   elucidated  as   much   as   possible   and   correcting   the   primary   cause   should   be   the   most   single  important   objective.   The   typical   blood   picture   is   that   of   hypochromia   microcytosis  (MCV   <75fL).  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  ferrous   sulphate   po   C   E   200mg   [60   mg   iron]   3   times   a   day   review  Paed   =   12   mg   iron,   <1yr   =   6   mg   iron  Expected   response   rise   is   haemoglobin   1g/dl/week.   Continue   treatment   for   3  months   after   haemoglobin   has   normalised   to   replenish   body   iron   stores.  Megaloblastic   Anaemia  This   is   due   to   deficiency   of   vitamin   B 12   and/or   folic   acid.   It   is   important   to  establish   the   cause   of   the   deficiency   for   appropriate   treatment.   The   typical   blood  picture   is   that   of   macrocytosis   with   or   without   reduced   platelet   count   (MCV   >105  fL).   Until   or   unless   blood   levels   are   available,   it   is   mandatory   to   give   both   vitamin  B 12   (parenteral)   and   folic   acid   to   prevent   precipitation   of   neuropathy.  Avoid   blood   transfusion   if   possible

EDLIZ   2025  522  Macrocytosis   also   occurs   in   liver   disease,   alcohol   excess,   hypothyroidism,   some  haemolytic   anaemia   and   myelodysplastic   syndromes.   In   the   presence   of   these  conditions,   vitamin   B 12   /folate   deficiency   must   be   excluded.   Macrocytosis   is  physiological   in   the   neonate.  Vitamin   B 12   Deficiency  Lifelong   replacement   is   mandatory   in   pernicious   anaemia,   post-gastrectomy   or  ileal   resection.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  hydroxocobalami  n   (vitamin   B 12 )   im  B   V   1mg   daily   3   days  then   weekly   for   3   weeks,   and   monthly   for   3   months  and   then   every   3   months   for   a   year   and   then   twice   a  year.  Folic   Acid   Deficiency  Occurs   in   most   prolonged   haemolytic   anaemia ,   pregnancy,   and   seasonally   in   dry  areas   with   no   access   to   fresh   vegetables,   in   malabsorption   up   to   15mg   daily   may  be   required.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  folic   acid   po   C   E   5mg   once   a   day   3   months   and   review  S ICKLE   C ELL   A NAEMIA  Management :  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  folic   acid   po   C   E   5mg   once   a   day   for   life  penicillin   V   po   C   E   250mg   once   a   day   for   life  For   malaria   prophylaxis   in   endemic   areas:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  pyrimethamine/  dapsone   po  C   E   one   tablet  12.5mg/100  mg  once   a   week   continual  In   a   painful   crisis ,   intravenous   rehydration,   regular   and   adequate   analgesia,  oxygen   by   mask   where   available   and   possibly   in   some   cases   antibiotics   are  required.

EDLIZ   2025  523  Morphine   is   necessary   to   control   severe   pain .   Weaker   opiates   (codeine)   or   non-  steroidal   anti-inflammatory   medicines   (e.g.   aspirin)   may   be   used   for   less   severe  pain.   See   chapter   on   Pain.  Antibiotic   therapy :  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  amoxicillin   po   C   V   500mg   3   times   a   day   5   days  Other   antibiotics   may   be   required   according   to   site   of   infection/causative  organism.  Osteomyelitis:   see   chapter   on   Bone   and   Joint   Conditions.  Other   types   of   crises:  In   aplastic   and   haemolytic   crisis,   red   cell   transfusion   may   be   required   to   treat  anaemic   heart   failure.   Sequestration   or   splenic   pooling   requires   splenic   massage  and   less   often,   blood   transfusion.   Note   that   over   transfusion   worsens   the   sickling  crisis   and   may   cause   iron   overload.   Special   precautions   must   be   taken   during  anaesthetizing   sickle   cell   disease   patients.   Adequate   hydration   and   oxygen  exposure   are   essential   to   avoid   red   cell   sickling.  Avoid   debridement   of   leg   ulcers   as   these   are   due   to   poor   circulation   rather   than  mere   dead   tissue.  Treat   priapism   conservatively   with   hydration   and   analgesia   before   resorting   to  surgery.  Patients   with   frequent   crises   need   to   be   started   on   hydroxyurea   where   possible,  refer   to   provincial   or   central   hospitals .   The   objective   is   to   increase   the   HbF   to   at  least   20%,   levels   which   do   not   lead   to   haemolysis.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  hydroxyurea   po   B   V   500mg   once   a   day   indefinitely  Note:   Use   hydroxyurea   500   mgs   daily   indefinitely,   titrate   against   HbF.   Monitor  white   cell   and   platelet   counts.  Sickle   cell   trait   requires   no   treatment,   and   does   not   cause   anaemia .  G6PD   DEFICIENCY  Glucose-6-phosphate   dehydrogenase   (G6PD)   deficiency   is   common   in   Zimbabwe.

EDLIZ   2025  524  All   patients   should   have   a   "Medic-alert"   bracelet   or   necklace.  Severe   anaemia   occurs   with   intravascular   haemolysis   and   haemoglobinuria   on  exposure   to   oxidant   medicines   (e.g.   primaquine,   dapsone,   sulphonamides,  quinolones,   nitrofurantoin   and   in   some   cases   quinine   and   chloroquine)   and   be  worsened   by   acute   infections   e.g.   malaria.   Treat   these   episodes   with   intravenous  fluids,   oral   iron   and   folate   supplement;   treat   or   remove   the   underlying   cause.  Quinine   and   primaquine   (higher   doses)   have   been   associated   with   haemolysis   in  G6PD   deficiency.   Use   artesunate   injection   for   the   management   of   severe   malaria.  However,   the   risk   of   malaria   outweighs   the   risk   of   haemolysis,   so   where  artesunate   is   not   available   quinine   can   be   used   if   indicated   for   malaria   treatment  unless   the   specific   patient   is   known   to   be   sensitive   to   the   specified   anti-malaria   in  question.  Avoid   blood   transfusion   unless   clinically   indicated.  O THER   A NAEMIAS  HIV   anaemia   is   a   common   finding   with   HIV   and   AIDS   patients.   Transfusion   is  only   indicated   in   treating   severe   anaemia   and   cardiac   failure.   The   causes   of   the  anaemia   include   medicines   such   as   zidovudine,   and   infections.  Other   cytopenias :   refer   to   the   next   section   on   Blood   Products.  Aplastic   anaemia   presents   as   pancytopenia .   Diagnosis   needs   confirmation   by  bone   marrow   examination.   Refer   to   central   hospitals   for   specialist   care   after  confirmation.  Myelodysplastic   syndromes:   refer   to   Central   Hospital   for   specialist   management.  Sideroblastic   anaemia   may   occur   in   alcoholism,   malignancy,   hypothyroidism   and  particularly   during   TB   treatment.   Some   respond   to   vitamin   B 6   but   refer   to   central  hospital   for   specialist   care.  Leukaemias :   refer   to   the   central   level.  HEREDITARY   BLEEDING   DISORDERS  Never   use   intramuscular   injections   and   aspirin   (paracetamol   is   safe).   All   patients  should   have   a   " Medic-alert "   bracelet   or   necklace.

EDLIZ   2025  525  Refer   early   for   specialist   management.  All   haemophilia   patients   should   be   registered   with   the   Haemophilia  Association/Centre,   Department   of   Haematology,   P.O.   Box   A   178,   Avondale,  Harare.   Clinics   are   held   at   Parirenyatwa   Hospital   every   month.  For   hemarthrosis   -   do   not   aspirate   joints .   Treat   by   replacement   of   specific   factor,  joint   support   and   analgesia   e.g.   paracetamol,   codeine,   morphine.   Rest   the   joint  during   the   acute   period,   give   appropriate   replacement   factor   and   start  physiotherapy   as   soon   as   the   bleed   resolves.   Some   Haemophilia   A   and   B   patients  are   on   a   home   therapy   programme.   They   have   written   instructions   on  recommended   dosage   but   may   require   assistance   from   health   personnel.  Haemophilia   A   (factor   VIII   deficiency)  The   amount   of   factor   VIII   given   depends   on   assessment   of   severity   of   bleed.   Use  the   table   below   to   determine   dosage,   for   both   children   and   adults   according   to  body   weight.  Ice   compression   should   be   applied   as   soon   as   possible,   as   this   reduces   the   bleed.  Dosage   of   Factor   VIII   –   Adults   –   per   dose  Severity   of   bleed   Required  FVIII   level  FVIII  Concentrate  [500   IU/bottle]  Cryoprecipitate  [80   IU/bag]   (=20mls)  1.   Mild   bleed   (nose,   gums  etc.)  14   IU/kg   1-2   bottles   6   bags  2.   Moderate   bleed   joint,  muscle,   GIT,   minor  surgery  20   IU/kg   2-4   bottles   12   bags  3.   Major   bleed   (e..g..  cerebral)  40   IU/kg   4-6   bottles   12   bags  4.   Prophylaxis   for   major  surgery  60   IU/kg   6-10   bottles   18   bags  Note:   For   1   and   2   above,   repeat   the   dose   12   hourly   if   bleeding   persists   or   swelling  is   increasing.   With   more   severe   bleeds   it   is   usually   necessary   to   continue  treatment   with   half   of   total   daily   dose   12   hourly   for   2   -3   days,   occasionally   longer.  Note:   For   3   and   4   above,   treatment   and   surgery   should   be   done   with   specialist  supervision   only .   Measure   levels,   (if   possible),   otherwise   give   immediately   before  surgery.   Continue   12   hourly   therapy   for   48   hours   postoperatively   and   if   no  bleeding   occurs,   scale   down   gradually   over   next   3   -5   days. EDLIZ   2025  529     Caution:   medicine   interactions   are   common   and   can   be   dangerous.   Below   are   a  few   examples:  Warfarin   Overdose     If   INR   4.5-7   without   haemorrhage   -   withhold   warfarin   for   1-2   days   then   review.     If   INR   >7   without   haemorrhage   -   withhold   warfarin   and   check   INR   daily. Unfractionated   heparin   is   given   subcutaneously   or   intravenously.   The   dose   is  dependent   on   the   activated   partial   thromboplastin   time   ratio   (aPTTR).   It   has   a  short   half-life   and   needs   laboratory   facilities.  Low   molecular   weight   heparin   is   given   subcutaneously   with   a   fixed   dose.   No  laboratory   monitoring   facilities   are   required. EDLIZ   2025  530  Prophylaxis   for   Deep   Vein   Thrombosis   (DVT)  This   is   indicated   for   all   patients   who   have   high   risk   factors   for   thrombosis   before  and   after   surgery.   These   conditions   include:     Obesity     Prolonged   immobility     Hereditary   thrombophilia   states   (antithrombin   III,   factor   V   Leiden   deficiency,  protein   C   &   S   deficiency   etc.)     Paroxysmal   nocturnal   haemoglobinuria     Previous   history   of   DVT     Various   malignancies     Other   pro-thrombotic   states   such   as   artificial   cardiac   valves   and   atrial   fibrillation  need   lifelong   anticoagulation.  Methods   of   prophylaxis   available  Physical   methods   include   stockings.   Early   mobility   must   be   encouraged   in   all  surgical   patients.  Medicine   management   (targeting   an   INR   of   2   to   2.5):  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  warfarin   po   B   V   10mg   once   a   day   2   days  then   review   based   on   INR   level  or   heparin   sc  (unfractionated)  B   V   5000   units   8   hourly   review  Treatment   of   DVT  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  heparin   sc  (unfractionated)  B   V   17500   units   twice   a   day   see   below*  or   low   molecular  heparin  B   V   40mg   once   daily  *Duration   is   4-6   months   except   in   pregnancy,   or   if   there   is   another   reason   for  prolonged   treatment.     Pulmonary   embolism:   4-6   months.     Atrial   fibrillation:   lifelong   treatment.     Heart   valve   prostheses:   lifelong   treatment.  Continue   heparin   until   warfarin   effective   -   usually   3-5   days.

EDLIZ   2025  531  Deep   Vein   Thrombosis   in   pregnancy  Continue   throughout   pregnancy,   aiming   for   APTT   2-3   times   normal:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  low   molecular  heparin  B   V   40mg   once   a   day   1   month   postpartum  or   warfarin   po  after   12   weeks   up   to  37   weeks  B   V   keep   INR   in  range   2-3  to   37   weeks,   then  change   to   heparin  CAUTION:   Warfarin   may   harm   the   foetus   and   should   not   be   used   under   12  weeks.   Monitor   closely   whichever   method   is   used.   Specialist   supervision   is  recommended.  Heparin   may   cause   thrombocytopenia ,   and   with   prolonged   use   osteoporosis.  Life   Threatening   Pulmonary   Embolism/   Arterial   Embolism  See   also   the   section   in   Cardiovascular   conditions.  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  hydrocortisone   iv  [for   allergic  reactions]  C   V   100mg   once   only   -  and   streptokinase   iv   A   N   loading   dose   of   250   000   units   over   30   minutes,  then  100   000   u   every   hour   24   –   72   hrs  Haematological   malignancy :   Refer   all   patients   to   a   Central  Hospital.

EDLIZ   2025  532  U SE   OF   B LOOD   AND   B LOOD   P RODUCTS  General   Principles  Efforts   should   be   made   to   avoid   transfusions   wherever   possible   because   of:     The   need   to   conserve   scarce   and   expensive   blood   products.     The   risk   of   Transfusion   Transmitted   Infections   e.g.   HIV   and   Hepatitis   C  (window   period)   and   other   transmissible   agents.     The   risk   of   transfusion   reactions.  There   are   limited   and   specific   indications   for   transfusion.   Use   the   appropriate  blood   fraction   to   treat   specific   defects.   Transfuse   patients   during   normal   working  hours:   avoid   night-time   transfusion   whenever   possible.  Note:   Anaemia   -   The   correct   management   of   a   patient   with   anaemia   is   to   identify  and   treat   the   cause.   Blood   transfusion   is   required   only   when   the   anaemia   is   life-  threatening   e.g.   cardiac   failure   or   when   it   prohibits   other   necessary   treatment   e.g.  chemotherapy   or   surgery.   A   slow   rise   of   haemoglobin   in   response   to   haematinics  is   not   an   indication   for   transfusion.  Note:   The   routine   use   of   furosemide   is   not   necessary.   When   needed,   e.g.   in   cardiac  failure,   give   small   doses   (e.g.   20   mg ).   Intravenous   furosemide   is   rarely   required.  Blood   must   be   kept   at   an   appropriate   temperature   as   much   as   possible.  S PECIFIC   I NDICATIONS   FOR   U SE   OF   B LOOD   AND   B LOOD   P RODUCTS  Red   Cell   Concentrate   (packed   cells)  Medicine  Give   packed   cells   in   the   following   situations:     Acute   major   haemorrhage.     Chronic   anaemia-when   patient   has   symptoms   of   cardiac   failure   due   to   low  haemoglobin   (<5g/dl);     Anaemic   patient   (<5g/dl)   due   to   have   haemodialysis;   (Elderly   patients   may  require   Hb   levels   higher   than   8g/dL).   Prior   to,   and   following   aggressive  cytotoxic   programmes,   maintain   haemoglobin   at/or   above   8g/dl;     Low   haemoglobin   (<8g/dl)   in   presence   of   severe   and   persistent   infections   and  septicaemia;     Acute   haemolysis   where   a   patient   has   symptoms   of   cardiac   failure.

EDLIZ   2025  533  Paediatrics  Small   packs   (100mls)   are   available.   Indications   are   as   for   adults   (see   above   list).  Where   transfusion   is   given   on   appropriate   indication   to   children   with   protein-  energy   malnutrition,   they   should   be   transfused   slowly   (not   more   than   2.5   ml/kg  body   weight/hour).  The   presence   of   anaemia   and   safety   of   procedures   must   be   evaluated   on   a   patient  to   patient   basis.   There   is   no   single   safe   haemoglobin   level,   patient   haemodynamics  must   be   evaluated   against   the   procedure   to   be   performed.  Patients   are   generally   hemodynamically   stable   at   haemoglobin   levels   above   8  gm/dL   for   several   procedures,   but   anticipation   of   blood   loss   must   be   assessed  before   each   procedure.  P LATELET   C ONCENTRATES  Note:   this   product   is   often   used   inappropriately  This   section   applies   to   all   disciplines.  Indications   for   Use   of   Platelet   Concentrates     Acute   bleeding   in   a   patient   with   a   low   platelet   count   less   than   30   x   10 9 /L     Disseminated   intravascular   coagulation   (DIC)   with   active   generalised   bleeding  and   platelets   <20   x   10 9 /L     Operations   with   platelets<30   x   10 9 /L     Cranial   operations   and   eye   operations   need   platelets   above   100   x   10 9 /L).  No   justification   for   use   of   platelets   in:     Low   platelet   count   in   patients   with   no   evidence   of   bleeding,   [most   transfused  platelets   will   be   eliminated   within   24   hrs.]     Autoimmune   thrombocytopenia.     HIV   thrombocytopenia   without   bleeding.     Aplastic   anaemia   without   bleeding.

EDLIZ   2025  534  Guidelines   for   platelet   transfusion   in   surgical   patients.  >100x10 9 /  L  All   surgical   procedures   including   eye  and   brain  No   platelet   transfusion  required.  >50x10 9 /L   All   surgical   procedures   with   the  exception   of   eye   and   brain.  No   platelet   transfusion  required.  >30x10 9 /L   Safe   for   minor   procedures   such   as   non-  traumatic   lumbar   puncture,   bone  marrow   aspirates,   GIT   scopes   etc.  No   platelet   transfusion  required.  *   Not   to   be   used   in   children   under   the   age   of   2   yrs.  F RESH   F ROZEN   P LASMA  Note:   Currently   this   is   the   most   inappropriately   used   product   in   Zimbabwe.  This   section   applies   to   all   disciplines.   The   risks   of   transmission   of   infection   are   no  less   than   with   packed   red   cells   or   whole   blood.  Indications   for   Use   of   FFP     Severe   acute   disseminated   intravascular   coagulation   (DIC)   with   active  generalised   bleeding     In   presence   of   bleeding   and   disturbed   coagulation   in   patients   with   liver   disease,  or   following   massive   transfusion     In   presence   of   disturbed   coagulation   in   patients   requiring   liver/renal   biopsy,   or  surgery     If   immediate   reversal   of   warfarin   effect   essential   (combined   with   Vitamin   K  administration)     Replacement   of   single   coagulation   factor   deficiencies   where   specific   factor  concentrate   not   available   e.g.   haemophilia   B     Thrombotic   thrombocytopenic   purpura  No   justification   for   use   of   FFP   in:     Hypovolaemia     Acute   haemorrhage   with   minimal   disturbance   of   coagulation     As   nutritional   support     Obstructive   jaundice   with   disturbed   coagulation   (vitamin   K   should   be   given).  W HOLE   B LOOD  To   be   used   in   cases   where   fractionated   blood   is   not   available.   The   indication   is   as  for   red   cell   concentrate,   and   never   as   a   source   of   plasma.  Exchange   transfusion   in   Neonates   STRICTLY   USE   WHOLE   BLOOD .

EDLIZ   2025  535  BLOOD   MUST   BE   LESS   THAN   5   DAYS   OLD.  Indications:     Definite   clinical   jaundice   on   Day   1   of   life     Clinical   signs   of   kernicterus     Total   Serum   Bilirubin   levels   as   shown   in   the   table   below   [26.3],   depending   on  gestational   and   postnatal   age   of   the   baby. EDLIZ   2025  536  ANTI-D  To   be   given   post-delivery   to   Rhesus   D   negative   patients   to   prevent   immunization.  INTRAVENOUS   IMMUNOGLOBULIN.  Can   be   used   in   several   autoimmune   diseases   to   include   ITP,   Guillain   Barre   and   in  selected   infections.  S ALT -P OOR   A LBUMIN  May   be   indicated   for   correction   of   chronic   hypoalbuminemia ;   in   special  circumstances   of   organ   failure   and   fluid   overload   e.g.   liver   disease   and   resistant  ascites.  F ACTOR   VIII   P REPARATIONS  Products   available:   (1)   freeze   dried   anti-haemophilic   factor   (AHF),   (2)  cryoprecipitate.   To   be   used   in   patients   with   haemophilia   A   with   mild,   moderate   or  severe   bleeds.   See   notes   above.  F ACTOR   IX   C ONCENTRATE  For   patients   with   haemophilia   B   who   are   bleeding.   See   notes   above.  C RYOPRECIPITATE  Indications   include   DIC,   von   Willebrand   Disease,   haemophilia,   and   bleeding  associated   with   renal   failure.

EDLIZ   2025  537  31.   I NTRAVENOUS   F LUID   R EPLACEMENT  GENERAL   NOTES   53 8  NORMAL   DAILY   REQUIREMENTS   53 8  MAINTENANCE   53 9  REPLACEMENT   OF   ABNORMAL   LOSSES   53 9  AVAILABLE   INTRAVENOUS   SOLUTIONS   54 1  CRYSTALLOIDS   54 1  COLLOIDS   5 4 3

EDLIZ   2025  538  G ENERAL   N OTES  NB:   Repeated   clinical   evaluation   of   patients   receiving   IV   fluid   therapy   is  necessary   to   avoid   the   dangers   of   over-transfusion   or   inadequate   rehydration.  Formulae   and   biochemical   estimations   are   no   substitute   for   clinical   evaluation.  Special   Notes     Only   give   intravenous   fluids   when   they   are   strictly   necessary.   It   is   wasteful   and  dangerous   to   give   iv   fluids   to   a   patient   who   can   drink   oral   fluids.     For   fluid   replacement   in   burns   see   relevant   chapter.     For   use   of   blood   and   blood   products   see   relevant   chapter.  Electrolyte   content   of   various   body   fluids  Electrolyte   content   of   various   body   fluids   should   be   taken   into   account.   For  practical   purposes   replacement   is   with   Normal   Saline   or   Ringer   Lactate   with  added   potassium,   except   for   diarrhoea,   particularly   in   children,   where   the   sodium  content   is   proportionately   lower   and   the   potassium   higher. A VAILABLE   I NTRAVENOUS   S OLUTIONS  These   can   be   divided   into   two   groups:   CRYSTALLOIDS   and   COLLOIDS.  C RYSTALLOIDS  The   composition   of   the   crystalloid   solutions   is   shown   in   Table   31.2.  Sodium   chloride   0.9%   (normal   saline)  Suitable   for   fluid   replacement   in   the   initial   therapy   of   haemorrhagic   shock   and  severe   dehydration.   The   sodium   content   sustains   the   circulating   blood   volume,   and  the   absence   of   potassium   allows   rapid   infusion.   Contains   no   calories.   May   be  given   as   part   of   maintenance   regimen.  If   normal   saline   is   being   given   as   part   of   maintenance   requirement   care   must   be  taken   not   to   overload   the   patient   particularly   in   the   postoperative   period   when  sodium   and   water   requirements   are   decreased   -   as   little   as   10mmol   of   sodium   may  be   required   in   24   hours   after   major   surgery   /trauma.

EDLIZ   2025  542  Ringer   lactate   solution  Suitable   for   the   same   purposes   as   Sodium   Chloride   0.9%.   In   addition   to   sodium,   it  contains   potassium   and   calcium   in   physiological   amounts   and   provides  bicarbonate.   Use   with   caution   in   diabetes   mellitus   and   renal   failure   and   severe  diarrhoea   with   alkalosis.  Maintelyte  Suitable   for   maintenance   but   MUST   NOT   BE   USED   FOR   RESUSCITATION   as  the   sodium   content   is   too   low   to   sustain   blood   volume   and   the   potassium   content   is  too   high   for   safe   rapid   infusion.   Avoid   in   renal   failure.   Since   this   solution   is   very  hypertonic   it   may   damage   vascular   endothelium.   It   should   be   avoided   in  hyperosmolar   states.   It   is   currently   suggested   that   hyperglycaemia   is   detrimental   to  patients   at   risk   of   cerebral   ischaemia   (owing   to   anaerobic   production   of   lactic  acidosis).   If   maintelyte   is   used,   monitor   blood   glucose   levels   regularly.   It   cannot  be   used   for   replacing   potassium   deficits   unless   more   potassium   is   added   as  maintelyte   contains   basic   requirements   of   potassium   only.  Half   strength   Darrow ’ s   solution   with   dextrose   2.5%  An   all-purpose   solution   with   an   electrolyte   content   intermediate   between   the  replacement   and   maintenance   solutions;   the   recommended   solution   for   both   initial  (replacement)   therapy   and   subsequent   (maintenance)   therapy   of   dehydrated   infants.  Use   with   caution   in   renal   failure.   For   classes   I   and   II   (mild-moderate)   blood   loss  use   normal   saline   as   crystalloid   replacement   fluid   of   choice.   Darrow ’ s   contains  too   little   dextrose   to   maintain   the   blood   sugar   level   in   neonates.   It   contains   too  little   sodium   to   be   used   in   the   postoperative   period   or   replacement   of   upper   gastro-  intestinal   and   small   bowel   losses.   Its   use   is   mainly   confined   to   rehydrating  children   with   diarrhoea   and   vomiting.  Dextrose   5%   in   water  Contains   no   electrolytes;   it   may   be   used:     as   part   of   maintenance   regimen;     as   a   replacement   fluid   where   pure   water   loss   predominates   (as   in   febrile   illness,  pneumonia   and   asthma);     as   full   maintenance   in   acute   renal   failure,   where   no   electrolytes   are   being   lost   in  urine;     as   a   vehicle   for   administration   of   some   medicines.  It   should   not   be   used   in   patients   with   head   injuries   (cerebral   oedema   may   result).

EDLIZ   2025  543  Dextrose   10%   in   water  Used   for   peri-operative   management   of   diabetic   patients   (undergoing   surgery)   and  for   patients   with   hepatitis,   hypo-glycaemia.  Dextrose   2.5%   and   sodium   chloride   0.45%  Used   as   a   maintenance   solution   and   as   a   vehicle   for   administration   of   some  medicines.  Dextrose   5%   and   sodium   chloride   0.9%  A   special   purpose   solution   useful   for   certain   surgical   patients   with   hyponatraemia  and   impaired   renal   function.  Maintenance   solution   neonatal   multi-electrolyte   ‘ neonatalyte ’  Used   as   a   maintenance   solution   for   neonates.   It   contains   phosphate   3.75   mmol/L  (as   HPO 4 ).   Use   with   caution   in   renal   failure.  Sodium   chloride   0.45%   (half   normal   saline)  Used   in   cases   of   sodium   overload   and   in   patients   with   hyperosmolar,   non-ketotic  diabetic   coma/precoma.  C OLLOIDS  Indication   for   colloids   includes   resuscitation   in   severe   hypovolaemia,   treatment   of  circulatory   collapse,   and   emergency   treatment   of   shock   due   to   fluid   loss   or   blood  loss   as   a   plasma   substitute.   Colloids   should   be   used   when   crystalloids   do   not  improve   volume   or   when   crystalloids   are   not   expected   to   improve   volume.  Examples   of   colloids   include   albumin,   plasma   protein   fraction,   dextran,   gelatin  and   etherifiedetherified   starches   (hetastarch,   pentastarch   and   tetrastarch).  Fresh   frozen   plasma   (FFP)   should   not   be   used   as   a   general   colloid,   but   only   when  specifically   indicated.   See   section   on   Blood   and   Blood   related   Products.  Dextran   70  Used   to   reduce   viscosity   and   prevent   venous   thrombosis.  Modified   gelatin  Used   to   expand   and   maintain   blood   volume   in   hypovolemic   shock.

EDLIZ   2025  545  32.   A NAPHYLAXIS  G ENERAL   N OTES   54 6  T REATMENT   54 7

EDLIZ   2025  546  G ENERAL   N OTES  Severe   anaphylaxis   is   a   life-threatening   immunological   response   to   a   substance   to  which   an   individual   is   sensitised.   It   is   a   medical   emergency   (life   and   death  situation)   in   which   seconds   count.   Prompt   treatment   is   required   for   acute   airway  obstruction,   bronchospasm   and   hypotension.  Triggers  Common   triggers   of   anaphylaxis   are   medicines,   (notably:   antibiotics,   non-steroidal  anti-inflammatory   medicines,   antiarrhythmics,   heparin,   parenteral   iron,  desensitising   preparations   and   vaccines),   blood   transfusions,   bee   and   other   insect  stings,   anaesthetic   medicines   and   certain   foods.   Latex   allergy   may   be   delayed   in  onset,   taking   up   to   60   minutes   to   manifest.   Some   anaesthetic   medicines   are   also  associated   with   anaphylactoid   reactions   (urticaria,   flushing   and   mild   hypotension).  Food   allergen   triggers   may   have   a   delayed   onset.   For   example,   nuts   may   have   a  delayed   onset   and   are   commonly   associated   with   urticaria.  Clinical   Presentation   of   Anaphylaxis  Anaphylactoid   reactions   range   from   minor   to   life-threatening.   The   major  presenting   features   are   commonly   cardiovascular.   It   is   important   to   recognise   and  address   the   following:  Cardiovascular   (hypotension,   tachycardia,   arrhythmias,   ECG   may   show   ischaemic  changes   even   cardiac   arrest)  Respiratory   system   (oedema   of   the   glottis,   tongue   and   airways,   stridor   and   airway  obstruction   and   bronchospasm)  Gastrointenstinal   (abdominal   pain,   diarrhoea   or   vomiting)  Cutaneous   (flushing,   erythema,   urticaria)  Note:   It   is   imperative   to   establish   a   causative   allergen   source   and   it   is   essentially  that   the   patient   is   advised   in   writing   of   the   allergy   and   advised   to   wear   a   medic-  alert   bracelet   indicating   the   sensitivity:   repeat   exposure   may   be   fatal.

EDLIZ   2025  547  T REATMENT  Discontinue   administration   of   any   suspect   agent   (for   example   medicine,   blood,  diagnostic   agent)  Lay   the   patient   flat   and   elevate   the   legs.  Follow   the   ABC   of   resuscitation  A-   Airway     Give   Adrenaline   IM   0.5-   1mg   (0.5   –   1ml   of   1:1000   solution)   in   adult,   child  above   50kg   repeated   each   ten   minutes   as   required.   See   dosing   on   table   below   for  other   weight   categories.     Note:   SC   route   is   not   recommended   because   absorption   is   erratic.     Ensure   a   clear   airway;   give   100%   oxygen,   6L/minute   via   face   mask.  B-   Breathing     Ensure   adequate   breathing   (intubate   and   ventilate   as   required)     Nebulised   bronchodilators   (for   example,   5mg   salbutamol)   or   iv   aminophylline  may   be   required   if   bronchospasm   is   refractory   (loading   dose   of   5mg/kg   followed  by   0.5mg/kg/hr).  C-   Circulation     Monitor   pulse,   blood   pressure,   bronchospasm   and   general   response/condition  every   3-5   minutes.     Start   CPR   if   cardiac   arrest   has   occurred  Give:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  adrenaline   1   in   1000  im  C   V   0.5   –   1ml  [=   10mg/kg]  Repeat   as   necessary   every  10mins   until   improvement  occurs. 40   kgs  30   kgs  20   kgs  15   kgs  0.4ml  0.3ml  0.2ml  0.15ml  10kgs   0.1ml  In   severe   allergic   reaction/   hypotensive   give:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  adrenaline   1   in   10   000   iv  [Add   9ml   normal   saline   0.9%  to   1ml   of   1   in   1   000  adrenaline   to   make   1:   10   000]  C   V   1ml   slow  [1-5yr  =0.1ml/kg]  repeat  every  minute  until   satisfactory  clinical   response

EDLIZ   2025  548  Start   IV   volume   expansion   with   normal   saline   (or   Ringer   lactate)   adjusting   rate  according   to   blood   pressure:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  normal   saline   iv   C   V   first   litre   run   in   over   15-20mins,   then   review.     Add:  Medicine   Codes   Adult   dose   Frequency   Duration  promethazine   slow  iv  B   V   25-50mg   8hourly   up   to   48hrs  Paed:   1-5yr   =   5mg  6-12yr   =12.5mg  or   chlorpheniramine  iv   (slowly)  B   V   10mg   6hourly   up   to   24hrs  and   hydrocortisone   iv   C   V   200mg   6hourly   as   required  Paed:   <   1yr   =   25mg  1-5yrs   =   50mg  6-12yrs   =   100mg  Monitor   pulse,   blood   pressure,   bronchospasm   and   general   response/condition  every   two   minutes.
